topics.title: Determination of Hardness of the Given Specimen by Brinell's Hardness Test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGL308 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Hardness of the Given Specimen by Brinell's Hardness Test

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Brinell Hardness Number (BHN)** is defined as the **ratio of the applied load (P)** to the **surface area of the spherical indentation (A)** produced on the test specimen by a hardened steel or tungsten carbide ball indenter under a known static load.

The Brinell Hardness Test is a **quasi-static indentation hardness test** standardized under **IS 1500:2005** (equivalent to **ASTM E10** and **ISO 6506-1:2014**). It is the oldest and most widely used indentation hardness test for materials possessing a coarse or heterogeneous grain structure, such as **cast iron, forged steel, and non-ferrous alloys**.

The hardness number is mathematically expressed as:

$$\text{BHN} = \frac{\text{Applied Load (P)}}{\text{Curved Surface Area of Indentation (A)}}$$

The unit is **kgf/mm²** (kilogram-force per square millimetre), although modern SI practice reports it as a **dimensionless number** (ratio of two forces of the same dimension).

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Watermelon Test"**
> Imagine pressing your thumb firmly into a fully ripe watermelon versus a raw, unripe one. The thumb sinks deeper into the ripe (softer) watermelon and barely leaves a mark on the unripe (harder) one. The Brinell test does precisely this — but instead of a thumb, it uses a **hardened steel ball**; instead of softness, it quantifies **resistance to permanent plastic deformation**.

Another everyday analogy: think of a carpenter pressing a marble into a slab of butter versus a slab of wood. The depth and diameter of the circular impression tell you how "soft" or "hard" the material is.

### 1.3 Standard Reference Materials & Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Indenter Standard** — A **hardened steel ball of 10 mm diameter** (or tungsten carbide ball for very hard materials) is the standard indenter per **IS 1500 / ASTM E10**.

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Ball Diameter (D) | **10 mm** (also 5 mm, 2.5 mm, 1 mm) |
| Load (P) | **3000 kgf** for steel & iron |
| Dwell Time | **10 to 15 seconds** |
| Standard P/D² Ratio | **30** for steel, **10** for copper alloys, **5** for aluminium |
| Indenter Material | Hardened steel (≥ 850 HV) or Tungsten Carbide |
| Typical Indentation Diameter (d) | **0.25 D ≤ d ≤ 0.5 D** (validity range) |

### 1.4 Why this Experiment Exists in the KTU Curriculum

Brinell Hardness is the **gateway experiment** in any Strength of Materials laboratory because it bridges:
- **Tensile strength estimation** (BHN ≈ 0.35 × UTS for steels — an empirical correlation),
- **Quality control** of heat-treated components,
- **Material selection** for gears, shafts, and structural members.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle (Step-by-Step Logic)

The Brinell test operates on the principle that a hard indenter of known geometry, when pressed into a specimen under a known load, produces an indentation whose **projected surface area** is inversely proportional to the material's hardness.

> [!NOTE]
> **Operational Logic Flow:**
> 1. A **hardened steel ball (D = 10 mm)** is pressed onto the prepared specimen surface.
> 2. A **static, gradually applied load (P)** is maintained for a fixed dwell time (≈ 10 s).
> 3. Upon load removal, a **circular, bowl-shaped indentation** of diameter **d** remains on the surface.
> 4. The diameter **d** is measured using a **low-power measuring microscope** along **two perpendicular directions** (to eliminate elliptical errors).
> 5. The mean diameter is substituted into the **BHN formula**.

### 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Step

- **Why a spherical indenter?** → It produces a large indentation, averaging out microstructural inhomogeneities (great for cast irons).
- **Why a defined P/D² ratio?** → To ensure the indentation diameter **d** always falls within the geometric validity range (0.25 D to 0.5 D), making test results comparable across materials.
- **Why measure twice (perpendicular)?** → Surface irregularities can cause the indentation to appear slightly elliptical; averaging eliminates this systematic error.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all examinable formulas** for the Brinell Hardness Test under KTU 2024 Scheme.

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Standard / Validity |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{BHN} = \dfrac{2P}{\pi D \left( D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2} \right)}$ | Master Brinell formula (kgf/mm²) | ASTM E10 / IS 1500 |
| 2 | $A = \dfrac{\pi D}{2} \left( D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2} \right)$ | Curved (lateral) surface area of spherical cap | Geometry of sphere |
| 3 | $h = \dfrac{1}{2}\left( D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2} \right)$ | Depth of indentation | Derived from Pythagoras |
| 4 | $\dfrac{P}{D^2} = K$ | Load index — constant for given material class | Empirical (Böhler) |
| 5 | $\sigma_u \approx 0.35 \times \text{BHN}$ (kgf/mm²) | Ultimate Tensile Strength correlation (steels only) | Empirical |
| 6 | Designation: **HBW 10/3000/15** | **HBW** = Tungsten Carbide ball, **10** = ball diameter (mm), **3000** = load (kgf), **15** = dwell time (s) | Modern ASTM E10 notation |
| 7 | Designation: **HBS 10/3000/10** | **HBS** = Steel ball; rest as above | Legacy notation |

**Standard P/D² Ratios (Critical for Exam):**

| Material Class | P/D² (K factor) | Typical Load (D = 10 mm) |
|---|---|---|
| Steel, Cast Iron, Nickel, Titanium alloys | **30** | 3000 kgf |
| Copper, Brass, Bronze (medium) | **10** | 1000 kgf |
| Aluminium, Magnesium alloys | **5** | 500 kgf |
| Lead, Tin, Bearing metals | **1** | 100 kgf |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** BHN is the most direct quality-control check for **heat-treated gears, crankshafts, and rolled plates**. In a foundry, a single Brinell reading can predict whether a casting will survive a year's service life or fail prematurely due to insufficient surface hardness.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Geometric Derivation of the BHN Formula

We derive the formula from first principles using the geometry of a spherical cap.

> [!NOTE]
> **Setup:** A spherical ball of diameter **D** indents the specimen, producing a circular impression of diameter **d** and depth **h**. We assume the indentation is a perfect spherical cap of the same radius **R = D/2** as the indenter.

**Step 1 — Geometry of the spherical cap:**

The depth of indentation is found by applying the Pythagoras theorem to the half-section of the cap. The radius of the ball (R = D/2) acts as the hypotenuse, half the indentation diameter (d/2) as one leg, and (R − h) as the other leg.

$$
\left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^2 + (R - h)^2
$$

$$
\frac{D^2}{4} = \frac{d^2}{4} + \left(\frac{D}{2} - h\right)^2
$$

**Step 2 — Expanding the squared term:**

$$
\frac{D^2}{4} = \frac{d^2}{4} + \frac{D^2}{4} - D h + h^2
$$

**Step 3 — Cancelling common terms (D²/4 on both sides):**

$$
0 = \frac{d^2}{4} - D h + h^2
$$

**Step 4 — Solving for h:**

$$
D h = h^2 + \frac{d^2}{4}
$$

$$
h = \frac{d^2}{4(D - h)}
$$

Since **h ≪ D**, we apply the approximation **(D − h) ≈ D** for a first-order simplification, yielding:

$$
h \approx \frac{d^2}{4D}
$$

However, the **exact** (non-approximated) depth, which is the formula used in KTU examinations, is obtained by solving the quadratic directly:

$$
h^2 - D h + \frac{d^2}{4} = 0
$$

$$
h = \frac{D \pm \sqrt{D^2 - d^2}}{2}
$$

The physically meaningful root (taking the minus sign, since h < D) is:

$$
h = \frac{1}{2}\left( D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2} \right)
$$

**Step 5 — Lateral Surface Area of the Spherical Cap:**

The curved surface area of a spherical cap of base radius **(d/2)** and sphere radius **(D/2)** is:

$$
A = 2 \pi R h = 2 \pi \left(\frac{D}{2}\right) h = \pi D h
$$

Substituting the exact value of h:

$$
A = \pi D \cdot \frac{1}{2}\left( D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2} \right) = \frac{\pi D}{2}\left( D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2} \right)
$$

**Step 6 — Final BHN Formula:**

By definition, BHN = Load ÷ Surface Area:

$$
\text{BHN} = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{P}{\frac{\pi D}{2}\left( D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2} \right)}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{BHN} = \frac{2P}{\pi D \left( D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2} \right)}}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A Brinell hardness test is conducted on a mild steel specimen using a ball of **10 mm** diameter and a load of **3000 kgf**. The mean indentation diameter measured is **4.6 mm**. Calculate the BHN.

**Given:** P = 3000 kgf, D = 10 mm, d = 4.6 mm

**Step 1 — Compute D² and d²:**

$$
D^2 = (10)^2 = 100 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$$
d^2 = (4.6)^2 = 21.16 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the inner radical:**

$$
\sqrt{D^2 - d^2} = \sqrt{100 - 21.16} = \sqrt{78.84} \approx 8.879 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute (D − √(D² − d²)):**

$$
D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2} = 10 - 8.879 = 1.121 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the master formula:**

$$
\text{BHN} = \frac{2 \times 3000}{\pi \times 10 \times 1.121}
$$

$$
\text{BHN} = \frac{6000}{35.213} \approx 170.4
$$

**Step 5 — Report with designation:**

$$
\boxed{\text{BHN} \approx 170 \text{ kgf/mm}^2 \quad \text{(reported as HBS 10/3000/10)}}
$$

### 3.3 Validity Check (Mandatory in KTU Answers)

A Brinell test result is considered **valid** only when:

$$
0.25 \, D \leq d \leq 0.5 \, D
$$

For D = 10 mm: d must lie between **2.5 mm and 5.0 mm**.
Our reading d = 4.6 mm lies in this range ✅ (valid test).

### 3.4 Experimental Procedure Matrix

| Stage | Action | Instrument / Specification |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Specimen preparation (flat, smooth, parallel faces) | Surface grinder, emery paper (180–600 grit) |
| 2 | Select ball diameter | **10 mm** (standard) |
| 3 | Select load using P/D² chart | K = 30 → **3000 kgf** for steel |
| 4 | Mount specimen on anvil | Ensure perpendicular contact |
| 5 | Apply load smoothly (no shock) | Hydraulic / lever loading system |
| 6 | Hold dwell time | **15 seconds** (steel); 30 s for soft metals |
| 7 | Release load and remove specimen | Indentation should be clearly visible |
| 8 | Measure indentation diameter | Travelling microscope, two perpendicular readings |
| 9 | Compute BHN | Substitute into master formula |
| 10 | Repeat 2–3 times | Report **mean BHN** for accuracy |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Apparatus Block Architecture (Mermaid Flow Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Specimen Mounted on Anvil"] --> nodeB["10 mm Hardened Steel Ball Indenter"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Hydraulic Load Application Lever"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Static Load 3000 kgf Applied"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Dwell Timer 10 to 15 seconds"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Load Released Indentation Formed"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Travelling Microscope Measurement"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Two Perpendicular Diameter Readings d1 d2"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["Mean Diameter d and BHN Calculation"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["Report HBS 10 by 3000 by 15"]
```

### 4.2 Indenter Geometry & Test Configuration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeK["Hardened Steel Ball Diameter D"] --> nodeL["Specimen Surface"]
    nodeL --> nodeM["Circular Indentation Diameter d"]
    nodeM --> nodeN["Depth of Indentation h"]
    nodeN --> nodeO["Spherical Cap Geometry"]
    nodeO --> nodeP["Surface Area A pi D h"]
    nodeP --> nodeQ["BHN equals P divided by A"]
```

### 4.3 Functional Block — Data Reduction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inpA["Raw Input: Load P in kgf"] --> proc1["Step 1: Identify Ball Diameter D"]
    inpB["Raw Input: Indentation Diameter d"] --> proc1
    proc1 --> proc2["Step 2: Compute D squared minus d squared"]
    proc2 --> proc3["Step 3: Take Square Root"]
    proc3 --> proc4["Step 4: Compute D minus sqrt term"]
    proc4 --> proc5["Step 5: Substitute into BHN Formula"]
    proc5 --> outA["Output: BHN in kgf per mm squared"]
    proc5 --> outB["Output: Designation HBS or HBW"]
```

### 4.4 Indentation Validity Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    chkA["Measure Indentation Diameter d"] --> chkB{"Is d between 0.25 D and 0.5 D"}
    chkB -- Yes --> ok1["Valid Test Report BHN"]
    chkB -- No below --> fail1["Reading Too Small Increase Load or Use Larger Ball"]
    chkB -- No above --> fail2["Reading Too Large Reduce Load or Use Smaller Ball"]
    fail1 --> retry["Repeat Test with New Parameters"]
    fail2 --> retry
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for Examiners:** In KTU viva-voce, students are routinely asked *"Why must d lie between 0.25D and 0.5D?"* — the answer lies in the fact that **outside this range**, the spherical cap geometry no longer produces a true Brinell indentation (the indenter either barely touches the surface or sinks too deep to assume constant ball curvature).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module Experiment 2]**

**Q1. Define Brinell Hardness Number. State the formula and explain each term.**
*(3 Marks — CO2, Remember Level)*

**Model Answer:**
BHN is defined as the ratio of the applied load (P) to the curved surface area (A) of the spherical indentation produced on the specimen surface by a hardened steel ball indenter.

$$
\text{BHN} = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{2P}{\pi D \left( D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2} \right)}
$$

Where: **P** = applied load (kgf), **D** = diameter of ball indenter (mm), **d** = mean diameter of indentation (mm). The unit is **kgf/mm²**.

---

**Q2. What is the significance of the P/D² ratio in the Brinell hardness test?**
*(3 Marks — CO2, Understand Level)*

**Model Answer:**
The P/D² ratio (also called the **load index K**) is a constant chosen such that the resulting indentation diameter lies between **0.25 D and 0.5 D**, ensuring geometric validity of the test. Standard values are **K = 30** for steel and cast iron, **K = 10** for copper alloys, **K = 5** for aluminium alloys, and **K = 1** for soft metals like lead. It allows results obtained with different ball diameters to be directly compared.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – End Semester Model Paper Pattern]**

---

#### ✦ Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**Q (a)** Explain the principle, apparatus, procedure, and observation method of the Brinell hardness test with a neat schematic diagram. *(7 Marks — CO3, Understand & Apply)*

**Model Answer:**

**Principle (2 Marks):**
A hardened steel ball of known diameter **D** is forced under a known static load **P** into the polished surface of the test specimen for a specified dwell time. The diameter **d** of the resulting spherical indentation is measured, and the BHN is computed as the load divided by the curved surface area of the indentation.

**Apparatus (2 Marks):**
- Brinell hardness testing machine (hydraulic / lever type)
- Indenter: Hardened steel ball (10 mm) or tungsten carbide ball
- Specimen holder / anvil
- Travelling microscope (accuracy ±0.01 mm)
- Surface grinding / polishing setup

**Procedure (2 Marks):**
1. Prepare specimen — flat, parallel, smooth surface.
2. Select appropriate ball and load using the P/D² table.
3. Mount specimen on anvil; ensure perpendicular contact.
4. Apply load gradually (no shock); maintain for **15 s**.
5. Release load, remove specimen, measure indentation along **two perpendicular directions** using the travelling microscope.
6. Compute mean diameter and substitute into the BHN formula.
7. Repeat **2–3 times** and report the mean value.

**Observation Method (1 Mark):**
The travelling microscope projects an illuminated image of the indentation onto a calibrated graticule. Two diameters d₁ and d₂ are measured perpendicular to each other to eliminate elliptical distortion.

---

**Q (b)** In a Brinell test on a cast iron specimen, a load of **3000 kgf** is applied through a **10 mm** steel ball. The mean indentation diameter is **4.2 mm**. Calculate the **BHN**, verify the **validity**, and estimate the **ultimate tensile strength**. *(7 Marks — CO3, Apply & Analyze)*

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** P = 3000 kgf, D = 10 mm, d = 4.2 mm

**Step 1 — Compute d²:**

$$
d^2 = (4.2)^2 = 17.64 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the radical:**

$$
\sqrt{D^2 - d^2} = \sqrt{100 - 17.64} = \sqrt{82.36} \approx 9.075 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into BHN formula:**

$$
\text{BHN} = \frac{2 \times 3000}{\pi \times 10 \times (10 - 9.075)} = \frac{6000}{\pi \times 10 \times 0.925}
$$

$$
\text{BHN} = \frac{6000}{29.06} \approx 206.5 \text{ kgf/mm}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Validity check:**

$$
0.25 D = 2.5 \text{ mm}, \quad 0.5 D = 5.0 \text{ mm}
$$

Since 2.5 mm ≤ 4.2 mm ≤ 5.0 mm, the test is **VALID** ✅.

**Step 5 — Estimated UTS (empirical for steels):**

$$
\sigma_u \approx 0.35 \times \text{BHN} = 0.35 \times 206.5 \approx 72.3 \text{ kgf/mm}^2 \approx 709 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Result:**

$$
\boxed{\text{BHN} \approx 207 \text{ kgf/mm}^2 ; \quad \text{UTS} \approx 709 \text{ MPa}}
$$

---

#### ✦ Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Alternative) — 14 Marks

**Q (a)** Derive the Brinell hardness formula from the geometry of a spherical indentation. State clearly the assumptions made. *(7 Marks — CO2, Understand & Apply)*

**Model Solution:**

**Assumption:** The indentation is a perfect spherical cap of radius **R = D/2** (the indenter does not deform plastically).

**Step 1 — Geometry:**
From the right-triangle formed by R, d/2, and (R − h):

$$
R^2 = \left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^2 + (R - h)^2
$$

**Step 2 — Expand and simplify:**

$$
\frac{D^2}{4} = \frac{d^2}{4} + \frac{D^2}{4} - Dh + h^2
$$

$$
Dh = h^2 + \frac{d^2}{4}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve the quadratic in h:**

$$
h = \frac{D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2}}{2} \quad \text{(physically valid root)}
$$

**Step 4 — Curved surface area of spherical cap:**

$$
A = 2 \pi R h = \pi D h = \frac{\pi D}{2}\left( D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2} \right)
$$

**Step 5 — Apply definition of BHN = P / A:**

$$
\boxed{\text{BHN} = \frac{2P}{\pi D \left( D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2} \right)}}
$$

---

**Q (b)** Compare Brinell, Rockwell, and Vickers hardness tests based on indenter type, load range, indentation shape, and typical applications. *(7 Marks — CO3, Analyze)*

**Model Answer (Tabular Comparison):**

| Feature | **Brinell (BHN)** | **Rockwell (HR)** | **Vickers (HV)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Indenter** | Hardened steel / WC ball (1–10 mm) | Diamond cone (HRA/HRc) or steel ball (HRB) | Square-based diamond pyramid (136° apex) |
| **Load Range** | 1–3000 kgf | 60–150 kgf (major) | 1–120 kgf |
| **Indentation Shape** | Circular (spherical cap) | Circular (conical or spherical) | Square (rhombic) |
| **Measurement** | Diameter (microscope) | Depth (direct dial reading) | Diagonal length (microscope) |
| **Formula** | $2P / [\pi D(D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2})]$ | Direct scale reading | $1.854 P / d^2$ |
| **Best Suited For** | Cast iron, forgings, coarse-grained metals | Hardened steels, production QC | Micro-hardness, thin sheets, case-hardened surfaces |
| **Surface Finish Reqd.** | Moderate | Smooth | Highly polished |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks (Up to 3–5 Marks Per Question):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply by 2** in the numerator of the BHN formula. Many students write `P / [πD(D − √(D² − d²))]` — this is **WRONG** and yields half the correct value.
> 2. **Failing to verify the validity range** (0.25D ≤ d ≤ 0.5D). Examiners explicitly look for this step; skipping it costs **1 mark**.
> 3. **Mixing up units** — Load in Newtons vs kgf. The KTU formula uses **kgf**. 1 kgf ≈ 9.81 N. Forgetting the conversion gives numerically wrong answers.
> 4. **Reporting BHN without proper designation** (e.g., HBS 10/3000/15). Examiners deduct marks for missing the standard notation.
> 5. **Using approximate h = d²/(4D)** in the formula — this gives an approximate BHN. KTU expects the **exact formula** unless explicitly stated otherwise.
> 6. **Not repeating the test** — a single reading is not statistically reliable; examiners expect at least **2–3 trials** with the mean reported.

### 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔥 Key Points to Memorize Before the KTU Exam:**

- ✅ **Master Formula:** $\text{BHN} = \dfrac{2P}{\pi D (D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2})}$
- ✅ **Standard Indenter:** 10 mm hardened steel ball; tungsten carbide for hard materials.
- ✅ **Standard Load for Steel:** 3000 kgf (P/D² = 30).
- ✅ **Dwell Time:** 10–15 s (15 s preferred for steel).
- ✅ **Validity Window:** $0.25 D \leq d \leq 0.5 D$.
- ✅ **Two Perpendicular Measurements** are mandatory; report the **mean**.
- ✅ **UTS Correlation:** $\sigma_u \approx 0.35 \times \text{BHN}$ (steel only).
- ✅ **Designation Format:** HBS 10/3000/15 (or HBW if WC indenter).
- ✅ **Geometry Origin:** Formula derived from spherical-cap geometry with depth $h = \frac{1}{2}(D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2})$.
- ✅ **Limitation:** Cannot be used on very hard materials (BHN > 450); not suitable for thin specimens or small parts.
- ✅ **Modern Note:** "HBW" replaces "HBS" per ASTM E10-18 to distinguish tungsten carbide (W) from steel (S) indenters.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-Line Summary for Viva:**
> *"Brinell hardness is the ratio of the applied load to the spherical-cap surface area of the indentation produced by a hardened steel ball, with the standard condition that P/D² is held constant for a given material class to ensure comparable results."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Hardness of the Given Specimen by Rockwell Hardness Test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGL308 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Hardness of the Given Specimen by Rockwell Hardness Test

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rockwell Hardness Test (ASTM E18 / IS 1586):** A non-destructive, indentation-based hardness measurement technique in which the hardness number is derived from the **net increase in depth of penetration** $(h)$ of a hard indenter (diamond cone or hardened steel ball) caused by applying and then removing a **major load**, while a constant **preliminary minor load** is maintained. The result is read directly on a dial gauge graduated in arbitrary Rockwell units.

Mathematically, for any scale:
$$HR = N - \frac{h}{s}$$
where $N$ is a scale-constant offset, $s$ is the scale unit (typically **0.002 mm** for standard scales), and $h$ is the permanent increment in penetration depth measured in mm after the major load is removed.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Pressing Your Thumb into Dough:**
> Imagine pressing your thumb into a piece of dough. If the dough is soft, your thumb sinks deep; if it's hard, your thumb sinks only a little. The **Rockwell test does the same thing** — it presses a very hard "thumb" (the indenter) into a metal specimen with a known force and measures **how deep** it goes. The shallower the dent, the harder the metal, and the **higher** the Rockwell Hardness Number (HR).
>
> The clever twist: the machine automatically **subtracts** the soft first push (minor load) so that surface roughness, scale, and minor imperfections don't fool the reading. Only the **extra depth** caused by the heavy load counts.

## 1.3 Standard Scales Used in KTU Labs

| Scale | Indenter | Major Load (kgf) | Typical Applications |
|-------|----------|------------------|----------------------|
| **HRA** | Diamond Cone (Brale) | 60 | Tungsten carbide, cemented carbides, thin steel |
| **HRB** | 1/16 in. steel ball | 100 | Annealed brass, soft steel, aluminium alloys |
| **HRC** | Diamond Cone (Brale) | **150** | Hardened steels, tool steels, heat-treated alloys |
| **HRD** | Diamond Cone | 100 | Thin steel, case-hardened surfaces |
| **HRE** | 1/8 in. ball | 100 | Very soft materials, bearing alloys |

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal minor load (preliminary load):** Always **10 kgf**, irrespective of scale. This eliminates the effect of surface finish, seating, and minor elasticity.

## 1.4 Why Rockwell and not Brinell/Vickers?

| Test | Reading Method | Indenter | Best For |
|------|----------------|----------|----------|
| Brinell | Optical (diameter of impression) | 10 mm ball / 3000 kgf | Rough, coarse-grained metals |
| Vickers | Optical (diagonal length) | Diamond pyramid | Micro-hardness, research |
| **Rockwell** | **Direct dial / digital readout** | **Diamond or ball** | **Production QC, rapid shop-floor testing** |

The Rockwell test's killer advantage: **no microscope, no measurement, no calculation** — the number is read straight off the machine in ~10–15 seconds, making it the most widely used industrial hardness test in the world.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — The "Two-Load" Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The genius of the Rockwell method is the **two-stage loading** procedure that automatically compensates for surface irregularities and elastic recovery of the specimen.

The test is performed in **three sequential stages**:

**Stage 1 — Minor Load Application (Seating)**
- The indenter is brought into contact with the test piece.
- A **minor (preliminary) load of 10 kgf** is applied gradually.
- This seats the indenter firmly, crushes surface scale, oxide films, and minor roughness, and overcomes the initial elastic compression of the testing machine frame.
- The dial indicator is set to a **reference datum (zero for diamond, 30 for ball scales)**. This depth is $h_0$.

**Stage 2 — Major Load Application**
- Without removing the minor load, the **major load** (60, 100, or 150 kgf depending on the scale) is applied smoothly over 4–8 seconds.
- The total load is now $F_{minor} + F_{major} = $ **10 kgf + major load**.
- The indenter penetrates to a maximum depth $h_{max}$.
- The dwell time at full major load is held for a specific period (typically 4 seconds for hard metals, up to 15 seconds for softer or more time-dependent materials) to allow **plastic flow** (creep) to stabilize.

**Stage 3 — Major Load Removal, Reading Under Minor Load**
- The major load is **removed** first, but the **minor load remains**.
- The specimen partially **elastically recovers**, and the indenter rises by an amount equal to the elastic recovery.
- The **permanent (plastic) depth of penetration** $h$ is what remains — and this is what the dial reads, expressed in Rockwell units.

$$h = h_{max} - h_{elastic\ recovery} - h_0$$

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use the LaTeX vertical bar `\vert` instead of `|` inside all formula tables to comply with markdown table syntax.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Rockwell Hardness (Diamond) | $HR = 100 - \dfrac{h}{0.002}$ | dimensionless | Scales A, C, D — uses 0.002 mm unit |
| 2 | Rockwell Hardness (Ball) | $HR = 130 - \dfrac{h}{0.002}$ | dimensionless | Scales B, E, F, G, etc. — uses 0.002 mm unit |
| 3 | General form | $HR = N - \dfrac{h}{s}$ | dimensionless | $N = 100$ (diamond), $N = 130$ (ball); $s = 0.002$ mm |
| 4 | Net depth | $h = h_{plastic} = h_{total} - h_{elastic} - h_0$ | mm | Measured under residual minor load |
| 5 | Minor (preliminary) load | $F_{minor}$ | **10 kgf** | Universal across all standard scales |
| 6 | Major load (HRC) | $F_{major}$ | **150 kgf** | Total = 160 kgf |
| 7 | Major load (HRA) | $F_{major}$ | **60 kgf** | Total = 70 kgf |
| 8 | Major load (HRB) | $F_{major}$ | **100 kgf** | Total = 110 kgf |
| 9 | Indenter geometry (diamond) | Apex angle 120°, tip radius 0.200 mm | — | Brale indenter per ISO 6508 |
| 10 | Indenter geometry (ball) | Diameter 1/16 in. (1.588 mm) hardened steel | — | For HRB |
| 11 | Approx. Brinell → HRC conversion | $HRC \approx \dfrac{HB - 40}{10}$ (approx) | — | Empirical, valid for steels 200–500 HB |
| 12 | Specimen minimum thickness | $t \ge 10 \times \text{depth of penetration}$ | mm | Rule of thumb to avoid anvil effects |

## 2.3 Why the Constants $N$ and $s$ Exist — A Geometric Insight

- The scale constant $N$ (100 for diamond, 130 for ball) gives a **convenient positive reading** even for the softest materials on the scale. Without $N$, soft materials would yield large negative numbers.
- The scale unit $s = 0.002$ mm = **2 μm** is chosen so that **one Rockwell unit corresponds to a depth change of 0.002 mm**. This gives reasonable resolution on the dial gauge (typically 100 divisions over a 0.2 mm range).
- A reading of **HRC 60**, for example, corresponds to a **permanent penetration depth** of:
$$h = (100 - 60) \times 0.002 = 0.080\ \text{mm} = 80\ \mu m$$
- A reading of **HRB 90** corresponds to:
$$h = (130 - 90) \times 0.002 = 0.080\ \text{mm}$$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Use of Rockwell Hardness |
|--------|--------------------------|
| **Heat treatment QC** | Verifying hardness after quenching, tempering, case-hardening (carburizing, nitriding) |
| **Tool manufacturing** | Acceptance testing of high-speed steel (HSS) and carbide cutting tools |
| **Automotive** | Hardness of crankshafts, camshafts, gears, brake discs |
| **Aerospace** | Aluminium alloy sheets (HRB), titanium alloys (HRC) |
| **Forging & casting** | Quick in-process checks on shop floor |
| **Failure analysis** | First-pass diagnostic of heat-treated components |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Rockwell Hardness Formula

### 3.1.1 Defining the Geometry of Penetration

Let:
- $h_0$ = depth of penetration under **minor load only** (10 kgf), measured from the original surface to the indenter tip.
- $h_1$ = depth of penetration under **total load** (minor + major).
- $h_2$ = depth of penetration after the **major load is removed** (only minor load remains). This is the **permanent plastic depth**.

The **net permanent (plastic) increment in depth** caused by the major load is:
$$h = h_2 - h_0$$

This is the quantity that defines hardness — it is **inversely proportional** to hardness (deep dent = soft, shallow dent = hard).

### 3.1.2 Inverting the Depth to Obtain a Hardness Number

Since $h$ decreases as hardness increases, we need a **decreasing linear function of $h$** that is **positive** for typical engineering materials. The most general linear form is:
$$HR = A - B \cdot h$$

The constants $A$ and $B$ are chosen to give:
1. **A "zero" reference point** for the hardest material on the scale.
2. **A unit (scale factor)** of 0.002 mm per Rockwell point.

**Choosing B (the scale unit):**
By definition, the depth scale unit is set as:
$$B = \frac{1}{s} = \frac{1}{0.002\ \text{mm}} = 500\ \text{mm}^{-1}$$

So each 0.002 mm change in depth = 1 Rockwell point. Therefore:
$$HR = A - \frac{h}{0.002}$$

**Choosing A (the offset constant):**

*Case 1 — Diamond Cone Indenter (used for HRA, HRC, HRD):*
The diamond cone is sharper and produces a shallower impression. To keep the readings on a convenient positive scale (and to match historical Brinell-like ranges), the constant is set to:
$$A = 100$$
yielding:
$$\boxed{HR_{diamond} = 100 - \frac{h}{0.002}}$$

*Case 2 — Steel Ball Indenter (used for HRB, HRE, HRF, etc.):*
The ball is less sharp and produces a deeper impression. To keep dial readings positive and continuous with the diamond scales, the constant is shifted up to:
$$A = 130$$
yielding:
$$\boxed{HR_{ball} = 130 - \frac{h}{0.002}}$$

### 3.1.3 Final Generalized Expression

The full KTU / ASTM / ISO-compatible expression is:
$$HR = N - \frac{h}{s}$$
where:
- $N = 100$ for diamond cone scales
- $N = 130$ for ball scales
- $s = 0.002$ mm (universal scale unit)
- $h$ is in mm, measured as the **permanent plastic depth** between the minor-load-only condition and the residual-minor-load condition.

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example (Numerical Evaluation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU-style):** In a Rockwell hardness test on the C scale (HRC), the depth of penetration under the 10 kgf minor load was measured as $h_0 = 0.040$ mm. After applying the major load (150 kgf) and removing it, the depth under residual minor load was $h_2 = 0.132$ mm. Determine the Rockwell C hardness number.

**Step 1 — Compute net permanent depth.**
$$h = h_2 - h_0 = 0.132\ \text{mm} - 0.040\ \text{mm} = 0.092\ \text{mm}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the diamond-cone formula.**
$$HRC = 100 - \frac{h}{0.002}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the fraction.**
$$\frac{h}{0.002} = \frac{0.092}{0.002} = 46.0$$

**Step 4 — Compute the final hardness number.**
$$HRC = 100 - 46.0 = 54.0$$

**Result:** $HRC = 54$ — a typical value for a **tempered tool steel**.

### 3.2.1 Reverse Calculation (Depth from Hardness Number)

If a specimen gives $HRC = 62$, the corresponding permanent depth is:
$$h = (100 - 62) \times 0.002 = 38 \times 0.002 = 0.076\ \text{mm}$$

### 3.2.2 Validation Across Common Engineering Materials

| Material | Typical Hardness | Computed Net Depth $h$ (mm) |
|----------|------------------|------------------------------|
| Annealed copper | HRB ~ 20 | $h = (130 - 20) \times 0.002 = 0.220$ |
| Mild steel (as-rolled) | HRB ~ 70 | $h = (130 - 70) \times 0.002 = 0.120$ |
| Hardened tool steel | HRC ~ 60 | $h = (100 - 60) \times 0.002 = 0.080$ |
| Tungsten carbide | HRA ~ 92 | $h = (100 - 92) \times 0.002 = 0.016$ |

## 3.3 Sources of Error — Analytical Breakdown

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners frequently test knowledge of error sources and their remedies. Memorize this table.

| Source of Error | Effect on HR Reading | Remedy |
|-----------------|----------------------|--------|
| Surface roughness or scale | Falsely low reading | Grind/polish specimen (Ra ≤ 0.4 μm) |
| Specimen too thin (anvil effect) | Falsely low reading (indenter punches through) | $t \ge 10 \times$ penetration depth, or use a smaller scale |
| Misalignment of indenter with surface normal | Asymmetric impression, low reading | Mount specimen on flat anvil, check perpendicularity |
| Vibration or shock loading | Erratic dial reading | Apply loads smoothly; use hydraulic dashpot |
| Indenter wear (blunt diamond or ball) | Falsely high reading | Calibrate daily on certified test blocks |
| Incorrect dwell time (creep) | Falsely high reading on soft metals | Hold 4–15 s as per scale |
| Reading dial before major load removed | Wrong reference | Always read after major load removal under minor load |
| Temperature drift of machine | Drift in zero | Calibrate at 20 ± 2 °C |

## 3.4 Specimen Preparation Rules (KTU Lab Standard)

1. **Flatness:** The test surface must be flat; a convex surface gives a falsely **low** reading.
2. **Smoothness:** Grind or polish the surface to remove scratches, oxide, decarburized layer.
3. **Thickness:** $t \ge 10 \times$ depth of penetration. For HRC 60, $h \approx 0.08$ mm, so $t \ge 0.8$ mm is a safe minimum (1.5 mm recommended).
4. **Parallelism:** Test and supporting surfaces must be parallel.
5. **Cleanliness:** Free from oil, dirt, and grease.
6. **No previous indentations within:** $3 \times$ indenter diameter from the new indent.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Rockwell Testing Machine

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    D --> E[Dwell Period 4 to 15 seconds]
    E --> F[Remove Major Load, Keep Minor Load]
    F --> G[Elastic Recovery of Specimen]
    G --> H[Read Permanent Plastic Depth h on Dial]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Rockwell hardness. State the formula and the values of the constants used.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Rockwell hardness** is a measure of the resistance of a material to indentation, determined by measuring the **net permanent depth of penetration** of a hard indenter (diamond cone or steel ball) under a major load, after a preliminary minor load.
>
> **Formula:**
> $$HR = N - \frac{h}{s}$$
>
> **Constants:** For the **diamond cone** indenter (HRA, HRC, HRD), $N = 100$. For the **ball** indenter (HRB, HRE, etc.), $N = 130$. The scale unit $s = 0.002$ mm is universal.
>
> *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for constants — distribution as per KTU valuation key.)*

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Why is a minor (preliminary) load of 10 kgf applied before the major load in the Rockwell test?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The 10 kgf minor load is applied to:
1. **Seat the indenter firmly** on the test surface, eliminating the effect of surface roughness, oxide films, and minor scale.
2. **Establish a datum (zero) reference** on the dial gauge before applying the major load.
3. **Pre-compress the testing machine frame** and overcome initial elastic deflections, so they do not enter the depth measurement.
4. Provide a consistent **starting point** for measuring the depth increment caused solely by the major load.
*(Any three points = 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Set A]**

**a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **Rockwell hardness testing procedure**. List the standard scales HRA, HRB, and HRC with their indenters and major loads. (7 marks)

**b)** In a Rockwell C test, the permanent depth of penetration is found to be $0.076$ mm. Calculate the HRC value. A specimen of $1.5$ mm thick hardened steel is to be tested. Is the HRC scale appropriate? Justify with reference to the specimen thickness rule. (7 marks)

---

#### Solution to Q-A(a) — Procedure with Indenters and Loads (7 marks)

**Procedure (any standard textbook ordering — 5 marks):**

1. **Select the appropriate scale** (HRA, HRB, HRC) based on material type and expected hardness.
2. **Mount the indenter** in the machine spindle and place the specimen on the anvil.
3. **Raise the anvil** to bring the specimen into contact with the indenter.
4. **Apply the minor load of 10 kgf** smoothly using the handwheel. The dial indicator pointer moves to a reference position — set this to **zero** (for diamond cone) or **30** (for ball).
5. **Apply the major load** (60, 100, or 150 kgf depending on scale) by releasing the dashpot-controlled lever.
6. **Hold the major load** for the specified dwell time (4–15 s) to allow plastic flow.
7. **Remove the major load** (return the lever) — keep the minor load applied.
8. **Read the dial gauge** under residual minor load. The reading is the Rockwell hardness number, suffixed by the scale letter (e.g., HRC 62).

**Standard Scales Table (2 marks):**

| Scale | Indenter | Major Load | Minor Load | Typical Use |
|-------|----------|------------|------------|-------------|
| HRA | Diamond cone, 120° | 60 kgf | 10 kgf | Hard carbides, thin steel |
| HRB | 1/16 in steel ball | 100 kgf | 10 kgf | Soft steel, brass, aluminium |
| HRC | Diamond cone, 120° | 150 kgf | 10 kgf | Hardened tool steel, dies |

---

#### Solution to Q-A(b) — Hardness Calculation and Thickness Check (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Apply the HRC formula (2 marks).**
$$HRC = 100 - \frac{h}{0.002}$$
$$HRC = 100 - \frac{0.076}{0.002}$$
$$HRC = 100 - 38 = \mathbf{62}$$

**Step 2 — Specimen thickness rule (3 marks).**

The KTU / ASTM rule states that the specimen thickness must satisfy:
$$t \ge 10 \times h$$
where $h$ is the net penetration depth for the measured hardness.

For HRC 62, the corresponding net depth is $h = (100 - 62) \times 0.002 = 0.076$ mm, hence:
$$t_{min} = 10 \times 0.076 = 0.76\ \text{mm}$$

**Step 3 — Check the given specimen (2 marks).**

Given specimen thickness $t = 1.5$ mm. Since $1.5\ \text{mm} > 0.76\ \text{mm}$, the specimen is **thick enough** for the HRC test. **Yes, the HRC scale is appropriate.**

Additional safeguard check — for absolute safety, the actual test surface should also be at least 0.76 mm below the anvil-side surface. The given 1.5 mm comfortably exceeds this requirement, leaving ~0.74 mm of material on the underside without the risk of the anvil punching through.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]** (Internal Choice)

**a)** Explain the **difference between minor load and major load** in the Rockwell test. What is the role of the **elastic recovery** of the specimen after the major load is removed? (7 marks)

**b)** A specimen tested on the HRB scale (1/16 in ball, 100 kgf major load) shows a net permanent penetration depth of $0.120$ mm. Compute the HRB value. Also state **two advantages and two limitations** of the Rockwell test over the Brinell test. (7 marks)

---

#### Solution to Q-B(a) — Minor vs Major Load + Elastic Recovery (7 marks)

**Difference between Minor and Major Load (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Minor (Preliminary) Load | Major Load |
|-----------|--------------------------|------------|
| Magnitude | **10 kgf** (universal) | **60, 100, or 150 kgf** (scale-dependent) |
| Purpose | Seat indenter, eliminate surface roughness, establish datum | Cause plastic (permanent) indentation |
| Removed before reading? | **No** — remains throughout | **Yes** — removed before reading |
| Effect on reading | Sets the zero reference | Determines the depth $h$ that defines hardness |

**Role of Elastic Recovery (3 marks):**
When the major load is removed, the specimen **elastically recovers** — the portion of penetration that was due to elastic deformation springs back. Only the **plastic (permanent) deformation** remains. The Rockwell test specifically measures this plastic component because:

1. Elastic recovery is **material-dependent** and reversible — it does not represent a permanent material property change.
2. By reading the depth under the **residual minor load**, the machine **mechanically subtracts** the elastic recovery and any frame deflection.
3. The result is a **stable, repeatable hardness value** that correlates directly with the material's resistance to plastic flow (yielding).
4. The dwell time at full major load is chosen to allow time-dependent creep to stabilize, ensuring consistent elastic recovery between tests.

---

#### Solution to Q-B(b) — HRB Calculation + Comparison (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Apply the HRB (ball) formula (2 marks).**
$$HRB = 130 - \frac{h}{0.002}$$
$$HRB = 130 - \frac{0.120}{0.002}$$
$$HRB = 130 - 60 = \mathbf{70}$$

**Step 2 — Two advantages of Rockwell over Brinell (2.5 marks):**
1. **Direct reading** — no optical measurement of indent diameter is required, eliminating human and instrument errors.
2. **Faster** — a complete Rockwell test takes 10–15 s, while a Brinell test requires impression measurement and a calculation step.
3. (Optional) Operates on both very soft and very hard materials using multiple scales.
4. (Optional) Suitable for finished parts and small specimens without major preparation.

**Step 3 — Two limitations of Rockwell over Brinell (2.5 marks):**
1. **Scale dependence** — the same material gives different HR numbers on different scales, so the scale must always be reported (e.g., HRC 60, not just "60").
2. **Less suitable for coarse-grained or heterogeneous materials** — a small indent may fall on a single grain, giving non-representative values; Brinell's larger impression averages over many grains.
3. (Optional) Requires a flat, parallel specimen; rough or curved surfaces give erratic results.
4. (Optional) Indenter (especially diamond) is sensitive to damage from shock loading.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Always include the scale suffix** when quoting a Rockwell value. Writing "62" instead of "HRC 62" costs at least 0.5 mark per occurrence.
> 2. **The minor load is always 10 kgf** — students frequently confuse it with the major load. Memorize: "minor = 10, major varies".
> 3. **Reading is taken AFTER major load removal, under residual minor load.** Reading at full major load is a common wrong answer.
> 4. **Specimen thickness rule:** $t \ge 10 \times h$. Many students quote $t \ge 10 \times d$ (Brinell rule) and lose 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Don't mix up the two formulas:** $100 - h/0.002$ is for diamond, $130 - h/0.002$ is for ball. Mixing these gives a wrong value of **30 Rockwell points** — a fatal error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Points to Memorize for KTU 2024 Viva + ESE:**

- **Definition:** Rockwell hardness measures the **net permanent penetration depth** under a major load, after seating with a **10 kgf** minor load.
- **Master formula:** $HR = N - h/s$, with $N = 100$ (diamond) or $130$ (ball), $s = 0.002$ mm.
- **HRA:** Diamond + 60 kgf major → for tungsten carbide, thin hard steel.
- **HRB:** 1/16 in ball + 100 kgf major → for soft steel, brass, aluminium.
- **HRC:** Diamond + 150 kgf major → for hardened tool steel, dies, gears.
- **Minor load = 10 kgf, ALWAYS** — universal across every standard Rockwell scale.
- **Reading point:** After major load removal, under residual **minor** load.
- **Dwell time:** 4–15 seconds at full major load.
- **Specimen thickness rule:** $t \ge 10 \times h$ (penetration depth).
- **Surface preparation:** Flat, parallel, ground/polished, no oxide/scale.
- **Indenter separation rule:** At least $3 \times$ indenter diameter between successive impressions.
- **Diamond cone (Brale) geometry:** 120° apex, 0.200 mm tip radius.
- **Ball indenter:** 1/16 in (1.588 mm) hardened steel; must be replaced when worn.
- **No microscope needed** — direct dial or digital readout is the principal advantage over Brinell and Vickers.
- **Calibration:** Use certified test blocks (HRC ~ 25, 45, 65 typical set) daily before testing.
- **Errors to avoid:** Convex/rough surfaces, thin specimens, blunt indenters, shock loading, misaligned indenter.
- **Industrial applications:** Heat-treatment QC, tool acceptance, automotive components, aerospace alloys, forging in-process checks.
- **Compare with Brinell:** Rockwell is faster, direct, scale-based; Brinell is optical, single-scale, better for rough/coarse materials.
- **KTU pattern recognition:** Questions typically test (i) procedure, (ii) formula application with given $h$, (iii) scale selection, (iv) specimen thickness validity, and (v) error sources.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Impact Load on a Specimen by Izod Impact Test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGL308 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Impact Load on a Specimen by Izod Impact Test

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Izod Impact Test** is a standardized high-strain-rate destructive mechanical test prescribed under **ASTM D256** (for plastics) and **ASTM E23** (for metals) used to determine the **impact resistance (toughness)** of a material. In this test, a standardized notched specimen is held as a **vertical cantilever beam** (clamped rigidly at its lower end with the notch facing the striker), and is struck and fractured by a single swing of a pendulum released from a known fixed height. The energy absorbed in fracturing the specimen is read directly from a calibrated dial gauge attached to the pendulum, and the **impact strength (Izod impact value)** is computed as the absorbed energy per unit original cross-sectional area at the notch.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCAGL308 – Module 2 Experiment):**
> The Izod test belongs to the family of **pendulum-impact tests** that quantify a material's ability to absorb energy and resist fracture under **dynamic/impact loading** — a loading regime distinctly different from the quasi-static tensile/compressive loading covered in Experiments 1 & 2 (Tension/Compression testing). The test is performed at **ambient temperature** unless otherwise specified, and the result is reported in **Joules (J)** or **Joules per meter (J/m)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine snapping a green twig versus a piece of dry chalk:
- The **green twig** bends a lot before breaking → it absorbs a lot of energy → **high toughness**.
- The **chalk** snaps instantly with little bending → absorbs very little energy → **low toughness**.

The Izod test is simply a **machine that swings a heavy hammer** at a small bar of material to measure *how much energy* that bar can soak up before snapping. Think of it like this:

> *You have a child on a swing (the pendulum). You pull the swing up to a known height (initial potential energy). You let go. The swing flies down, hits a small bar of chocolate (the specimen) clamped in its path, breaks it, and continues forward but not as high as before (because some energy was used to break the chocolate). The difference in swing height — before versus after — tells you how much energy the chocolate absorbed while breaking.*

- **Heavy pendulum, high initial drop, small specimen** → easy comparison between materials.
- **The notch** is a deliberate stress-concentration feature (a tiny pre-crack) that forces the fracture to initiate at a known location — this is what makes the test **repeatable and standardized**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (Izod vs Charpy) — Frequently tested in KTU viva:**
> - **Izod**: Specimen held **vertically as a cantilever**, notch on the **same side as the striker** (tension side).
> - **Charpy**: Specimen held **horizontally as a simply-supported beam**, notch on the **opposite side** (tension side, away from striker).
> - Both measure the *same physical quantity* (impact energy) but use different specimen geometries and clamping methods, leading to different numerical values for the *same* material.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Test Parameters

| Parameter | Standard Value (Metals – ASTM E23) | Standard Value (Plastics – ISO 180 / ASTM D256) |
|---|---|---|
| Specimen length | **75 mm** | **80 mm** (ISO 180 Type 1A) |
| Specimen cross-section | 10 mm × 10 mm | 10 mm × 4 mm |
| Notch type | **45° V-notch** | **45° V-notch** |
| Notch depth | **2 mm** | **2 mm** (Type A) |
| Notch radius (root) | 0.25 mm | 0.25 mm |
| Striker velocity at impact | 3 – 6 m/s | 3 – 6 m/s |
| Striker edge radius | 8 mm | 8 mm |
| Result units | Joules (J) | kJ/m² (ISO) or J/m (ASTM) |

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Background — Why Impact Testing?

Under **quasi-static loading** (tension/compression tests at low strain rates), a ductile material like mild steel may show excellent strength and ductility. However, many engineering components experience **sudden, high-velocity loads** — for example:
- A hammer striking a chisel
- A bullet striking armor plate
- A drop hammer on forging dies
- Sudden failure of a crane hook during lifting

The behavior of materials under such **impact loads** is fundamentally different from their static behavior because:
1. **Strain rate is very high** (10² to 10⁴ s⁻¹) → dislocations have less time to move → yield strength rises.
2. Materials that are ductile under static load may become **brittle under impact** (a phenomenon called **strain-rate sensitivity**).
3. The presence of **notches, sharp corners, or pre-existing cracks** dramatically reduces impact strength — this is the **notch sensitivity** of the material.

Hence, the impact test was developed to **quantify a material's notch toughness under dynamic loading**, providing a single engineering index for material selection in safety-critical applications.

### 2.2 The Physics of the Pendulum — Step-by-Step Logic

The Izod impact testing machine is essentially a **gravity pendulum** with a calibrated energy-dissipation dial. The test logic unfolds as follows:

**Step 1 — Cocking the Pendulum:**
The pendulum hammer is raised to a precise initial angle **α** (typically 150° from the vertical, on the opposite side of the specimen). At this position, the pendulum possesses a known **gravitational potential energy** $E_i$.

**Step 2 — Release and Free Fall:**
A spring-loaded latch releases the pendulum. The pendulum swings down under gravity, converting $E_i$ entirely into **kinetic energy** at the bottom-most (striking) position. This kinetic energy is what will be partly transferred to the specimen.

**Step 3 — Impact and Fracture:**
At the lowest point of swing, the pendulum's striker edge strikes the notched specimen (held as a cantilever). The specimen absorbs a portion of the kinetic energy to:
- Initiate and propagate a crack from the notch root
- Plastically deform the region around the crack
- Throw away the broken halves (kinetic energy of fragments)
- Produce sound and heat (minor energy loss)

**Step 4 — Overswing and Final Reading:**
The pendulum continues forward after breaking the specimen, but rises only to a smaller final angle **β** (the *overswing angle*). The **energy absorbed by the specimen** equals the difference in potential energy of the pendulum at angles α and β.

**Step 5 — Calculation of Impact Value:**
The dial gauge is calibrated such that the **angular difference is directly converted to energy in Joules**, so the reading on the dial gives the impact energy absorbed.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Initial potential energy of pendulum | $E_i$ | $E_i = W \cdot R \cdot (1 - \cos\alpha)$ | J (Joules) | W = weight of pendulum (N), R = pendulum arm length to center of gravity (m) |
| 2 | Final potential energy (after fracture) | $E_f$ | $E_f = W \cdot R \cdot (1 - \cos\beta)$ | J | $\beta$ = overswing (rise) angle |
| 3 | **Energy absorbed by specimen** | $U$ | $U = W \cdot R \cdot (\cos\beta - \cos\alpha)$ | **J** | The KEY formula for the experiment. Note the **sign flip** vs $E_i$ expression. |
| 4 | Initial kinetic energy of striker | $KE$ | $KE = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ | J | m = pendulum mass, v = striker velocity at impact (≈ √(2gR(1−cosα))) |
| 5 | **Izod Impact Strength** | $IS$ | $IS = \dfrac{U}{A_{notch}}$ | **J/m²** or **J/mm²** | $A_{notch}$ = cross-sectional area *at the notch root* (original area, not fractured area) |
| 6 | Cross-section at notch | $A_{notch}$ | $A_{notch} = b \cdot d_n$ | mm² or m² | b = width, $d_n$ = residual depth at notch (10 − notch depth) |
| 7 | Striking velocity | $v$ | $v = \sqrt{2gR(1-\cos\alpha)}$ | m/s | g = 9.81 m/s² |
| 8 | Pendulum energy range (typical) | $E_{max}$ | 0.5 – 400 J | J | Different machines for different material toughness ranges |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial Sign Convention for the Energy Equation:**
> Since $\beta < \alpha$ always (pendulum can never rise higher after losing energy), we have $\cos\beta > \cos\alpha$ (cosine is decreasing on [0, π]), which makes $(\cos\beta - \cos\alpha) > 0$ — a **positive** value, as it should be for an absorbed energy.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive crashworthiness**: Selecting body panels that absorb energy in a crash.
- **Pressure vessel and pipeline design** (ASME codes require Charpy/Izod testing for low-temperature service to prevent brittle fracture).
- **Defense and armor design** (ballistic limit calculations).
- **Selection of materials for cold-environment applications** (Arctic pipelines, ship hulls in polar waters) — impact tests are routinely run at sub-zero temperatures to detect the **ductile-to-brittle transition temperature (DBTT)** of steels.
- **Quality control in steel mills** for incoming batch inspection.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Energy Equation

**Statement:** Derive the energy absorbed by the specimen, $U$, in terms of the initial angle $\alpha$, the overswing angle $\beta$, the weight of the pendulum $W$, and the pendulum arm length to its center of gravity $R$.

**Setup:**
- Let point **O** be the pivot of the pendulum.
- Let point **G** be the center of gravity of the pendulum hammer assembly.
- The distance $OG = R$ (constant, a geometric property of the machine).
- The pendulum weight is $W$ (in Newtons), so its mass is $m = W/g$.

**Position 1 — Pendulum raised to initial angle $\alpha$:**
The center of gravity G is lifted to a height $h_1$ above the lowest (reference) position. From the geometry of a pendulum:

$$
h_1 = R - R\cos\alpha = R(1 - \cos\alpha)
$$

**Position 2 — Pendulum has fractured the specimen and overswung to angle $\beta$:**
The center of gravity G is now at a height $h_2$ above the reference position:

$$
h_2 = R - R\cos\beta = R(1 - \cos\beta)
$$

**Step 1 — Compute the loss in potential energy of the pendulum:**
By the **Work-Energy Theorem** (assuming friction at the pivot is negligible — the dial gauge is calibrated to compensate for this), the loss in potential energy of the pendulum equals the work done in fracturing the specimen:

$$
U = W \cdot h_1 - W \cdot h_2
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the geometric heights:**

$$
U = W \cdot R(1 - \cos\alpha) - W \cdot R(1 - \cos\beta)
$$

**Step 3 — Expand and simplify:**

$$
U = WR - WR\cos\alpha - WR + WR\cos\beta
$$

**Step 4 — The $WR$ terms cancel pairwise:**

$$
U = WR\cos\beta - WR\cos\alpha
$$

**Step 5 — Factor the common term $WR$:**

$$
U = WR(\cos\beta - \cos\alpha)
$$

**Conclusion:** The energy absorbed by the specimen is:

$$
\boxed{U = W R (\cos\beta - \cos\alpha)}
$$

Since $\beta < \alpha$ and both lie in $(0, \pi/2]$, we have $\cos\beta > \cos\alpha$, ensuring $U > 0$. The result has units of $\text{N} \cdot \text{m} = \text{J}$, as expected for an energy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** The dial gauge on a real Izod machine is *pre-calibrated* by the manufacturer such that the pointer deflection directly reads $U$ in Joules. So in a lab exam, you typically just **read $U$ from the dial** — but you must still be able to **derive this formula from first principles** if asked (a common 3-mark KTU question).

### 3.2 Worked Example — Full Numerical Evaluation

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
In an Izod impact test on a mild steel specimen, the following data are recorded:
- Weight of pendulum, $W = 25 \text{ N}$
- Distance from pivot to center of gravity of pendulum, $R = 0.8 \text{ m}$
- Initial angle of pendulum, $\alpha = 150° = \dfrac{5\pi}{6} \text{ rad}$
- Overswing angle after fracture, $\beta = 90° = \dfrac{\pi}{2} \text{ rad}$
- Specimen cross-section: $10 \text{ mm} \times 10 \text{ mm}$ with a 2 mm deep 45° V-notch

**Find:**
(a) The energy absorbed in fracturing the specimen.
(b) The Izod impact strength of the material.

---

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Energy absorbed, $U$:**

$$
U = W R (\cos\beta - \cos\alpha)
$$

Substitute values (angles in degrees — be careful, the cosine function is the same in degree or radian mode as long as your calculator is set correctly):

$$
U = (25)(0.8)\left[\cos(90°) - \cos(150°)\right]
$$

$$
U = 20 \left[0 - (-0.8660)\right]
$$

$$
U = 20 \times 0.8660
$$

$$
\boxed{U = 17.32 \text{ J}}
$$

**Part (b) — Izod Impact Strength, $IS$:**

The cross-sectional area at the notch root is computed using the **residual depth** $d_n$ (depth of material *remaining* below the notch):

$$
d_n = 10 \text{ mm} - 2 \text{ mm} = 8 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
A_{notch} = b \times d_n = 10 \text{ mm} \times 8 \text{ mm} = 80 \text{ mm}^2 = 80 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2
$$

Now the impact strength:

$$
IS = \frac{U}{A_{notch}} = \frac{17.32 \text{ J}}{80 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2}
$$

$$
IS = 216{,}500 \text{ J/m}^2 = 2.165 \times 10^{5} \text{ J/m}^2
$$

Or equivalently:

$$
IS = \frac{17.32 \text{ J}}{80 \text{ mm}^2} = 0.2165 \text{ J/mm}^2 = 216.5 \text{ kJ/m}^2
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unit Convention Pitfall:** KTU accepts **both J/m² and J/mm²** in the answer, but you **must** show unit conversion. A common 1-mark loss in valuation is writing "$17.32 / 80 = 0.2165$" *without units* — this is meaningless physically and will be penalized.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Interpretation of Results

| Observed Energy $U$ (J) | Material Behavior | Likely Material Class |
|---|---|---|
| < 5 | Very brittle; little plastic deformation | Cast iron, ceramics, glass, hardened tool steel |
| 5 – 50 | Moderately tough; some plastic deformation | Medium-carbon steel, brass, aluminum alloys |
| 50 – 200 | Tough; significant plastic deformation | Mild steel, low-alloy structural steels |
| > 200 | Very tough; high plastic deformation | Ductile copper, aluminum, austenitic stainless steel |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Viva Question (Frequently Asked):**
> *"If the same specimen gives a low Izod value at −40 °C and a high value at +25 °C, what does that indicate?"*
> **Answer:** The material exhibits a **Ductile-to-Brittle Transition Temperature (DBTT)**. The test should be repeated at several intermediate temperatures to plot the transition curve. This is critical for materials used in low-temperature service.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Izod Impact Test Machine — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional flow** of the Izod impact testing machine, including the specimen clamping geometry and the energy-transfer sequence. (Direct physical drawings of the pendulum-striker geometry are not native to Mermaid, so this block diagram represents the equivalent functional architecture.)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Base Frame and Pillars] --> B[Pivot Bearing Assembly at Top]
    B --> C[Pendulum Hammer with Striker Edge]
    C --> D[Calibrated Dial Gauge for Energy Readout]
    C --> E[Specimen Clamping Vice - Vertical Cantilever]
    E --> F[Notched Specimen - Notch Facing Striker]
    G[Cocking Lever and Latch Mechanism] --> C
    H[Safety Guard and Brake] --> C
    F --> I[Crack Initiation at Notch Root]
    I --> J[Crack Propagation Through Cross Section]
    J --> K[Fracture - Broken Halves]
    K --> L[Energy Absorbed U Read from Dial]
    M[Striker Velocity v at Impact] --> L
    N[Specimen Cross Section at Notch] --> O[Izod Impact Strength IS]
    L --> O
```

### 4.2 Specimen Geometry and Loading — Schematic Block View

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph "Izod Specimen Geometry (ASTM E23 Standard)"
        SA[Specimen Length: 75 mm]
        SB[Cross Section: 10 mm x 10 mm]
        SC[45 Degree V Notch - 2 mm Deep]
        SD[Notch Root Radius: 0.25 mm]
        SE[Clamping Length: 25 mm from Notch Side]
    end
    subgraph "Loading Configuration"
        LA[Fixity: Cantilever - Lower End Clamped]
        LB[Striker: Single Blow from Pendulum]
        LC[Notch Side: Faces the Striker - Tension Side]
        LD[Striker Edge Radius: 8 mm]
    end
    subgraph "Measured Quantities"
        MA[Energy Absorbed U in Joules]
        MB[Initial Angle alpha]
        MC[Overswing Angle beta]
        MD[Cross Section at Notch An in mm squared]
    end
    SA --> MA
    SB --> MD
    SC --> MA
    LA --> LC
    LB --> LA
    MA --> ME[Impact Strength IS = U divided by An]
    MD --> ME
```

### 4.3 Step-by-Step Experimental Procedure — Block Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Step 1: Inspect Specimen - Measure Dimensions b and d] --> S2[Step 2: Measure Notch Depth and Confirm Geometry]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Clamp Specimen Vertically in Vice with Notch Facing Striker]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Set Dial Gauge to Zero]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Release Pendulum from Latched Position]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Pendulum Strikes Specimen and Fractures It]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Pendulum Overswings to Final Angle beta]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Read Absorbed Energy U from Dial in Joules]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9: Calculate Cross Section at Notch An]
    S9 --> S10[Step 10: Compute Izod Impact Strength IS = U divided by An]
    S10 --> S11[Step 11: Record Observations and Repeat for 3 Specimens for Mean Value]
```

### 4.4 Functional State Transition of the Pendulum

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[State 1: Cocked at Angle alpha - Max Potential Energy] -->|Release| P2[State 2: At Bottom - Max Kinetic Energy - About to Strike]
    P2 -->|Impact| P3[State 3: Fracturing Specimen - Energy Transfer]
    P3 -->|Overswing| P4[State 4: Reaches Angle beta - Reduced Potential Energy]
    P4 -->|Difference in PE| P5[Energy Absorbed U equals W times R times cos beta minus cos alpha]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Aid:** The four-state pendulum transition (P1 → P2 → P3 → P4) is the conceptual heart of the experiment. The energy **lost** in transition P2 → P3 is the energy **gained by the specimen** as fracture work. This is the same principle as a ballistic pendulum used to measure bullet velocity.
