## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Question Paper]**
**Define the term "impact strength" of a material. Why is the impact test preferred over a simple tensile test for evaluating materials used in low-temperature service?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Impact strength is defined as the *energy absorbed per unit cross-sectional area at the notch* when a notched specimen is fractured by a single blow of a pendulum hammer under specified conditions of specimen geometry, notch geometry, and striker velocity.

**Low-temperature service reasoning (2 marks):** Under static tensile loading, many materials (notably BCC metals like ferritic steels) appear ductile at room temperature but undergo a **ductile-to-brittle transition (DBTT)** at lower temperatures, where the fracture mode changes from ductile (high energy absorption) to cleavage (low energy absorption). A tensile test at room temperature cannot detect this transition, whereas the impact test — when performed at sub-zero temperatures — *does* show a dramatic drop in absorbed energy, allowing engineers to identify the **nil-ductility transition (NDT) temperature** and design accordingly. This is why the ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code mandates Charpy/Izod testing at the *minimum design temperature* for pressure-vessel steels.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Differentiate between the Izod and Charpy impact tests in any three aspects.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per aspect):**

| Aspect | Izod Test | Charpy Test |
|---|---|---|
| **Specimen orientation** | Held **vertically** as a cantilever (clamped at one end) | Held **horizontally** as a **simply-supported beam** (supported at both ends) |
| **Notch position** | Notch is on the **same side as the striker** (tension side, facing the hammer) | Notch is on the **opposite side from the striker** (tension side, away from the hammer) |
| **Striking direction** | Pendulum strikes the **free (top) end** of the specimen | Pendulum strikes the **mid-span** of the specimen (directly behind the notch) |
| **Result units** | Often reported in J/m (ASTM) or J/m² (ISO) | Often reported in J (absorbed energy) |
| **Common standards** | ASTM D256, ISO 180 | ASTM E23, ISO 148 |

---

### 5.2 PART B — ESE Module Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each module in the ESE (End Semester Exam) offers an internal choice of two 14-mark questions. The student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each question is split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *With a neat sketch, describe the Izod impact testing machine. Explain the procedure for conducting the test and the principle of energy measurement.*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *In an Izod test, the initial and final (overswing) angles of a 20 N pendulum of effective length 0.85 m are 140° and 85° respectively. The specimen cross-section is 10 mm × 8 mm at the notch. Calculate (i) the energy absorbed in breaking the specimen, and (ii) the Izod impact strength in J/m².*

**MODEL SOLUTION:**

**(a) [7 marks] — Description, Sketch & Procedure:**

**Description of the machine (3 marks):**
The Izod impact testing machine consists of:
- A **heavy base** with two vertical pillars supporting a top cross-member.
- A **pendulum** pivoted on bearings at the top cross-member, free to swing in a vertical plane. The pendulum carries a **striking edge** (radius 8 mm) at its lower end.
- A **graduated dial gauge** mounted concentrically with the pivot, with a pointer driven by a friction-drive from the pendulum. The dial is graduated directly in Joules of absorbed energy.
- A **specimen vice** mounted rigidly on the base, positioned so that the notched specimen (clamped vertically) lies directly in the path of the pendulum striker.
- A **cocking handle** to lift the pendulum to a precise initial angle, latched in position by a spring-loaded catch.
- A **safety guard** to protect the operator from flying fragments.
- A **brake mechanism** to bring the pendulum to rest after the test.

**Procedure (2 marks):**
1. Measure the specimen dimensions at the notch (width $b$ and residual depth $d_n$) using a vernier caliper.
2. Clamp the specimen vertically in the vice with the notch facing the striker. Ensure firm clamping up to the specified clamping depth (typically 25 mm from the notch side).
3. Set the dial gauge pointer to zero.
4. Cock the pendulum to the initial position (angle $\alpha$).
5. Release the catch — the pendulum swings, strikes the specimen, and fractures it.
6. Record the energy $U$ directly from the dial gauge reading.
7. Repeat the test for at least 3 specimens to obtain a mean value.

**Principle of energy measurement (2 marks):**
The energy absorbed by the specimen equals the loss in potential energy of the pendulum between its initial position (angle $\alpha$) and its final overswing position (angle $\beta$):

$$
U = WR(\cos\beta - \cos\alpha)
$$

The dial is pre-calibrated such that the pointer deflection directly indicates the value of $U$ in Joules.

---

**(b) [7 marks] — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $W = 20 \text{ N}$
- $R = 0.85 \text{ m}$
- $\alpha = 140°$, so $\cos(140°) = -0.7660$
- $\beta = 85°$, so $\cos(85°) = 0.0872$
- $b = 10 \text{ mm}$, $d_n = 8 \text{ mm}$ (residual depth at notch)

**Part (i) — Energy absorbed:**

$$
U = WR(\cos\beta - \cos\alpha)
$$

$$
U = (20)(0.85)\left[(0.0872) - (-0.7660)\right]
$$

$$
U = 17.0 \times (0.0872 + 0.7660)
$$

$$
U = 17.0 \times 0.8532
$$

$$
\boxed{U = 14.504 \text{ J}}
$$

**Part (ii) — Izod Impact Strength:**

Cross-sectional area at the notch:

$$
A_{notch} = b \times d_n = 10 \times 8 = 80 \text{ mm}^2 = 80 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2
$$

Impact strength:

$$
IS = \frac{U}{A_{notch}} = \frac{14.504}{80 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
\boxed{IS = 1.813 \times 10^{5} \text{ J/m}^2 = 181.3 \text{ kJ/m}^2}
$$

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper – Alternative]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain the significance of the notch in an impact test specimen. What factors affect the impact strength of a material?*

**(b) [7 Marks]** *A pendulum of weight 30 N and center of gravity at 0.9 m from the pivot is released from 160° in an Izod test. After breaking the specimen, the pendulum overswings to 95°. If the specimen cross-section at the notch is 10 mm × 9 mm, determine the impact strength of the material in J/m² and J/mm².*

**MODEL SOLUTION:**

**(a) [7 marks] — Significance of notch & affecting factors:**

**Significance of the notch (4 marks):**
1. **Simulates real engineering defects:** Most engineering structures contain geometric discontinuities (sharp corners, keyways, threads, holes) that act as **stress concentrators**. The notch in the test specimen simulates these real-life stress raisers.
2. **Standardizes the fracture location:** Without a notch, the fracture would initiate at an unpredictable weak point in the material, making the test non-reproducible. The notch forces the crack to start at a known location, ensuring consistent results.
3. **Promotes brittle fracture:** The notch creates a **triaxial state of stress** at its root, which suppresses plastic deformation and favors brittle (cleavage) fracture. This makes the test *more sensitive* to the brittle-fracture tendency of the material — the very property we want to measure.
4. **Provides a quantitative measure of notch sensitivity:** By comparing impact values of notched vs. unnotched specimens, one can determine the material's **notch sensitivity ratio**, an important design parameter.

**Factors affecting impact strength (3 marks):**
1. **Temperature** — Lowering temperature generally *decreases* impact strength (especially in BCC metals with a sharp DBTT).
2. **Notch geometry** — Sharper, deeper notches reduce the measured impact strength.
3. **Material composition & microstructure** — Carbon content, grain size, alloying elements, heat treatment, and presence of inclusions all affect toughness.
4. **Strain rate** — Higher strain rates reduce impact strength (materials become more brittle).
5. **Specimen size** — Larger specimens may give lower values (size effect on fracture).

---

**(b) [7 marks] — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $W = 30 \text{ N}$
- $R = 0.9 \text{ m}$
- $\alpha = 160°$, so $\cos(160°) = -0.9397$
- $\beta = 95°$, so $\cos(95°) = -0.0872$
- $b = 10 \text{ mm}$, $d_n = 9 \text{ mm}$

**Step 1 — Energy absorbed:**

$$
U = WR(\cos\beta - \cos\alpha)
$$

$$
U = (30)(0.9)\left[(-0.0872) - (-0.9397)\right]
$$

$$
U = 27 \times 0.8525
$$

$$
\boxed{U = 23.018 \text{ J}}
$$

**Step 2 — Cross-sectional area at notch:**

$$
A_{notch} = 10 \times 9 = 90 \text{ mm}^2 = 90 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Impact strength in J/m²:**

$$
IS = \frac{23.018}{90 \times 10^{-6}} = 2.5576 \times 10^{5} \text{ J/m}^2
$$

$$
\boxed{IS = 2.558 \times 10^{5} \text{ J/m}^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Impact strength in J/mm²:**

$$
IS = \frac{23.018}{90} = 0.2558 \text{ J/mm}^2
$$

$$
\boxed{IS = 0.2558 \text{ J/mm}^2}
$$

**Cross-check:** $0.2558 \text{ J/mm}^2 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^2/\text{m}^2 = 2.558 \times 10^5 \text{ J/m}^2$ ✓

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in the KTU Board Exam:**
>
> 1. **Sign error in cosine values** — Students often forget that $\cos(150°) = -0.866$ and write it as $+0.866$, leading to a *negative* absorbed energy. Always draw the angle on paper and verify the sign.
> 2. **Confusing $R$ with the pendulum arm length** — $R$ is the distance from the pivot to the **center of gravity** of the pendulum (NOT to the striking point). The dial gauge is calibrated using this $R$.
> 3. **Using full specimen area instead of notch area** — The impact strength formula uses the *reduced* area at the notch root, not the original $10 \times 10$ mm². Marks lost: typically 1 mark per part-question.
> 4. **Unit inconsistency** — Mixing mm and m without $10^{-6}$ or $10^{-3}$ conversion. Always show the unit-conversion step explicitly.
> 5. **Confusing Izod and Charpy clamping** — In a viva or diagram question, drawing a horizontal specimen for Izod (it should be vertical!) costs 2–3 marks.
> 6. **Forgetting to convert degrees to a consistent mode** on the calculator. Use **degree mode** unless explicitly told radians.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the Izod Impact Test (PCAGL308 – Experiment 2):**
>
> - **What is being measured:** Energy absorbed (in Joules) in fracturing a notched specimen struck by a pendulum — a measure of *dynamic toughness*.
> - **Specimen type:** Notched (45° V-notch, 2 mm deep), held as a **vertical cantilever**, notch facing the striker.
> - **Key formula (must memorize):** $U = WR(\cos\beta - \cos\alpha)$ — energy absorbed by the specimen.
> - **Key formula (must memorize):** $IS = \dfrac{U}{A_{notch}}$, where $A_{notch} = b \times (d - \text{notch depth})$.
> - **Units of $U$:** Joules (J). Units of $IS$:** J/m² (SI) or J/mm² (CGS).
> - **Izod vs Charpy (viva favorite):** Vertical cantilever + notch on striker side vs Horizontal simply-supported + notch opposite to striker.
> - **DBTT:** BCC metals (e.g., mild steel) show a sharp ductile-to-brittle transition with temperature; the impact test is used to detect this.
> - **Notch sensitivity:** A material with a large drop from unnotched to notched impact value has *high notch sensitivity*.
> - **Striker velocity range:** 3 – 6 m/s (governed by the initial angle and arm length via $v = \sqrt{2gR(1-\cos\alpha)}$).
> - **Repeatability:** Always test at least **3 specimens** and report the *mean* (and ideally the *range*) of absorbed energy.
> - **Calibration:** The dial pointer is *factory-calibrated* to read $U$ directly in Joules — no need to compute $\alpha$ and $\beta$ in routine lab testing, but the derivation is mandatory in the ESE.
> - **Safety:** Always close the safety guard before releasing the pendulum; broken fragments fly at high velocity.
> - **Sources of error:** Specimen mis-clamping (notch in wrong direction), friction in the pivot bearings, dial-gauge zeroing error, and specimen-to-specimen variation in notch geometry.

---

**End of Module Experiment 2 Notes — Izod Impact Test**
*Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme — PCAGL308 Strength of Materials*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Impact Load on a Specimen by Charpy's Impact Test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGL308 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Impact Load on a Specimen by Charpy's Impact Test

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Charpy's Impact Test** is a standardized high-strain-rate destructive testing procedure used to determine the **amount of energy absorbed by a notched specimen during fracture** under a sudden dynamic (impact) load applied by a swinging pendulum. It is governed by **IS 1757**, **ASTM E23**, **EN 10045-1**, and **ISO 148-1** standards.

The test quantifies a material's **toughness** (ability to absorb energy and plastically deform before fracturing) under impact conditions, simulating sudden shock or impact loading encountered in service. The specimen, held as a simply supported beam, is fractured by a single overload from a swinging pendulum, and the **difference in potential energy** of the pendulum before and after impact gives the **impact energy absorbed** (in Joules, J).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Notched Chocolate Bar"**

Imagine you have two identical chocolate bars:
- **Bar A:** Smooth and uniform along its length.
- **Bar B:** Has a small **V-shaped notch** cut into its middle.

If you try to break both bars by **snapping them quickly with your hands**, you will find that **Bar B breaks much more easily** with far less effort than Bar A. The notch acts as a **stress concentrator** that drastically reduces the energy required for fracture.

Now imagine you want to **measure exactly how much energy** is saved when the bar fractures at the notch. You build a swinging hammer (pendulum) that releases from a known height, smashes the bar, and swings up to a lesser height on the other side. The **difference in heights, multiplied by the weight of the hammer, equals the energy absorbed by the bar during fracture**. That is precisely the Charpy Impact Test!

**Real-World Engineering Relevance:**
- Selecting steels for **Arctic pipelines** and **ship hulls** that must resist brittle fracture in cold seas.
- Quality control of **railway tracks, bridges, and pressure vessels**.
- Comparing heat-treated alloys in **automotive and aerospace crashworthiness** design.

## 1.3 Specimen and Notch Standards

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Specimen Dimensions (IS 1757 / ASTM E23):**
> - Length = **55 mm**
> - Width = **10 mm**
> - Height = **10 mm**
> - Notch position: **Mid-length**
> - Notch types: **V-Notch** (2 mm deep, 45° angle, 0.25 mm root radius), **U-Notch** (5 mm deep, 1 mm root radius), or **Keyhole Notch**.

The **V-notch** is the most commonly used because it produces a sharper stress concentration and more severe triaxial stress state at the notch root.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Standard pendulum capacities:** **300 J** and **150 J** (Charpy); pendulum weight varies accordingly.
> - **Striker velocity at impact:** typically **5.0 to 5.5 m/s** (for a 300 J machine).
> - **Span between specimen supports:** **40 mm**.
> - **Gravitational acceleration:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

## 1.5 Charpy vs Izod — A Quick Comparison

> [!NOTE]
> **Charpy Test:** Specimen is mounted as a **simply supported beam (horizontal)** and struck *behind* the notch.
> **Izod Test:** Specimen is mounted as a **cantilever (vertical)** and struck *on the notched side*.
> Both use the same pendulum principle; the difference lies in specimen orientation and clamping.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Principle of Operation

The Charpy impact testing machine is essentially a **precision pendulum** designed to deliver a known amount of kinetic energy to a specimen at the bottom of its swing.

**Working Logic — Step by Step:**

1. The pendulum is **raised (latched)** to a known initial angle $\alpha$ from the vertical, gaining **gravitational potential energy**.
2. The pendulum is **released** from the latch and begins to swing downward under gravity.
3. At the **lowest point of its arc**, the pendulum's striker edge strikes the **notched side of the specimen** (positioned horizontally on anvil supports).
4. The specimen **fractures**; part of the pendulum's kinetic energy is **absorbed by the specimen** during plastic deformation and crack propagation.
5. The pendulum **continues to swing upward** on the opposite side, rising to a **lower final angle $\beta$** (since energy was lost to fracture).
6. A **pointer on a graduated dial** is carried by the pendulum to its highest point and remains there, indicating the **final (swing) angle $\beta$**.
7. The **energy absorbed** is calculated from the difference in potential energy of the pendulum before and after the strike.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The whole event (swing down → strike → swing up) occurs in approximately **0.6 to 1.5 seconds**, ensuring a **high strain rate** of roughly **10³ s⁻¹**, which is the essence of an "impact" test.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Description |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Initial Height | $h_1 = R(1 - \cos\alpha)$ | m | Height of pendulum above lowest point before release |
| 2 | Final Height | $h_2 = R(1 - \cos\beta)$ | m | Height of pendulum above lowest point after fracture |
| 3 | Initial PE | $E_1 = W \cdot h_1 = WR(1 - \cos\alpha)$ | J (N·m) | Potential energy at start |
| 4 | Final PE | $E_2 = W \cdot h_2 = WR(1 - \cos\beta)$ | J (N·m) | Potential energy after fracture |
| 5 | **Energy Absorbed** | $\boxed{E = WR(\cos\beta - \cos\alpha)}$ | **J** | **Impact energy / Notch toughness** |
| 6 | Impact Strength | $I_s = \dfrac{E}{A_n}$ | J/mm² or J/m² | Energy per unit area at notch |
| 7 | Striker Velocity | $v = \sqrt{2gR(1 - \cos\alpha)}$ | m/s | Velocity at impact point |
| 8 | Kinetic Energy at Impact | $KE = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^2 = WR(1 - \cos\alpha)$ | J | Equals initial PE (energy conservation) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Sign Convention:** The initial angle $\alpha$ is always **greater than** the final angle $\beta$ (i.e., $\alpha > \beta$). Therefore, $\cos\beta > \cos\alpha$, ensuring $E > 0$. If the specimen absorbs **zero** energy (e.g., no specimen mounted), then $\beta = \alpha$, and the formula correctly returns $E = 0$.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
|-------|-------------|
| **Pressure Vessel Design** | Mandatory Charpy testing per ASME Sec. VIII to prevent catastrophic brittle failure. |
| **Shipbuilding** | Hull steel must meet Charpy impact criteria at sub-zero temperatures to avoid brittle fracture in icy seas. |
| **Pipeline Engineering** | API 5L linepipe steels require Charpy V-notch (CVN) energy minimums for safe gas/oil transport. |
| **Aerospace** | Turbine alloys and landing-gear steels are impact-tested for crashworthiness. |
| **Foundry Quality Control** | Verifies homogeneity of castings and effectiveness of heat treatments. |

## 2.4 Physical Significance of "Impact Load"

An **impact load** is a load applied **suddenly** with a very high strain rate, such that the body's inertia cannot be neglected. Unlike static loads (where the entire body shares the load uniformly), impact loads create **localized stress waves** propagating through the material. Brittle materials, which cannot deform plastically, fail catastrophically under such loads — which is precisely why Charpy testing is so critical for safety-sensitive applications.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Impact Energy Absorbed Formula

### Setup

Consider a pendulum of:
- **Weight** = $W$ (in Newtons)
- **Effective length** (pivot to centre of gravity) = $R$ (in metres)
- **Initial angle from vertical** = $\alpha$
- **Final (swing) angle from vertical** = $\beta$ (where $\beta < \alpha$)

### Step 1 — Geometry of Initial Position

When the pendulum is latched at angle $\alpha$, its centre of gravity is raised above the lowest (vertical) position by a vertical height $h_1$. From the geometry of a pendulum:

$$h_1 = R - R\cos\alpha$$

$$\boxed{h_1 = R(1 - \cos\alpha)}$$

### Step 2 — Geometry of Final Position

After striking and fracturing the specimen, the pendulum swings up to angle $\beta$. Its centre of gravity is now raised above the lowest position by:

$$\boxed{h_2 = R(1 - \cos\beta)}$$

### Step 3 — Initial Potential Energy

At the latched position, the pendulum possesses gravitational potential energy (with the lowest swing point as the datum):

$$E_1 = W \cdot h_1 = W \cdot R(1 - \cos\alpha)$$

$$E_1 = WR(1 - \cos\alpha)$$

### Step 4 — Final Potential Energy

After the swing-through, the pendulum rises to height $h_2$, retaining only:

$$E_2 = W \cdot h_2 = W \cdot R(1 - \cos\beta)$$

$$E_2 = WR(1 - \cos\beta)$$

### Step 5 — Energy Balance (Conservation of Energy)

By the principle of **conservation of energy**, the **loss in potential energy** of the pendulum equals the **energy absorbed by the specimen** during fracture (assuming negligible air drag and friction, which are accounted for by the friction brake calibration).

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{absorbed}} &= E_1 - E_2 \\
&= WR(1 - \cos\alpha) - WR(1 - \cos\beta) \\
&= WR\bigl[(1 - \cos\alpha) - (1 - \cos\beta)\bigr] \\
&= WR\bigl[1 - \cos\alpha - 1 + \cos\beta\bigr] \\
&= WR(\cos\beta - \cos\alpha)
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{E_{\text{absorbed}} = WR(\cos\beta - \cos\alpha)}$$

### Step 6 — Derivation of Impact Strength

**Impact strength** (also called **notch toughness** or **impact value**) is defined as the energy absorbed per unit cross-sectional area **at the notch root**:

$$\boxed{I_s = \frac{E_{\text{absorbed}}}{A_n} = \frac{WR(\cos\beta - \cos\alpha)}{A_n}}$$

Where:
- $A_n = b \times (h - d)$ for V-notch
- $b$ = specimen width (mm)
- $h$ = specimen height (mm)
- $d$ = notch depth (mm)

### Step 7 — Velocity of Striker at Impact (Auxiliary Derivation)

Using energy conservation between initial PE and KE at the bottom of the swing:

$$WR(1 - \cos\alpha) = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^2$$

Since $W = mg$, this simplifies to:

$$gR(1 - \cos\alpha) = \tfrac{1}{2}v^2$$

$$\boxed{v = \sqrt{2gR(1 - \cos\alpha)}}$$

For a typical 300 J Charpy machine with $R \approx 0.8$ m and $\alpha = 90°$:

$$v = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.8 \times (1 - 0)} \approx 3.96 \text{ m/s}$$

(Standard machines compensate for air drag to deliver nominal 5 m/s.)

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Typical KTU Problem

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A Charpy impact test is conducted on a mild steel specimen. The pendulum weighs **300 N** and has an effective length of **0.80 m**. It is released from an initial angle of **90°** and, after fracturing the specimen, swings to a final angle of **40°**. If the cross-sectional area at the notch is **80 mm²**, calculate:
> (a) The energy absorbed by the specimen.
> (b) The impact strength of the material.

### Given Data

- $W = 300$ N
- $R = 0.80$ m
- $\alpha = 90°$, so $\cos\alpha = 0$
- $\beta = 40°$, so $\cos\beta = 0.7660$
- $A_n = 80 \text{ mm}^2$

### Part (a): Energy Absorbed

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= WR(\cos\beta - \cos\alpha) \\
&= 300 \times 0.80 \times (\cos 40° - \cos 90°) \\
&= 240 \times (0.7660 - 0) \\
&= 240 \times 0.7660 \\
&= 183.85 \text{ J}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{E_{\text{absorbed}} = 183.85 \text{ J}}$$

### Part (b): Impact Strength

$$\begin{aligned}
I_s &= \frac{E}{A_n} = \frac{183.85 \text{ J}}{80 \text{ mm}^2} \\
&= 2.298 \text{ J/mm}^2 \\
&= 2.298 \times 10^6 \text{ J/m}^2 \\
&= 2.298 \text{ MJ/m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

Alternatively, converting to the more common laboratory unit:

$$I_s = \frac{183.85}{0.80} = 229.8 \text{ J/cm}^2$$

$$\boxed{I_s \approx 2.30 \text{ J/mm}^2 = 230 \text{ J/cm}^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** A typical mild steel exhibits Charpy impact strength in the range **150–350 J/cm²** at room temperature. Our result of **~230 J/cm²** is consistent with a ductile mild-steel specimen. Brittle cast iron or hardened tool steel would give values of **< 30 J/cm²**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common Exam Scenarios

| Scenario | What to Compute | Key Formula |
|----------|----------------|-------------|
| Energy absorbed only | $E$ | $WR(\cos\beta - \cos\alpha)$ |
| Impact strength given | $I_s$ | $E / A_n$ |
| Find final angle $\beta$ given $E$ | Solve for $\cos\beta$ | $\cos\beta = \dfrac{E}{WR} + \cos\alpha$ |
| Find pendulum weight given $E$ | Solve for $W$ | $W = \dfrac{E}{R(\cos\beta - \cos\alpha)}$ |
| Striker velocity | $v$ | $\sqrt{2gR(1-\cos\alpha)}$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Experimental Procedure

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Testing Machine Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    blkFrame[HEAVY CAST IRON BASE FRAME PROVIDES RIGIDITY]
    blkPivot[TWO PIVOT BEARINGS AT TOP OF FRAME]

```

## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic — Pendulum Swing Geometry

```mermaid

    schemPivot((PIVOT POINT))

    schemPivot --- schemInit
    schemPivot --- schemStrike
    schemPivot --- schemFinal
    
    schemInit -->|FALL UNDER GRAVITY| schemStrike
```
