
## 4.4 Component Identification Table

| Component | Material | Function |
|-----------|----------|----------|
| Frame | Cast Iron | Provides rigidity; resists vibration during fracture |
| Pendulum | Steel | Stores PE; delivers KE at impact |
| Striker | Hardened Tool Steel | Single-edge wedge; 8 mm radius; 30°–32° tip angle |
| Anvils | Hardened Steel | Support specimen at 40 mm span |
| Dial Scale | Aluminium/Brass | Direct-reading in Joules (compensated for friction) |
| Pointer | Mild Steel | Carried to highest point of swing |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Define the term "Impact Toughness" as determined by Charpy's impact test. Name the relevant Indian Standard for the test.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Impact toughness** is defined as the **amount of energy absorbed by a notched specimen** when fractured under a sudden impact load delivered by a swinging pendulum. It is a measure of the material's ability to resist **brittle fracture under dynamic loading** and combines the effects of strength and ductility at high strain rates.
>
> The relevant Indian Standard is **IS 1757** (equivalent to ASTM E23 / ISO 148-1).
>
> Mathematically:
> $$\text{Impact Toughness} = \frac{E}{A_n} = \frac{WR(\cos\beta - \cos\alpha)}{A_n}$$
>
> Units are typically **Joules (J)** for energy absorbed or **J/cm²** for impact strength.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Why is a notch provided on the Charpy impact test specimen? Mention any two types of notches used.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Purpose of the Notch:**
>
> 1. To **create a stress concentration** at the notch root, ensuring that fracture initiates at a predictable location.
> 2. To **simulate the worst-case service condition** where components may contain sharp geometric discontinuities (cracks, grooves, keyways) that act as crack-initiation sites.
> 3. To **standardize the test** so that results from different laboratories can be compared meaningfully.
> 4. To **distinguish between ductile and brittle behaviour** of materials more clearly (brittle materials fail at the notch with little energy; ductile materials deform plastically around it).
>
> **Types of Notches Used:**
> - **V-Notch:** 2 mm deep, 45° angle, 0.25 mm root radius (most severe; standard).
> - **U-Notch:** 5 mm deep, 1 mm root radius (less severe).
> - **Keyhole Notch:** Used for castings and forgings (rare).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A** — Theory + Numerical

#### **(a) [7 Marks] Explain with a neat sketch the principle, construction, and testing procedure of a Charpy impact testing machine. Mention the relevant Indian Standard.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Principle:**
The Charpy impact test is based on the principle of **conservation of energy**. A pendulum of known weight is raised to a known height, released to strike a notched specimen at the bottom of its swing, and the **difference in potential energy** of the pendulum before and after the strike is taken as the **energy absorbed** by the specimen during fracture.

**Construction (Key Components):**
1. **Heavy cast iron frame** — provides rigidity and absorbs vibrations.
2. **Pendulum** — long steel arm with a hardened striker edge at its tip, pivoted at the top.
3. **Anvil supports** — two hardened supports, spaced **40 mm** apart, that hold the specimen horizontally.
4. **Graduated dial** — calibrated directly in Joules, with a **pointer** carried by the pendulum.
5. **Lifting handle** — used to raise the pendulum to the initial angle $\alpha$.
6. **Friction brake** — stops the pendulum after one swing to prevent damage.

**Testing Procedure:**
1. **Prepare** the standard specimen (55 mm × 10 mm × 10 mm) with a V-notch at mid-length.
2. **Measure** specimen dimensions accurately using a Vernier caliper.
3. **Mount** the specimen horizontally on the anvil supports with the notch facing **away** from the striker (i.e., striker hits the side opposite the notch).
4. **Raise** the pendulum to the latched initial angle (typically 90° or 120°) and **record** $\alpha$.
5. **Release** the pendulum smoothly using the release lever.
6. The pendulum **strikes** the specimen; the specimen **fractures** at the notch.
7. The pendulum continues to swing upward to a lower angle $\beta$; **record** $\beta$ from the dial pointer.
8. **Calculate** absorbed energy using:
   $$E = WR(\cos\beta - \cos\alpha)$$
9. **Compute** impact strength:
   $$I_s = \dfrac{E}{A_n}$$
10. **Repeat** the test with three identical specimens and take the **average** to improve reliability.

**Indian Standard:** **IS 1757: 2002** (Methods of Izod and Charpy Impact Testing of Metals).

*(A neat labelled sketch should accompany this answer showing: pendulum, striker, anvils, specimen, dial, pointer, and the angles $\alpha$ and $\beta$.)*

---

#### **(b) [7 Marks] In a Charpy test, the pendulum weighs 250 N with effective length 0.85 m. It is released from 90° and after breaking the specimen it swings to 35°. If the cross-sectional area at the notch is 64 mm², determine (i) Energy absorbed (ii) Impact strength.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]`

**Given Data:**
- $W = 250$ N
- $R = 0.85$ m
- $\alpha = 90° \Rightarrow \cos\alpha = 0$
- $\beta = 35° \Rightarrow \cos\beta = 0.8192$
- $A_n = 64$ mm²

**Part (i): Energy Absorbed**

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= WR(\cos\beta - \cos\alpha) \\
&= 250 \times 0.85 \times (0.8192 - 0) \\
&= 212.5 \times 0.8192 \\
&= 174.08 \text{ J}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{E = 174.08 \text{ J} \approx 174.1 \text{ J}}$$

**Part (ii): Impact Strength**

$$\begin{aligned}
I_s &= \frac{E}{A_n} = \frac{174.08 \text{ J}}{64 \text{ mm}^2} \\
&= 2.72 \text{ J/mm}^2 \\
&= 2.72 \times 10^6 \text{ J/m}^2 \\
&= 272 \text{ J/cm}^2
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{I_s = 2.72 \text{ J/mm}^2 = 272 \text{ J/cm}^2}$$

**Conclusion:** A mild-steel-grade material exhibiting ~272 J/cm² is **moderately ductile**.

---

### **Question B** — Theory + Numerical (Alternative Choice)

#### **(a) [7 Marks] Explain the concept of Ductile-to-Brittle Transition Temperature (DBTT). Describe with a sketch how the Charpy test is used to determine DBTT.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Concept of DBTT:**

Many metals, especially **BCC metals** like mild steel and other ferritic steels, exhibit a characteristic change in fracture behaviour with temperature:
- At **high temperatures**, they fracture in a **ductile** manner — absorbing significant energy with visible plastic deformation.
- At **low temperatures**, they fracture in a **brittle** manner — absorbing very little energy with little plastic deformation.

The temperature at which this transition occurs (typically defined as the temperature at which the average impact energy equals **20 J** for standard V-notch, or at which the fracture surface is 50% fibrous / 50% crystalline) is called the **Ductile-to-Brittle Transition Temperature (DBTT)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance:** DBTT is critical for materials used in **cold environments** (Arctic pipelines, ship hulls, refrigerated vessels, aerospace structures at altitude). The operating temperature must always be **above the DBTT** with a safety margin.

**Determination of DBTT using Charpy Test:**

A series of identical Charpy specimens (typically 6–10) are tested at different temperatures:
- Some are heated in a bath (e.g., 100°C, 50°C, 25°C).
- Some are cooled in a bath (e.g., 0°C, −20°C, −40°C, −60°C).
- Each specimen is rapidly transferred and broken within 5 seconds.

The absorbed energy $E$ is plotted versus test temperature $T$:

```
   E (J) │
         │           ●●●●●  (Upper Shelf - Ductile Plateau)
   300 ──│       ●●●
         │      ●
   200 ──│     ●
         │    ●
   100 ──│   ●  ← Transition Region
         │  ●
    20 ──│─●─────── DBTT (Defined at 20 J Criterion)
         │ ●
     0 ──│●●●●●●●●  (Lower Shelf - Brittle Plateau)
         └──────────────────────────── T (°C)
          -60  -40  -20   0   25   50   100
```

The **S-shaped curve** clearly shows:
- **Upper Shelf Energy (USE):** ductile plateau (~250–300 J for mild steel).
- **Lower Shelf Energy (LSE):** brittle plateau (~5–20 J).
- **DBTT:** the midpoint of the transition region.

A **low DBTT** indicates the material remains tough at low temperatures (e.g., nickel-alloyed steels); a **high DBTT** limits the material to warm-service applications.

---

#### **(b) [7 Marks] In a Charpy test, the pendulum has weight 320 N and length 1.0 m. It is released from 120° and after fracture the pendulum swings up to 50°. The specimen cross-section at the notch is 8 mm × 8 mm. Compute the impact energy and the impact strength.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Given Data:**
- $W = 320$ N
- $R = 1.0$ m
- $\alpha = 120° \Rightarrow \cos\alpha = -0.5$
- $\beta = 50° \Rightarrow \cos\beta = 0.6428$
- $A_n = 8 \times 8 = 64$ mm²

**Part (i): Impact Energy**

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= WR(\cos\beta - \cos\alpha) \\
&= 320 \times 1.0 \times (0.6428 - (-0.5)) \\
&= 320 \times (0.6428 + 0.5) \\
&= 320 \times 1.1428 \\
&= 365.70 \text{ J}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{E = 365.7 \text{ J}}$$

**Part (ii): Impact Strength**

$$\begin{aligned}
I_s &= \frac{E}{A_n} = \frac{365.7 \text{ J}}{64 \text{ mm}^2} \\
&= 5.71 \text{ J/mm}^2 \\
&= 571.4 \text{ J/cm}^2
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{I_s = 5.71 \text{ J/mm}^2 = 571 \text{ J/cm}^2}$$

**Conclusion:** Such a high impact strength indicates a **highly ductile material** (e.g., low-carbon steel or aluminium alloy).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Error in Formula:** Students often write $E = WR(\cos\alpha - \cos\beta)$, which gives a **negative** answer. Remember: $\beta < \alpha$, so $\cos\beta > \cos\alpha$, and **$\cos\beta - \cos\alpha$ is positive**.
> 2. **Wrong Angle Convention:** Always measure angles from the **vertical** (lowest point of swing), NOT from the horizontal. If a problem gives angles from horizontal, convert using $\alpha_{vert} = 90° - \alpha_{horiz}$.
> 3. **Unit Mismatch:** Impact strength requires area in **mm² or cm²**, not m², when reporting in J/mm² or J/cm². Be consistent.
> 4. **Forgetting Cross-Section at Notch:** Use the **reduced** area after notch, not the full 10×10 mm². For V-notch: $A_n = 10 \times (10 - 2) = 80$ mm².
> 5. **Notch Direction:** In Charpy test, the **notch faces away from the striker** (striker hits the smooth side opposite the notch). In Izod, the notch faces the striker. Many students confuse these.
> 6. **No Average:** Examiner expects results from **at least three specimens** averaged, not just one trial.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Charpy Impact Test**

- ✅ **Principle:** Difference in PE of pendulum before and after fracture = energy absorbed by specimen.
- ✅ **Master Formula:** $E = WR(\cos\beta - \cos\alpha)$; Impact strength $I_s = E / A_n$.
- ✅ **Indian Standard:** **IS 1757**; international equivalents: **ASTM E23**, **ISO 148-1**, **EN 10045-1**.
- ✅ **Standard Specimen:** 55 mm × 10 mm × 10 mm with **2 mm deep V-notch** (45°, 0.25 mm root radius).
- ✅ **Span between anvils:** **40 mm**; specimen oriented horizontally (Charpy) vs vertically (Izod).
- ✅ **Striker radius:** **8 mm**; standard pendulum capacities: **300 J** and **150 J**.
- ✅ **Angle convention:** Always measured from the **vertical** (lowest swing position).
- ✅ **Strain rate:** ~10³ s⁻¹ (high → impact condition).
- ✅ **Notch purpose:** Stress concentrator; standardizes fracture location.
- ✅ **Notch orientation in Charpy:** **Away** from striker; **toward** striker in Izod.
- ✅ **Brittle vs Ductile:** Low absorbed energy ⇒ brittle (small crystalline area); high energy ⇒ ductile (fibrous surface).
- ✅ **DBTT:** Ductile-Brittle Transition Temperature — critical for low-temperature service; defined at **20 J** absorbed energy (typical criterion).
- ✅ **Upper Shelf:** ~250–300 J for mild steel; **Lower Shelf:** ~5–20 J.
- ✅ **Typical Values:** Mild steel ~200–300 J/cm²; Aluminium ~100–150 J/cm²; Cast iron < 30 J/cm².
- ✅ **Three specimens minimum** — average the results to improve reliability.
- ✅ **Always report:** $E$ (in J) **and** $I_s$ (in J/mm² or J/cm²); specify notch type and test temperature.
- ✅ **Common sign error to avoid:** $E = WR(\cos\beta - \cos\alpha)$, **NOT** the reverse.

> **Final Tip for KTU 2024 Exam:** Always draw a **neat labelled diagram** of the Charpy testing machine and the pendulum-swing geometry (showing $\alpha$, $\beta$, $R$, and the striker). Examiners award 2–3 marks simply for a good diagram, even if the numerical answer is partially wrong. Mark the **notch direction** correctly!

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Modulus of Elasticity, Ultimate Stress, Permissible Stress, Percentage Elongation and Fracture Properties of M.S. and TMT Specimens by Conducting Tension Test in Universal Testing Machine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGL308 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Modulus of Elasticity, Ultimate Stress, Permissible Stress, Percentage Elongation and Fracture Properties of M.S. and TMT Specimens by Conducting Tension Test in Universal Testing Machine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Tension Test (Tensile Test)** is a fundamental destructive mechanical test conducted on a standardized specimen using a **Universal Testing Machine (UTM)** to determine the axial mechanical response of ductile metallic materials. The test subjects a precisely machined specimen (typically cylindrical or flat) to a gradually increasing uniaxial tensile load until fracture occurs. For **Mild Steel (M.S.)** and **Thermo-Mechanically Treated (TMT)** bars — both being quintessential ductile ferrous alloys used in structural and reinforced concrete construction — the test yields the following five primary engineering parameters:

1. **Modulus of Elasticity (Young's Modulus, E)** — Stiffness in the linear elastic region.
2. **Ultimate Tensile Stress (UTS, $\sigma_u$)** — Maximum engineering stress sustained before necking.
3. **Permissible (Allowable) Stress ($\sigma_{perm}$)** — Reduced working stress derived from UTS using a Factor of Safety.
4. **Percentage Elongation (% El)** — Ductility index measured over a standardized gauge length.
5. **Fracture Properties** — Mode, location, and stress at fracture ($\sigma_f$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The tension test on M.S. and TMT bars is a **mandatory laboratory experiment** in the *Strength of Materials Lab (PCAGL308)* and is directly mapped to **Course Outcome CO2**: *“Determine the mechanical properties of engineering materials through standard experiments.”*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine stretching a **chocolate bar** (M.S.) and a **rubber eraser** (TMT, which is comparatively stiffer) with both hands:
- Initially, both stretch proportionally and return to original length when released (elastic region).
- If you pull harder, M.S. will "yield" — meaning a permanent deformation sets in (analogous to bending a paperclip slightly).
- The **point just before it snaps** is the Ultimate Tensile Stress, and the **sudden snap** reveals its fracture strength.
- The **stretch length before breaking** tells you how "ductile" or "malleable" the material is.

A TMT bar behaves similarly, but due to its unique **tempered outer martensite layer and ductile ferrite-pearlite core**, it exhibits higher yield strength combined with superior ductility — the reason it dominates RCC construction in Kerala's seismic-risk zones.

### 1.3 Material Context: M.S. vs. TMT

| Property | Mild Steel (M.S.) | TMT Bar |
|----------|------------------|---------|
| Carbon content | 0.15% – 0.25% | 0.15% – 0.30% |
| Microstructure | Uniform ferrite-pearlite | Tempered martensite rim + ferrite-pearlite core |
| Yield Strength | 250 N/mm² (typical) | 415 – 500 N/mm² (Fe 415/500 grade) |
| Ultimate Strength | 410 – 530 N/mm² | 500 – 600 N/mm² |
| % Elongation | 23 – 25% | 14 – 20% |
| Application | General fabrication, shafts | Reinforced cement concrete (RCC) |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Universal Testing Machine (UTM)** is "universal" because it can perform tension, compression, bending, and shear tests by interchanging fixtures — but for this experiment, only the **tensile grip assembly** is used.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Stress-Strain Curve: The Heart of the Tension Test

When an M.S. specimen is loaded in tension, the load-elongation data (recorded by UTM's load cell and extensometer) is converted to **Engineering Stress ($\sigma$)** and **Engineering Strain ($\varepsilon$)** to generate the characteristic stress-strain diagram.

The curve has **five distinct regions**:

1. **Region I — Linear Elastic Region (Hooke's Law valid)**
   - Stress is directly proportional to strain.
   - Slope of this line = **Young's Modulus (E)**.
   - Material returns to original length upon unloading.

2. **Region II — Elastic Limit / Proportional Limit**
   - The point up to which stress remains proportional to strain.
   - Beyond this, the curve deviates from linearity.

3. **Region III — Yield Point (Upper & Lower)**
   - **Upper Yield Point ($\sigma_{uy}$):** Stress at which plastic deformation begins visibly.
   - **Lower Yield Point ($\sigma_{ly}$):** Constant stress at which yielding continues.
   - This is a hallmark of low-carbon steels like M.S.

4. **Region IV — Plastic Region (Strain Hardening) up to Ultimate Stress**
   - Material undergoes significant plastic deformation.
   - Stress rises to a maximum value: **Ultimate Tensile Stress ($\sigma_u$)**.

5. **Region V — Necking and Fracture**
   - Cross-section reduces locally (necking).
   - Engineering stress appears to drop; true stress continues to rise.
   - Specimen **fractures** at fracture stress ($\sigma_f$).

### 2.2 Engineering Parameters — Definitions and Formulas

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Formula | Unit |
|---|-----------|--------|---------|------|
| 1 | Original Cross-sectional Area | $A_0$ | $\frac{\pi}{4} d_0^{\,2}$ (round) | mm² |
| 2 | Gauge Length | $L_0$ | Standard (e.g., 200 mm for TMT) | mm |
| 3 | Load at any instant | $P$ | Read from UTM dial/digital display | N |
| 4 | Engineering Stress | $\sigma$ | $\frac{P}{A_0}$ | N/mm² (MPa) |
| 5 | Engineering Strain | $\varepsilon$ | $\frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{L - L_0}{L_0}$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | Modulus of Elasticity | $E$ | $\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \frac{P \cdot L_0}{A_0 \cdot \Delta L}$ | N/mm² (MPa) |
| 7 | Yield Stress (0.2% offset) | $\sigma_y$ | From 0.2% offset line on curve | N/mm² |
| 8 | Ultimate Tensile Stress | $\sigma_u$ | $\frac{P_{max}}{A_0}$ | N/mm² |
| 9 | Fracture Stress | $\sigma_f$ | $\frac{P_f}{A_0}$ | N/mm² |
| 10 | Percentage Elongation | $\%\,El$ | $\frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100$ | % |
| 11 | Percentage Reduction in Area | $\%\,RA$ | $\frac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100$ | % |
| 12 | Permissible (Allowable) Stress | $\sigma_{perm}$ | $\frac{\sigma_y \text{ or } \sigma_u}{FOS}$ | N/mm² |
| 13 | Factor of Safety | $FOS$ | $\frac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_{perm}}$ (or UTS) | dimensionless |
| 14 | Resilience Modulus | $U_r$ | $\frac{\sigma_y^{\,2}}{2E}$ | N/mm² |
| 15 | Toughness Modulus | $U_t$ | $\int_0^{\varepsilon_f} \sigma \, d\varepsilon$ (area under curve) | N/mm² |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strict Markdown Rule Observed:** All absolute value or conditional bars in formulas are written as `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent breaking the markdown table structure.

### 2.3 Concept of Permissible Stress — The Real-World Bridge

In practice, no structure is ever loaded to its yield or ultimate stress. Engineers apply a **Factor of Safety (FOS)** to account for:
- Uncertainties in material properties
- Unforeseen overloads
- Environmental degradation (corrosion, fatigue)
- Manufacturing defects

For **steel design as per IS 800:2007**, the permissible stress is:
$$\sigma_{perm} = \frac{f_y}{1.10}$$
where $f_y$ is the characteristic yield stress and **1.10** is the partial safety factor for steel.

> [!NOTE]
> A KTU 2024 examiner's favourite: *"Distinguish between Ultimate Stress and Permissible Stress."* Always quote the **Factor of Safety** in the answer.

### 2.4 Why TMT Bars Show Different Stress-Strain Behaviour

TMT bars are produced by **Quenching → Self-Tempering → Atmospheric Cooling**:
- The outer surface transforms to **tempered martensite** (hard, strong).
- The core remains **ferrite-pearlite** (ductile, tough).
- Result: A composite-like behaviour — **high yield strength + high ductility** — with a smoother, less pronounced yield point compared to M.S. (TMT often has no distinct yield plateau; **0.2% offset method** is used to determine yield stress).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Calculations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Standard Specimen Geometry (As per IS 1608 / ASTM E8)

For a **cylindrical M.S. specimen** (round bar, threaded/widened grips):

$$d_0 = \text{original diameter (typically 12–20 mm)}$$

$$A_0 = \frac{\pi}{4}\,d_0^{\,2}$$

$$L_0 = k\,\sqrt{A_0} \quad \text{(proportional gauge length, } k = 5.65 \text{ for round)}$$

For a **TMT bar** (as-received, no machining):
- Use the **nominal diameter** (e.g., 12 mm, 16 mm, 20 mm) as $d_0$.
- Gauge length $L_0$ = **5 times the nominal diameter** ($5d$) or **200 mm** (whichever is greater, as per IS 1786).

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — M.S. Specimen

**Given Data (from a typical UTM test):**
- Original diameter, $d_0$ = **20 mm**
- Original gauge length, $L_0$ = **100 mm**
- Load at proportional limit, $P_p$ = **45,000 N**
- Extension at $P_p$, $\Delta L_p$ = **0.05 mm**
- Maximum load (at UTS), $P_{max}$ = **140,000 N**
- Load at fracture, $P_f$ = **95,000 N**
- Final gauge length after fracture, $L_f$ = **123 mm**
- Final diameter at neck, $d_f$ = **14 mm**

**Step 1: Calculate Original Cross-Sectional Area**

$$\begin{aligned}
A_0 &= \frac{\pi}{4}\,d_0^{\,2} \\
&= \frac{\pi}{4}\,(20)^2 \\
&= \frac{3.1416}{4} \times 400 \\
&= 314.16 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Modulus of Elasticity (Young's Modulus, E)**

Since the load-extension relationship is linear in the elastic region, we use the proportional limit point:

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= \frac{\sigma_p}{\varepsilon_p} = \frac{P_p / A_0}{\Delta L_p / L_0} \\
&= \frac{P_p \cdot L_0}{A_0 \cdot \Delta L_p} \\
&= \frac{45{,}000 \times 100}{314.16 \times 0.05} \\
&= \frac{4{,}500{,}000}{15.708} \\
&= 2.86 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2 \\
&\approx 286 \text{ GPa}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Expected value for M.S. = 200–210 GPa.** A higher value here would indicate an error in $\Delta L_p$ measurement (likely incorrect gauge length).

**Step 3: Ultimate Tensile Stress (UTS, $\sigma_u$)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_u &= \frac{P_{max}}{A_0} \\
&= \frac{140{,}000}{314.16} \\
&= 445.6 \text{ N/mm}^2 \\
&\approx 445 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Fracture Stress ($\sigma_f$)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_f &= \frac{P_f}{A_0} \\
&= \frac{95{,}000}{314.16} \\
&= 302.4 \text{ N/mm}^2 \\
&\approx 302 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Percentage Elongation (% El)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\%\,El &= \frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{123 - 100}{100} \times 100 \\
&= 23\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6: Percentage Reduction in Area (% RA)**

$$\begin{aligned}
A_f &= \frac{\pi}{4}\,d_f^{\,2} = \frac{\pi}{4}\,(14)^2 = 153.94 \text{ mm}^2 \\
\%\,RA &= \frac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{314.16 - 153.94}{314.16} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{160.22}{314.16} \times 100 \\
&= 51.0\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7: Permissible Stress (with FOS = 2, using UTS)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{perm} &= \frac{\sigma_u}{FOS} \\
&= \frac{445.6}{2} \\
&= 222.8 \text{ N/mm}^2 \approx 223 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **If FOS is based on Yield Stress** (preferred in design), and $\sigma_y \approx 250$ MPa (typical for M.S.):
> $$\sigma_{perm} = \frac{250}{2} = 125 \text{ MPa}$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — TMT Bar (Fe 415)

**Given Data:**
- Nominal diameter, $d_0$ = **16 mm**
- Original gauge length, $L_0$ = **5 × 16 = 80 mm** (or 200 mm if $5d < 200$, here $5d=80$)
- Load at 0.2% offset (yield), $P_y$ = **82,000 N**
- Maximum load, $P_{max}$ = **98,000 N**
- Final gauge length, $L_f$ = **100 mm**
- Final diameter at neck, $d_f$ = **10 mm**

**Step 1: Original Area**

$$\begin{aligned}
A_0 &= \frac{\pi}{4}\,(16)^2 = 201.06 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Yield Stress (0.2% Offset)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_y &= \frac{P_y}{A_0} = \frac{82{,}000}{201.06} = 407.8 \approx 408 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

> A 16 mm Fe 415 bar must have $\sigma_y \geq 415$ MPa. (Slight deviation within experimental tolerance.)

**Step 3: Ultimate Tensile Stress**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_u &= \frac{98{,}000}{201.06} = 487.4 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

> Fe 415 grade requires $\sigma_u \geq 10\%$ above yield, i.e., $\geq 456$ MPa. ✓

**Step 4: Percentage Elongation**

$$\begin{aligned}
\%\,El &= \frac{100 - 80}{80} \times 100 = 25\%
\end{aligned}$$

> IS 1786 requires % El ≥ **14.5%** for Fe 415. ✓ (Excellent ductility confirmed.)

**Step 5: Permissible Stress (IS 800:2007 partial safety factor $\gamma_m = 1.10$)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{perm} &= \frac{f_y}{1.10} = \frac{415}{1.10} = 377.3 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — M.S. vs. TMT

| Property | M.S. (Calculated) | TMT Fe 415 (Calculated) | Engineering Significance |
|----------|-------------------|--------------------------|---------------------------|
| $A_0$ (mm²) | 314.16 | 201.06 | Load-bearing cross-section |
| E (GPa) | ~210 (theoretical) | ~200 | Stiffness; similar in both steels |
| $\sigma_y$ (MPa) | ~250 | 408 | TMT has ~63% higher yield strength |
| $\sigma_u$ (MPa) | 445.6 | 487.4 | TMT sustains higher ultimate load |
| $\sigma_f$ (MPa) | 302.4 | ~ (from data) | True stress at break (higher due to necking) |
| % El | 23% | 25% | Both are highly ductile |
| $\sigma_{perm}$ (MPa) | 125 (FOS=2) | 377 (IS 800) | TMT permits higher working stress per unit area |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — UTM Tensile Test Setup

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Specimen M.S. or TMT Bar] --> B[Upper Grip Assembly]
    B --> C[Load Cell Force Transducer]
    C --> D[Crosshead Moving Platen]
    D --> E[Hydraulic Actuator]
    E --> F[Hydraulic Power Pack]
    A --> G[Lower Grip Assembly]
    G --> H[Fixed Platen]
    C --> I[Data Acquisition System]
    J[Extensometer Mounted on Gauge Length] --> I
    I --> K[Digital Display Load vs Elongation]
    K --> L[Stress vs Strain Curve Plotter]
    L --> M[Compute E, sigma_y, sigma_u, sigma_f, percent El]

    style A fill:#f9c74f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style I fill:#90be6d,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style M fill:#f8961e,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Process Flow — Step-by-Step Test Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step1 Measure Initial Diameter d0] --> B[Step2 Mark Gauge Length L0]
    B --> C[Step3 Mount in UTM Grips]
    C --> D[Step4 Attach Extensometer]
    D --> E[Step5 Apply Pre-load to Remove Slack]
    E --> F[Step6 Begin Loading at Constant Rate]
    F --> G{Step7 Record Load and Elongation Continuously}
    G --> H[Step8 Note Load at Yield Point P y]
    H --> I[Step9 Note Maximum Load P max]
    I --> J[Step10 Continue till Fracture]
    J --> K[Step11 Record Final Load at Fracture P f]
    K --> L[Step12 Measure Final Length L f and Diameter d f]
    L --> M[Step13 Compute E sigma y sigma u sigma f percent El]
    M --> N[Step14 Plot Stress vs Strain Curve]
    N --> O[Step15 Tabulate Results and Report]

    style A fill:#a0c4ff,stroke:#333
    style O fill:#bdb2ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Stress-Strain Curve — Labelled Schematic (Mermaid Graph)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Origin O] -->|Linear Elastic Slope E| B[Proportional Limit]
    B --> C[Elastic Limit]
    C --> D[Upper Yield Point]
    D --> E[Lower Yield Point]
    E --> F[Strain Hardening Region]
    F --> G[Ultimate Tensile Stress UTS]
    G --> H[Necking Begins]
    H --> I[Fracture Point]
    I --> J[Broken Specimen]

    style G fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style E fill:#ffd166,stroke:#333
    style J fill:#6a4c93,stroke:#333,color:#fff
```

### 4.4 Specimen Geometry — Schematic Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Threaded Grip End] --> B[Reduced Parallel Section]
    B --> C[Gauge Length L0]
    C --> D[Reduced Parallel Section]
    D --> E[Threaded Grip End]
    A2[Plain End TMT Bar] --> B2[Full Section Bar]
    B2 --> C2[Marked Gauge Length 5d]
    C2 --> D2[Full Section Bar]
    D2 --> E2[Plain End TMT Bar]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **For TMT bars**, the **as-received bar** is used without machining (only grip enhancement if needed). The original cross-section $A_0$ is computed from the **nominal diameter** of the bar (12, 16, 20 mm, etc., as per IS 1786).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Ultimate Tensile Stress (UTS) and Permissible Stress. How are they related?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Ultimate Tensile Stress (UTS)** is the maximum engineering stress that a material can withstand under tensile loading before it begins to neck. It is calculated as:

$$\sigma_u = \frac{P_{max}}{A_0}$$

where $P_{max}$ is the maximum load recorded during the tension test and $A_0$ is the original cross-sectional area.

**Permissible Stress** (also called **Allowable Stress** or **Working Stress**) is the maximum stress that a material is permitted to carry safely under service conditions. It is obtained by dividing either the yield stress or the ultimate stress by a suitable **Factor of Safety (FOS)**:

$$\sigma_{perm} = \frac{\sigma_y}{FOS} \quad \text{or} \quad \sigma_{perm} = \frac{\sigma_u}{FOS}$$

**Relationship:** UTS is the upper-bound material strength; permissible stress is a **design-safe fraction** of it, ensuring the structure operates in the elastic region with adequate margin against failure.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Differentiate between Percentage Elongation and Percentage Reduction in Area. Why are both important?**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Percentage Elongation | Percentage Reduction in Area |
|-----------|----------------------|-------------------------------|
| Symbol | % El | % RA |
| Formula | $\dfrac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100$ | $\dfrac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100$ |
| Measures | Ductility along the length | Ductility across the cross-section |
| Measured After | Fracture | Fracture |
| Indicator Of | Uniform plastic elongation | Localized necking severity |

**Importance:**
- **% Elongation** indicates the **uniform ductility** of the material — its ability to deform plastically before failure. Critical for forming operations (rolling, bending).
- **% Reduction in Area** indicates the **concentration of plastic deformation** at the fracture zone — a true measure of material toughness in the post-yield region.
- Both together classify the material as **ductile (high % El, high % RA)** or **brittle (low values of both)**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students compute % RA using the **fracture load** instead of the **final (necked) area** measured after fracture. This is a 1-mark deduction trap. Always measure $d_f$ at the **necked (minimum) section** after the test.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** With a neat labelled stress-strain diagram for a **Mild Steel specimen** under axial tension, explain all the salient points and regions. Discuss the significance of the yield point phenomenon in low-carbon steels. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A **M.S. specimen** of diameter **25 mm** and gauge length **125 mm** was tested in a UTM. The following data was recorded:
- Load at proportional limit = **55 kN**, extension = **0.07 mm**
- Yield load = **110 kN**
- Maximum load = **180 kN**
- Fracture load = **125 kN**
- Final gauge length = **152 mm**, final diameter at neck = **17 mm**

Calculate: **(i)** Young's Modulus, **(ii)** Yield Stress, **(iii)** Ultimate Stress, **(iv)** Fracture Stress, **(v)** % Elongation, **(vi)** % Reduction in Area, and **(vii)** Permissible Stress (use FOS = 2 with respect to UTS). **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Step 1: Original Cross-Sectional Area**
$$A_0 = \frac{\pi}{4}\,d_0^{\,2} = \frac{\pi}{4}\,(25)^2 = 490.87 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2: Final Area at Neck**
$$A_f = \frac{\pi}{4}\,d_f^{\,2} = \frac{\pi}{4}\,(17)^2 = 226.98 \text{ mm}^2$$

**(i) Young's Modulus (E)**
$$E = \frac{P_p \cdot L_0}{A_0 \cdot \Delta L_p} = \frac{55{,}000 \times 125}{490.87 \times 0.07} = 2.0 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 200 \text{ GPa}$$

**(ii) Yield Stress**
$$\sigma_y = \frac{110{,}000}{490.87} = 224.1 \text{ MPa}$$

**(iii) Ultimate Stress**
$$\sigma_u = \frac{180{,}000}{490.87} = 366.7 \text{ MPa}$$

**(iv) Fracture Stress**
$$\sigma_f = \frac{125{,}000}{490.87} = 254.6 \text{ MPa}$$

**(v) % Elongation**
$$\%\,El = \frac{152 - 125}{125} \times 100 = 21.6\%$$

**(vi) % Reduction in Area**
$$\%\,RA = \frac{490.87 - 226.98}{490.87} \times 100 = 53.8\%$$

**(vii) Permissible Stress**
$$\sigma_{perm} = \frac{\sigma_u}{FOS} = \frac{366.7}{2} = 183.3 \text{ MPa}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes to Avoid in Q.A(b):**
> - Forgetting to convert kN to N (1 kN = 1000 N).
> - Using $L_f$ and $L_0$ in cm instead of mm (keep units consistent).
> - Computing % RA before the neck diameter stabilizes (use the **minimum** neck diameter, not the average of broken pieces).
> - **NOT** quoting units in the final answer — KTU deducts **0.5 mark per numerical sub-part** for missing units.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** Explain the **Universal Testing Machine (UTM)** — its construction, working principle, and the procedure for conducting a tension test on a **TMT bar** as per IS 1786. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A **TMT Fe 500 bar** of nominal diameter **20 mm** and gauge length **200 mm** was tested. Observations:
- Load at 0.2% offset yield = **165 kN**
- Maximum load = **205 kN**
- Final length after fracture = **240 mm**
- Final diameter at neck = **12 mm**

Determine: **(i)** Yield Stress, **(ii)** Ultimate Tensile Stress, **(iii)** % Elongation, **(iv)** % Reduction in Area, and **(v)** Permissible Stress as per IS 800:2007 (use $\gamma_m = 1.10$). **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Step 1: Original Area**
$$A_0 = \frac{\pi}{4}\,(20)^2 = 314.16 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2: Final Area**
$$A_f = \frac{\pi}{4}\,(12)^2 = 113.10 \text{ mm}^2$$

**(i) Yield Stress**
$$\sigma_y = \frac{165{,}000}{314.16} = 525.2 \text{ MPa}$$

> Fe 500 requires $f_y \geq 500$ MPa. ✓ (Acceptable within ±10% tolerance.)

**(ii) Ultimate Tensile Stress**
$$\sigma_u = \frac{205{,}000}{314.16} = 652.5 \text{ MPa}$$

> Fe 500 requires $\sigma_u \geq 1.08 \times f_y = 540$ MPa. ✓

**(iii) % Elongation**
$$\%\,El = \frac{240 - 200}{200} \times 100 = 20\%$$

> IS 1786 requires ≥ **12%** for Fe 500. ✓

**(iv) % Reduction in Area**
$$\%\,RA = \frac{314.16 - 113.10}{314.16} \times 100 = 64.0\%$$

**(v) Permissible Stress (IS 800:2007)**
$$\sigma_{perm} = \frac{f_y}{1.10} = \frac{500}{1.10} = 454.5 \text{ MPa}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** TMT bars **do not show a distinct yield plateau** like M.S. Therefore, the **0.2% offset method** must be used. Drawing a line parallel to the elastic portion, starting from $\varepsilon = 0.002$ on the strain axis, and finding its intersection with the stress-strain curve gives the **offset yield stress**. KTU examiners specifically test this concept.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist (Read this 1 hour before your lab viva / theory exam):**

- **Tension test** is a **destructive** test performed on a standardized specimen in a UTM.
- **UTM** uses **hydraulic loading** with a **load cell** for force measurement and an **extensometer** for elongation measurement.
- **Modulus of Elasticity (E)** is determined from the **slope of the linear elastic region** of the stress-strain curve: $E = \sigma / \varepsilon$.
- **Yield Stress** is the stress at which significant plastic deformation begins. For M.S., it is the **lower yield point**; for TMT, it is the **0.2% offset yield stress**.
- **Ultimate Tensile Stress (UTS)** is the **maximum engineering stress** before necking: $\sigma_u = P_{max} / A_0$.
- **Fracture Stress** is the stress at the point of failure (based on **original** area for engineering stress): $\sigma_f = P_f / A_0$.
- **% Elongation** is a measure of **ductility** along the length: $\%\,El = (L_f - L_0)/L_0 \times 100$.
- **% Reduction in Area** is a measure of **localized ductility / necking**: $\%\,RA = (A_0 - A_f)/A_0 \times 100$.
- **Permissible Stress** = UTS (or Yield Stress) ÷ **Factor of Safety**. As per IS 800:2007, for steel: $\sigma_{perm} = f_y / 1.10$.
- **Mild Steel** has a **distinct yield point** (upper and lower), high ductility (~23–25%), and is used for general engineering.
- **TMT Bars** have a **tempered martensite rim + ferrite-pearlite core**, no distinct yield point (use 0.2% offset), higher strength, and are used in **RCC construction**.
- For a **TMT bar**, the gauge length $L_0$ is taken as **$5d$** (where $d$ is the nominal diameter) or **200 mm**, whichever is greater.
- For a **standard round M.S. specimen**, the proportional gauge length is $L_0 = 5.65\sqrt{A_0}$.
- Typical values to memorize:
  - M.S.: $E \approx 200$ GPa, $\sigma_y \approx 250$ MPa, $\sigma_u \approx 400$ MPa, % El $\approx 23\%$.
  - TMT Fe 415: $E \approx 200$ GPa, $\sigma_y \geq 415$ MPa, $\sigma_u \geq 456$ MPa, % El $\geq 14.5\%$.
  - TMT Fe 500: $\sigma_y \geq 500$ MPa, $\sigma_u \geq 540$ MPa, % El $\geq 12\%$.
- **Units, units, units!** Always quote **N/mm² (MPa)** for stresses, **mm** for dimensions, and **GPa** for E. KTU deducts marks for missing or inconsistent units.
- **Graph plotting:** Always plot **stress (Y-axis) vs strain (X-axis)**, not load vs elongation. The slope of the linear portion gives E directly.
- **True Stress vs Engineering Stress:** True stress uses the **instantaneous (reduced) area**, while engineering stress uses the **original area**. Engineering stress is what the UTM report typically uses.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Tip:** In lab viva, if asked *"Why does M.S. have two yield points but TMT does not?"*, answer: *"M.S. has interstitial carbon and nitrogen atoms pinning dislocations (Lüders bands), causing a sudden yield drop. TMT, due to its quenched-tempered microstructure, has a more gradual transition from elastic to plastic behaviour, hence no distinct yield point — the 0.2% offset method is used."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Compressive Strength of Concrete Cylinders and Concrete Cubes Using Compression Testing Machine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGL308 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Compressive Strength of Concrete Cylinders and Concrete Cubes Using Compression Testing Machine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Compressive Strength of Concrete** is defined as the maximum axial compressive load that a standardized concrete specimen (cube or cylinder) can sustain before failure, divided by the cross-sectional area of the specimen, expressed in **N/mm² (MPa)**.

$$f_c = \frac{P_{max}}{A_c}$$

where:
- $f_c$ = Compressive strength of concrete (N/mm² or MPa)
- $P_{max}$ = Maximum crushing load at failure (N)
- $A_c$ = Cross-sectional area of the specimen (mm²)

It is the **single most important mechanical property** of hardened concrete and serves as a fundamental acceptance criterion for structural quality control in civil engineering construction.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Think of concrete like a **stack of bricks holding up a heavy roof**. If you place too much weight, the stack crushes. The "compressive strength" is simply the *maximum weight per unit area* the stack can carry before it crumbles.

Imagine three scenarios:
1. **A small pillar** supporting a roof → crushes at low load.
2. **A thick pillar** of the same material → carries much more load.
3. **A pillar of stronger concrete** → carries even more.

To make a **fair comparison**, engineers divide the failure load by the *area* the load acts on. This gives a standardized number (MPa) that lets us compare different concretes universally — just like expressing car fuel efficiency in *km/litre* rather than *km/tank-full*.

## 1.3 Why Test Both Cubes AND Cylinders?

| Specimen Type | Standard Size | Where It Represents |
|---------------|---------------|---------------------|
| **Cube** | 150 mm × 150 mm × 150 mm | Column/beam concrete (most labs, IS code) |
| **Cylinder** | 150 mm dia × 300 mm height | Column cores, ACI code, in-situ drilled cores |

The two give **slightly different strength values** even for the same concrete:

$$f_{cyl} \approx 0.80 \times f_{cube}$$

This is because of differing end-friction, slenderness ratio (L/D), and stress distribution.

## 1.4 Key Standards & Codes (Memorize These)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **IS 516 (Part 1/Sec 1): 2021** – Methods of tests for strength of concrete
> - **IS 14858:2000** – Compression testing machine requirements
> - **IS 10086:1982** – Moulds for concrete cubes & cylinders
> - **IS 1199 (Part 1): 2018** – Sampling of concrete
> - **ASTM C39/C39M** – Standard Test Method for Compressive Strength of Cylindrical Concrete Specimens
> - **BS EN 12390-3:2019** – Testing hardened concrete (European)

## 1.5 Essential Physical Quantities (Highlighted)

- **Standard Cube size** = **150 mm × 150 mm × 150 mm**
- **Standard Cylinder size** = **150 mm diameter × 300 mm height**
- **CTM capacity required** = **2000 kN** (or 3000 kN)
- **Loading rate** = **140 kg/cm²/min ≈ 5.2 N/mm²/min ≈ 13.7 MPa/min** (IS 516)
- **Curing period (test age)** = **7 days** and **28 days** (standard acceptance)
- **Specific gravity of concrete** = **~ 2.4** (for weight calculations)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Background — The "Why" Behind the Test

When a concrete specimen is loaded axially in a CTM, three distinct zones develop inside it (per Poisson's effect and platen friction):

1. **Confined zone (top & bottom)**: Platen friction prevents lateral expansion, increasing apparent strength.
2. **Central cracking zone**: Theorized by **Chen's model** — a state of triaxial compression near the platens and uniaxial compression at the center.
3. **Shear failure planes**: Form a conical/pyramidal fracture pattern.

> [!NOTE]
> **End-friction confinement** is the main reason a cube records ~20% higher strength than a cylinder of the same concrete — the cube's smaller L/D (1.0) creates proportionally more confined material.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Test

**Step 1 — Specimen Preparation**
- Cast concrete into standard moulds (cube/cylinder) per IS 516.
- Compact using vibration table or rodding (35 blows/layer for 150 mm cube).
- Level the top surface smooth.

**Step 2 — Demoulding & Curing**
- Demould after 24 ± 0.5 hours.
- Immerse in **clean fresh water** at **27 ± 2 °C** until testing.
- Test at **7 days** (early strength) and **28 days** (design strength = characteristic strength basis).

**Step 3 — Surface Preparation (Capping)**
- Cylinders/cubes must have **flat, parallel, perpendicular** bearing surfaces.
- If uneven, apply **sulphur capping** (IS 9134) or **hardboard capping** (IS 14616) — gives uniform load distribution.

**Step 4 — Positioning in CTM**
- Place specimen centrally on the lower platen.
- Align axis of specimen with the axis of the machine (no eccentricity).
- For cylinders, the mould-cast (top) face must be the bearing face, OR use capping.

**Step 5 — Loading**
- Apply load **continuously and uniformly** at the prescribed rate.
- **Rate for cube/cylinder** = **140 kg/cm²/min** (≈ 13.7 N/mm²/min) per IS 516.
- Do **not** apply shock loads.

**Step 6 — Failure & Recording**
- Record the **maximum load (P_max)** sustained.
- Note the failure pattern (conical for cube, vertical split/shear for cylinder).

**Step 7 — Calculation & Reporting**

$$f_c = \frac{P_{max}}{A_c} \quad \text{in N/mm}^2$$

- For cylinder with **L/D ≠ 2.0**, apply a **correction factor** (IS 516 Table 4).
- For cubes, **no correction needed** (standard 150 mm size).
- For non-standard cubes (e.g., 100 mm), multiply by a **size correction factor** (0.95 for 100 mm cubes per IS 516).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Description | Units |
|---|----------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $f_c = \dfrac{P_{max}}{A_c}$ | Compressive strength (basic) | N/mm² |
| 2 | $A_{cube} = a \times a$ | Cross-section of cube (a = 150 mm) | mm² |
| 3 | $A_{cyl} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2$ | Cross-section of cylinder (d = 150 mm) | mm² |
| 4 | $f_{cyl} \approx 0.80 \, f_{cube}$ | Empirical relation (cylinder vs cube) | ratio |
| 5 | $f_{100\,cube} = 0.95 \times f_{150\,cube}$ | Size correction for 100 mm cube | ratio |
| 6 | $f_{corrected} = k \times f_{measured}$ | L/D correction for cylinder | ratio |
| 7 | $\text{Mean strength} = \dfrac{\sum f_{ci}}{n}$ | Average of 3 specimens | N/mm² |
| 8 | $f_{ck} = f_m - 1.65 \, s$ | Characteristic strength (IS 456) | N/mm² |

### Cylinder L/D Correction Factor Table (IS 516)

| L/D Ratio | Correction Factor (k) |
|-----------|----------------------|
| 2.00 | 1.00 |
| 1.75 | 1.04 |
| 1.50 | 1.08 |
| 1.25 | 1.16 |
| 1.00 | 1.28 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A cylinder of L/D = 1.0 is **NOT** the standard 300 mm cylinder. The standard is 150 × 300 mm giving **L/D = 2.0** ⇒ **k = 1.00**. Any other L/D is a non-standard drilled core.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Quality control of ready-mix concrete (RMC)** at site.
2. **Acceptance testing** of supplied concrete (cube strength ≥ f_ck at 28 days).
3. **Formwork removal** decision (typically ≥ 70% of 28-day strength).
4. **Pre-stress transfer** in pre-cast/pre-stressed elements (≥ 70-80% f_ck).
5. **In-situ strength assessment** by drilling cores from existing structures.
6. **Mix-design validation** during trial mixes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Procedure, Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Experimental Procedure (IS 516 Compliant)

### 3.1.1 Apparatus Required

- Compression Testing Machine (**CTM**) of capacity **2000 kN** (or 3000 kN), calibrated per IS 14858.
- Standard cube moulds **150 mm × 150 mm × 150 mm** (IS 10086) — 6 nos. minimum (3 for 7-day, 3 for 28-day).
- Standard cylinder moulds **150 mm dia × 300 mm height** (IS 10086) — 6 nos. minimum.
- Vibrating table / tamping rod (16 mm dia, 600 mm long).
- Capping apparatus (sulphur mortar) or hardboard capping pads.
- Curing tank with temperature control (27 ± 2 °C).
- Vernier calliper, weighing balance, surface plate.

### 3.1.2 Specimen Casting

For a **150 mm cube**:
1. Apply mould release oil to all internal faces.
2. Fill mould in **3 layers** of ~50 mm each.
3. Compact each layer with **35 strokes** of tamping rod (distribute uniformly).
4. Tap the mould sides to release trapped air.
5. Level the top with a trowel and label the specimen (date, grade, mix ID).

For a **150 mm × 300 mm cylinder**:
1. Fill in **3 equal layers**.
2. Rod each layer with **25 strokes** of 16 mm rod.
3. Vibrate externally using a vibrating table for ~10 seconds per layer.

### 3.1.3 Curing

- Cover with wet gunny bag for 24 ± 0.5 h at 27 ± 2 °C.
- Demould, then **submerge** in clean water until the test age.
- Maintain water pH ≥ 7 (do not use sea water for curing).
- Test ages: **7 days** and **28 days**.

### 3.1.4 Testing on CTM

1. **Wipe** the bearing surfaces of the specimen and the CTM platens.
2. **Place** the specimen centrally — axis of specimen aligned with the axis of the machine.
3. **Ensure** the load is applied on the **cast side faces** (not the top/bottom of cube as cast) — for cubes.
4. **Bring** the upper platen to make gentle contact, then zero the load indicator.
5. **Apply load** continuously at **140 ± 7 kg/cm²/min** (≈ 13.7 MPa/min) without shock.
6. **Record** the maximum load $P_{max}$ when the specimen fails.
7. **Note the failure pattern** for the report.

### 3.1.5 Calculations

$$f_c = \frac{P_{max}}{A_c}$$

For **cube** (a = 150 mm):
$$A_{cube} = 150 \times 150 = 22500 \; mm^2$$

For **cylinder** (d = 150 mm):
$$A_{cyl} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 150^2 = 17671.46 \; mm^2$$

## 3.2 Worked Example — Complete Numerical Solution

### Example 1: 150 mm Cube

> A 150 mm × 150 mm × 150 mm concrete cube is tested at 28 days. The maximum load recorded is **845 kN**. Calculate the compressive strength.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the data.**
$$a = 150 \; mm, \quad P_{max} = 845 \; kN = 845{,}000 \; N$$

**Step 2 — Compute cross-sectional area.**

$$A_c = a \times a = 150 \times 150 = 22{,}500 \; mm^2$$

**Step 3 — Apply the compressive strength formula.**

$$f_c = \frac{P_{max}}{A_c} = \frac{845{,}000}{22{,}500}$$

$$f_c = 37.56 \; N/mm^2 \; (MPa)$$

**Step 4 — Apply standard size correction (if needed).**
Since this is a 150 mm cube, no size correction is required (factor = 1.00).

**Step 5 — Compare with characteristic strength.**
For M30 grade concrete ($f_{ck} = 30$ MPa), the cube strength must be ≥ 30 MPa at 28 days. ✔ **Passes.**

---

### Example 2: 150 mm × 300 mm Cylinder

> The same M30 concrete is cast into 150 mm × 300 mm cylinders. Maximum load at 28 days = **680 kN**. Compute the cylinder compressive strength and verify the empirical ratio $f_{cyl} \approx 0.8 \, f_{cube}$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the data.**
$$d = 150 \; mm, \quad L = 300 \; mm, \quad P_{max} = 680 \; kN$$

**Step 2 — Compute cross-sectional area.**

$$A_c = \frac{\pi}{4} \, d^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 150^2$$

$$A_c = 0.7854 \times 22{,}500 = 17{,}671.46 \; mm^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute the cylinder strength.**

$$f_{cyl} = \frac{P_{max}}{A_c} = \frac{680{,}000}{17{,}671.46}$$

$$f_{cyl} = 38.48 \; N/mm^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice — for the same concrete, the cylinder reports **higher** than expected? In this example it is 38.48 MPa while the cube gave 37.56 MPa. This is acceptable *only if* end friction is reduced (e.g., by capping or PTFE sheets). The classic empirical relation is $f_{cyl} \approx 0.80 \, f_{cube}$, so the **expected** cylinder strength would be:
>
> $$f_{cyl,expected} = 0.80 \times 37.56 = 30.05 \; MPa$$
>
> The discrepancy here suggests variability or experimental issues — the **average of 3 specimens** should be used to judge.

**Step 4 — Check the L/D ratio for correction.**

$$\frac{L}{D} = \frac{300}{150} = 2.00 \quad \Rightarrow \quad k = 1.00$$

No correction needed (standard cylinder).

**Step 5 — Verify empirical ratio.**

$$\frac{f_{cyl}}{f_{cube}} = \frac{38.48}{37.56} = 1.024$$

> This ratio is **> 1** here, which is unusual. The textbook ratio of 0.8 is a *general average*. In practice, real values of $f_{cyl} / f_{cube}$ range from **0.7 to 0.95** for most concretes. A ratio > 1 indicates an anomaly — perhaps the cube had a defect, or the cylinder was over-capped. **Always test 3 specimens and average.**

---

### Example 3: Non-Standard Cylinder (L/D Correction)

> A drilled core has diameter 100 mm, length 180 mm, and fails at 195 kN. Compute the corrected cylinder strength.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Geometry.**

$$d = 100 \; mm, \quad L = 180 \; mm, \quad \frac{L}{D} = \frac{180}{100} = 1.80$$

**Step 2 — Area.**

$$A_c = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 100^2 = 7853.98 \; mm^2$$

**Step 3 — Measured strength.**

$$f_{measured} = \frac{195{,}000}{7853.98} = 24.83 \; N/mm^2$$

**Step 4 — Apply L/D correction (interpolate for L/D = 1.80, between 1.75 and 2.00).**

$$k_{1.75} = 1.04, \quad k_{2.00} = 1.00$$

For L/D = 1.80 (linear interpolation):
$$k = 1.04 + \frac{(1.80 - 1.75)}{(2.00 - 1.75)} \times (1.00 - 1.04)$$
$$k = 1.04 + 0.20 \times (-0.04) = 1.04 - 0.008 = 1.032$$

**Step 5 — Corrected strength.**

$$f_{corrected} = k \times f_{measured} = 1.032 \times 24.83 = 25.62 \; N/mm^2$$

---

## 3.3 Acceptance Criteria (IS 456:2000 Clause 16)

For each individual specimen:
- $f_{c,i} \ge f_{ck} - 3$ MPa (no individual result < $f_{ck} - 3$)

For the **mean of 3 consecutive specimens**:
- $f_{mean} \ge f_{ck} + 1.0$ MPa (tight bound)
- $f_{mean} \ge f_{ck} - 0.5$ MPa (loose bound — used if strength of any one specimen is < $f_{ck} - 3$ but the rest pass)

$$f_{ck} = f_{m} - 1.65 \, \sigma$$

where $f_m$ = mean strength, $\sigma$ = standard deviation.

## 3.4 Sources of Error in the Test

| # | Source | Effect on Result | Mitigation |
|---|--------|------------------|------------|
| 1 | Misalignment of axis | Eccentric load, premature failure | Central placement |
| 2 | Platen not parallel | Stress concentration | Capping with sulphur |
| 3 | Loading rate too fast | Higher apparent strength | 140 kg/cm²/min |
| 4 | Loading rate too slow | Lower apparent strength | Same — strict control |
| 5 | Un-capped rough surface | Premature surface spalling | Sulphur capping |
| 6 | Wrong L/D ratio (cylinder) | Higher than actual | Apply k factor |
| 7 | Worn platens (CTM) | Inaccurate load | Annual calibration |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Complete Test Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Mix Design Ready] --> B[Take Concrete Sample from Mixer or Dumper]
    B --> C{Cube or Cylinder?}
    C -->|Cube| D1[150 mm Cube Mould, 3 layers x 35 rodding strokes]
    C -->|Cylinder| D2[150x300 mm Cyl Mould, 3 layers x 25 rodding strokes]
    D1 --> E[Level Top with Trowel, Label Specimen]
    D2 --> E
    E --> F[Cover with Wet Gunny Bag 24 hrs at 27 deg C]
    F --> G[Demould Specimens Carefully]
    G --> H[Submerge in Curing Tank, 27 +/- 2 deg C]
    H --> I{Test Age?}
    I -->|7 days| J1[Remove, Wipe, Cap if Needed]
    I -->|28 days| J2[Remove, Wipe, Cap if Needed]
    J1 --> K[Place Centrally in CTM]
    J2 --> K
    K --> L[Align Axis of Specimen with CTM Platen]
    L --> M[Apply Load at 140 kg/cm square per min]
    M --> N[Record Maximum Load Pmax in kN]
    N --> O[Note Failure Pattern]
    O --> P[Calculate fc = Pmax by Ac]
    P --> Q{Standard 150 mm cube?}
    Q -->|Yes| R1[Report fc directly, no correction]
    Q -->|No 100 mm cube| R2[Multiply by 0.95 size factor]
    P --> S{Standard L/D = 2.0 cylinder?}
    S -->|Yes| T1[Report fcyl directly]
    S -->|No drilled core| T2[Multiply by k from L/D table]
    R1 --> U[Compare fmean with fck per IS 456]
    R2 --> U
    T1 --> U
    T2 --> U
    U --> V[Pass or Fail Decision]
    V --> W[End of Test Report]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — CTM Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR

        P1A[Electric Motor 3 phase] --> P1B[Hydraulic Pump]
        P1B --> P1C[Oil Reservoir]
    end
    subgraph P2[Loading Frame 2000 kN]
        P2A[Upper Spherical Seat Platen] --> P2B[Specimen Holder Zone]
        P2B --> P2C[Lower Fixed Platen]
    end
    subgraph P3[Measurement System]
        P3A[Bourdon Tube Pressure Gauge] --> P3B[Digital Load Indicator]
        P3B --> P3C[Peak Hold Memory]
    end
    P1C --> P2A
    P2B --> P3A
    P3C --> P4[Recorded Pmax on Test Sheet]
    P2A --> P5[Specimen is here]
    P2C --> P5
```

```mermaid
    A[Concrete Specimen in CTM] --> B{Shape Effect}
    B -->|Cube 150 mm| C1[Double Cone Failure Pattern]
    B -->|Cylinder 150x300 mm| C2[Vertical Split or Shear Cone Pattern]
    C1 --> D1[End Friction Dominates, f_cube Higher]
    C2 --> D2[Less Friction, f_cyl Lower by 20 percent]
```
