<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question A1
> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | 3 Marks]**
> Define compressive strength of concrete. Why is it tested on 150 mm cubes as per IS 516?

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Compressive strength of concrete is the maximum compressive stress that a standardized concrete specimen (cube or cylinder) can resist under axial loading without failure. It is calculated as the ratio of the maximum load to the cross-sectional area.

$$f_c = \frac{P_{max}}{A_c}$$

**Why 150 mm cubes (3 key points):**
1. **IS 516 standardization** — 150 mm cube is the reference specimen; 100 mm cubes need a size correction factor of 0.95, so 150 mm avoids extra correction.
2. **Repeatability and uniformity** — the larger size reduces scatter due to aggregates (max 20 mm aggregate fits comfortably).
3. **Familiarity and comparison** — Indian industry uses cube strength as the basis for mix design (IS 10262) and acceptance (IS 456).

> **Board Tip:** Mention "cross-sectional area" and "maximum load" — examiners look for these two terms.

---

### Question A2
> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | 3 Marks]**
> State the rate of loading prescribed by IS 516 for testing concrete cubes in a Compression Testing Machine. What happens if the rate is too high or too low?

**Model Answer:**

**Standard Rate (IS 516):**
The prescribed rate of loading is **140 kg/cm² per minute** (equivalent to **13.7 N/mm² per minute** or **5.2 MPa per minute** for a 150 mm cube).

**Effect of high rate (too fast):**
- Specimen fails at a **higher** apparent strength.
- Brittle failure — no time for micro-crack propagation to develop fully.
- Result is **non-conservative** (unsafe in practice).

**Effect of low rate (too slow):**
- Specimen fails at a **lower** apparent strength.
- Creep effects add to the load, masking true short-term strength.
- Result is **over-conservative** — wastes material in mix design.

> **Board Tip:** Always state the rate with units — examiners will deduct 0.5 mark if units are missing.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### Question B (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | 14 Marks]**
> **(a)** Describe the procedure for the determination of compressive strength of hardened concrete using 150 mm cubes, as per IS 516. Mention the apparatus required and the precautions to be taken. **[7 Marks]**
>
> **(b)** The following loads were recorded at failure for three 150 mm concrete cubes tested at 28 days: 870 kN, 895 kN and 905 kN. Calculate the average compressive strength and check whether the concrete satisfies the acceptance criteria for M30 grade as per IS 456. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Apparatus required:**
1. Compression Testing Machine (CTM) of capacity 2000 kN, calibrated as per IS 14858.
2. Standard 150 mm cube moulds conforming to IS 10086.
3. Tamping rod — 16 mm dia, 600 mm long with bullet tip.
4. Vibrating table (alternative to hand rodding).
5. Curing tank with temperature 27 ± 2 °C.
6. Sulphur capping compound (if needed) and capping frame.
7. Vernier calliper, weighing balance.

**Procedure:**

1. **Mould preparation:** Apply a thin layer of mould release oil inside clean moulds; assemble and tighten properly.
2. **Casting:** Fill the mould with fresh concrete in **3 equal layers** of 50 mm each. Compact each layer uniformly with **35 strokes** of the tamping rod. Tap the sides of the mould to release entrapped air.
3. **Finishing:** Level the top surface with a trowel and label the specimen (date, grade, mix ID).
4. **Curing:** Cover the specimens with a wet gunny bag for 24 ± 0.5 h. Demould carefully and **submerge** in clean water at 27 ± 2 °C until the test age (7 or 28 days).
5. **Testing:** Remove the specimen from the curing tank, wipe off surface water. Check that the bearing surfaces of the specimen and CTM platens are clean. Place the specimen **centrally** on the lower platen such that the load is applied on the **side faces** (cast faces) of the cube.
6. **Loading:** Apply the load **continuously and uniformly** at **140 kg/cm²/min (≈ 13.7 MPa/min)** without any shock. Record the **maximum load (P_max)** at failure.
7. **Reporting:** Note the failure pattern (typically double-cone for cubes) and compute $f_c = P_{max}/A_c$.

**Precautions:**
- The axis of the specimen must coincide with the axis of the machine (no eccentricity).
- Platens must be clean and parallel.
- Loading rate must be strictly maintained.
- The cube must be tested while still wet (saturated surface dry) — never let it dry.
- For uneven surfaces, use sulphur capping to ensure uniform load distribution.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Given data:**
$$P_1 = 870 \; kN, \quad P_2 = 895 \; kN, \quad P_3 = 905 \; kN$$
$$a = 150 \; mm, \quad A_c = 150 \times 150 = 22{,}500 \; mm^2$$
$$f_{ck} = 30 \; MPa \; \text{(M30 grade per IS 456)}$$

**Step 1 — Compute individual strengths:**

$$f_{c1} = \frac{870 \times 10^3}{22{,}500} = 38.67 \; MPa$$

$$f_{c2} = \frac{895 \times 10^3}{22{,}500} = 39.78 \; MPa$$

$$f_{c3} = \frac{905 \times 10^3}{22{,}500} = 40.22 \; MPa$$

**Step 2 — Mean strength:**

$$f_m = \frac{38.67 + 39.78 + 40.22}{3} = \frac{118.67}{3} = 39.56 \; MPa$$

**Step 3 — Apply IS 456 acceptance criteria:**

**Criterion 1** — No individual strength < $f_{ck} - 3$ MPa:
$$f_{ck} - 3 = 30 - 3 = 27 \; MPa$$
$$f_{c1} = 38.67 \ge 27 \; \text{✔ Passes}$$

**Criterion 2** — Mean of 3 consecutive results ≥ $f_{ck} + 1.0$ MPa (tight bound):
$$f_{ck} + 1.0 = 31.0 \; MPa$$
$$f_m = 39.56 \ge 31.0 \; \text{✔ Passes}$$

**Conclusion:**
The concrete **satisfies** the acceptance criteria for M30 grade. It is **acceptable** for use. The actual mean strength (39.56 MPa) is about **31.9% above** the characteristic strength, indicating good quality concrete with adequate margin.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | 14 Marks]**
> **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the working of a 2000 kN Compression Testing Machine (CTM). Mention the function of the spherical seat. **[7 Marks]**
>
> **(b)** A 150 mm × 300 mm concrete cylinder recorded a maximum load of 720 kN at 28 days. Calculate the cylinder compressive strength. If a 150 mm cube of the same concrete recorded 38.5 MPa, compute the ratio $f_{cyl}/f_{cube}$ and comment on the result. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Working of CTM (2000 kN):**

A Compression Testing Machine (CTM) is a **hydraulically operated** machine used to apply controlled axial compressive loads on concrete specimens. The main components are:

1. **Loading frame** — a robust four-column frame carrying the upper and lower platens. The lower platen is fixed; the upper platen is movable.
2. **Hydraulic power pack** — consists of an electric motor coupled to a hydraulic pump that pressurizes oil from a reservoir.
3. **Pressure gauge / digital load indicator** — measures the hydraulic pressure, which is calibrated to read directly as load (kN).
4. **Spherical seat (upper platen)** — allows the upper platen to tilt slightly and self-align with the top surface of the specimen, ensuring **uniform contact** and **zero eccentricity** of load.
5. **Control valve** — regulates the flow of oil, thus controlling the loading rate.

**Working principle:**
- The specimen is placed on the lower platen and aligned with the machine axis.
- Oil is pumped into the hydraulic cylinder; the piston rises and pushes the upper platen (via the spherical seat) onto the specimen.
- The pressure builds up; the load applied equals the oil pressure multiplied by the piston area.
- The load is read on the gauge; the **peak hold** function captures the maximum load at failure.
- After failure, the valve is released, the piston retracts, and the broken specimen is removed.

**Function of the spherical seat (3 points):**
1. **Self-alignment** — automatically tilts to match minor surface irregularities of the specimen, ensuring uniform contact.
2. **Prevents eccentric loading** — without it, even a 1° tilt would cause a bending moment and premature failure.
3. **Distributes load uniformly** — gives a more accurate measurement of true compressive strength.

> **Sketch (textual description):** A rectangular loading frame with two horizontal platens, a spherical seat between the upper platen and the crosshead, the specimen in the middle, and a hydraulic cylinder with pressure gauge and control valve on the side.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Given data:**
$$d = 150 \; mm, \quad L = 300 \; mm, \quad P_{max} = 720 \; kN$$
$$f_{cube} = 38.5 \; MPa$$

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area of cylinder:**

$$A_c = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 150^2 = 17{,}671.46 \; mm^2$$

**Step 2 — Cylinder strength:**

$$f_{cyl} = \frac{P_{max}}{A_c} = \frac{720{,}000}{17{,}671.46} = 40.74 \; MPa$$

**Step 3 — Compute the ratio:**

$$\frac{f_{cyl}}{f_{cube}} = \frac{40.74}{38.50} = 1.058$$

**Step 4 — Comment:**

The textbook empirical relation is $f_{cyl} \approx 0.8 \, f_{cube}$, which means for a cube strength of 38.5 MPa, the **expected** cylinder strength would be:

$$f_{cyl,expected} = 0.8 \times 38.5 = 30.8 \; MPa$$

However, the **measured** value is 40.74 MPa, giving a ratio of 1.058 — i.e., the cylinder is **stronger** than the cube, which is **unusual**. Possible reasons:

1. The cube may have a defect (e.g., poor compaction, honeycombing).
2. The cylinder capping may have been done exceptionally well, reducing end-friction effects.
3. Statistical variation in a single test — averaging 3 specimens would give a more reliable ratio.

**Recommendation:** The cube strength is the design reference for IS code compliance. The cylinder test is supplementary. The unusually high ratio should prompt re-testing of fresh specimens.

---

## 5.3 ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly LOSE marks in this experiment (read carefully!):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert kN → N** when applying $f_c = P/A$. P must be in Newtons, A in mm². A common error: writing $f_c = 870/22500$ (wrong) instead of $870000/22500$ (correct).
>
> 2. **Wrong area formula for cylinder.** Some students use $\pi d^2$ instead of $\pi d^2 / 4$. The factor of 1/4 is non-negotiable.
>
> 3. **Not stating loading rate with units.** "Apply load at 140 kg/cm²/min" — missing units = **–0.5 mark**.
>
> 4. **Confusing cube and cylinder in the empirical ratio.** The correct relation is $f_{cyl} = 0.8 \, f_{cube}$, NOT $f_{cube} = 0.8 \, f_{cyl}$.
>
> 5. **Skipping the L/D correction for non-standard cylinders.** A drilled core (L/D < 2) MUST be corrected using the IS 516 table.
>
> 6. **Not mentioning the spherical seat function** in the CTM diagram question — this is a **2-mark** item in long answers.
>
> 7. **Writing "spherical ball joint" instead of "spherical seat"** — the standard term is *spherical seat*.
>
> 8. **Mixing up $f_{ck}$ and $f_m$** in the acceptance criteria. $f_{ck}$ is the **characteristic** (target) value; $f_m$ is the **measured mean**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — must-know facts in 60 seconds:**

- ☐ **Test standards:** IS 516 (procedure), IS 14858 (CTM), IS 10086 (moulds), IS 456 (acceptance).
- ☐ **Standard cube** = 150 mm × 150 mm × 150 mm. **Standard cylinder** = 150 mm dia × 300 mm height (L/D = 2.0).
- ☐ **CTM capacity** = 2000 kN. **Loading rate** = 140 kg/cm²/min ≈ 13.7 MPa/min.
- ☐ **Master formula:** $f_c = P_{max} / A_c$. Units: N and mm² → result in N/mm² = MPa.
- ☐ **Cube area** = $a^2$. **Cylinder area** = $\pi d^2 / 4$.
- ☐ **Empirical relation:** $f_{cyl} \approx 0.80 \, f_{cube}$ (cube is always stronger due to platen friction).
- ☐ **Cylinder L/D correction** (k): 2.0 → 1.00, 1.75 → 1.04, 1.50 → 1.08, 1.25 → 1.16, 1.00 → 1.28.
- ☐ **100 mm cube correction** = multiply by 0.95.
- ☐ **Test ages:** 7 days (early) and 28 days (design = characteristic strength basis).
- ☐ **Curing:** 24 h in mould, then immersed in water at 27 ± 2 °C.
- ☐ **Capping:** Sulphur mortar capping for non-flat surfaces; hardboard pads as alternative.
- ☐ **Spherical seat** self-aligns the upper platen — prevents eccentric loading.
- ☐ **Failure pattern — cube** = double cone (pyramidal). **Cylinder** = vertical split / shear cone.
- ☐ **Mean of 3 specimens** is reported; minimum sample size per test age = 3.
- ☐ **Acceptance per IS 456:** No individual < $f_{ck} - 3$ MPa AND mean of 3 ≥ $f_{ck} + 1.0$ MPa.
- ☐ **Characteristic strength formula:** $f_{ck} = f_m - 1.65 \sigma$ (basis of mix-design margin).
- ☐ **Lab precautions:** central placement, no shock load, axis alignment, calibrated CTM.

> **Final 1-line memory aid:** *"Compressive strength = Load ÷ Area, at 13.7 MPa per minute, on a 150 mm cube, after 28 days of water curing at 27 degrees."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Various Physical and Mechanical Properties of M.S. Girder, Timber Beam, and R.C.C. Beam by Performing the Bending Test in Universal Testing Machine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGL308 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Various Physical and Mechanical Properties of M.S. Girder, Timber Beam, and R.C.C. Beam by Performing the Bending Test in Universal Testing Machine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bending Test (Flexural Test)** is a standardized mechanical test in which a beam specimen (M.S. Girder, Timber Beam, or R.C.C. Beam) is subjected to a transverse load under a defined support span in a **Universal Testing Machine (UTM)**, while the resulting deflection and failure load are continuously recorded. The test enables the determination of physical dimensions, flexural strength (modulus of rupture), bending stress, deflection, modulus of elasticity, and the overall load–deflection behaviour of the material under flexure.

In KTU terminology, this experiment is classified under **PCAGL308 – Strength of Materials Laboratory (Module Experiment 2)** and is mapped to the following Course Outcomes:

- **CO1:** Understand the fundamental mechanical response of engineering materials under flexural loading.
- **CO2:** Apply bending theory (Euler–Bernoulli beam theory) to calculate stresses and deflections.
- **CO3:** Interpret load–deflection curves and classify material behaviour (brittle, ductile, quasi-brittle).
- **CO4:** Evaluate and report flexural properties as per relevant Indian Standard codes (IS 1608, IS 1708, IS 516).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bridge Plank" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine walking across a wooden plank placed between two stools. As you step into the middle, the plank bends slightly downward. If you jump, it bends more. If the plank is thick, it bends very little; if it is thin, it bends a lot. The plank eventually **snaps** if your weight is high enough. The bending test is exactly this — except we measure the *exact force*, the *exact bend*, and the *exact moment of snap* using a machine.

Each beam behaves differently:
- **M.S. Girder:** Like a strong steel ruler — bends a lot before permanently deforming, then yields and finally fails.
- **Timber Beam:** Like a wooden cricket bat handle — moderately stiff, fails with a sudden fibrous crack.
- **R.C.C. Beam:** Like a concrete lintel reinforced with steel rods — very stiff, fails with cracking of concrete after the steel yields.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Young's Modulus of Mild Steel (E):** ≈ **200 GPa (2 × 10⁵ N/mm²)**
- **Young's Modulus of Timber (Teak):** ≈ **10–13 GPa**
- **Young's Modulus of Concrete:** ≈ **25–30 GPa**
- **Density of Mild Steel:** ≈ **7850 kg/m³**
- **Density of Hardwood (Teak):** ≈ **650–750 kg/m³**
- **Density of Concrete:** ≈ **2400 kg/m³**
- **Standard Gravity:** **g = 9.81 m/s²**
- **Standard Loading Rates:** As per IS codes (e.g., concrete: **0.06 N/mm²/s**; timber: **2.5 mm/min**)

## 1.4 Key Standards Followed

| Material | Indian Standard | Test Configuration |
|---|---|---|
| Mild Steel | IS 1608, IS 2062 | 3-point bending on I-section |
| Timber | IS 1708, IS 875 (Part 1) | 3-point or 4-point bending |
| R.C.C. Beam | IS 516, IS 456 | 2-point (third-point) loading |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** For R.C.C. beams, the **third-point loading (4-point bending)** is the *preferred* configuration as per IS 516 because it produces a region of **pure bending (zero shear)** between the two loading points.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Theory of Bending — The Governing Framework

The bending test relies on the **Euler–Bernoulli beam theory**, valid when:

1. Deflections are small compared to the span.
2. Plane sections remain plane and perpendicular to the neutral axis after bending.
3. The material is homogeneous and isotropic (strictly valid for M.S.; approximate for timber and R.C.C.).

### 2.1.1 Bending Stress Equation

The bending stress at any fibre located at a distance *y* from the neutral axis is given by the **Flexure Formula**:

$$\sigma = \frac{M \cdot y}{I}$$

where:

- $\sigma$ = bending (flexural) stress at the fibre (N/mm²)
- $M$ = bending moment at the section (N·mm)
- $y$ = distance from neutral axis to the fibre (mm)
- $I$ = second moment of area about the neutral axis (mm⁴)

The **section modulus**, $Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{max}}$, simplifies the maximum bending stress to:

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M}{Z}$$

### 2.1.2 Three-Point Bending vs Four-Point Bending

| Feature | 3-Point Bending | 4-Point Bending (Third-Point) |
|---|---|---|
| Load application | Single central load | Two equal loads at L/3 and 2L/3 |
| Max Bending Moment | $M_{max} = \dfrac{PL}{4}$ | $M_{max} = \dfrac{PL}{6}$ |
| Pure bending region | Only at mid-span point | Between the two load points |
| Shear present at max moment | Yes | No |
| Preferred for | Metals, timber | Concrete (per IS 516) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When the question specifies "simply supported beam with central point load," the formula is $\sigma = \dfrac{3PL}{2bd^2}$ for a rectangular cross-section. This is derived by substituting $M = \dfrac{PL}{4}$ and $Z = \dfrac{bd^2}{6}$.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below consolidates **every formula** you will need to solve bending-test problems in the KTU 2024 Scheme examination. **Memorize these.**

| # | Property | Formula | Units | Applicable To |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Max Bending Moment (3-point) | $M = \dfrac{PL}{4}$ | N·mm | All beams |
| 2 | Max Bending Moment (4-point) | $M = \dfrac{PL}{6}$ | N·mm | R.C.C. beams |
| 3 | Max Bending Stress (rectangular) | $\sigma = \dfrac{3PL}{2bd^2}$ | N/mm² | Timber, R.C.C. |
| 4 | Max Bending Stress (I-section) | $\sigma = \dfrac{M}{Z_e} = \dfrac{M \cdot y_{max}}{I_{xx}}$ | N/mm² | M.S. Girder |
| 5 | Section Modulus (Rectangle) | $Z = \dfrac{bd^2}{6}$ | mm³ | Timber, R.C.C. |
| 6 | Moment of Inertia (Rectangle) | $I = \dfrac{bd^3}{12}$ | mm⁴ | Timber, R.C.C. |
| 7 | Max Deflection (3-point, simply supported) | $\Delta = \dfrac{PL^3}{48EI}$ | mm | All beams |
| 8 | Max Deflection (4-point) | $\Delta = \dfrac{23 PL^3}{1296 EI}$ | mm | R.C.C. beams |
| 9 | Modulus of Elasticity (from deflection) | $E = \dfrac{PL^3}{4 \Delta b d^3}$ (3-point) | N/mm² | Timber |
| 10 | Modulus of Elasticity (4-point) | $E = \dfrac{P a (3L^2 - 4a^2)}{4 \Delta b d^3}$ | N/mm² | R.C.C. (a = L/3) |
| 11 | Modulus of Rupture (flexural strength) | $f_r = \dfrac{3 P_{max} L}{2 b d^2}$ | N/mm² | Timber, R.C.C. |
| 12 | Radius of Curvature | $\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{M}{EI}$ | 1/mm | All beams |

> **NOTE on notation:** $P$ = applied load, $L$ = effective span, $b$ = breadth, $d$ = depth, $\Delta$ = mid-span deflection, $a$ = shear span (distance from support to nearest load point).

## 2.3 Material-Specific Theory

### 2.3.1 Mild Steel (M.S.) Girder

- M.S. is a **ductile material** with a clearly defined **yield point** (≈ 250 N/mm² for Fe 410 grade).
- The I-section geometry maximizes $I_{xx}$ while minimizing self-weight — the most efficient shape for bending.
- Standard ISMB (Indian Standard Medium-weight Beam) sections such as **ISMB 100, ISMB 150, ISMB 200** are commonly used.
- The test reveals: proportional limit, yield point, ultimate strength, and percentage elongation (indirectly through curvature).

### 2.3.2 Timber Beam

- Timber is **anisotropic** — its strength along the grain is much greater than across the grain.
- Bending test on timber is governed by **IS 1708** (small clear specimens) and **IS 875 (Part 1)** for structural timber.
- Standard specimen size for small clear specimens: **50 mm × 50 mm × 750 mm** (length) with span = **700 mm** (i.e., overhang of 25 mm at each end).
- Timber exhibits **quasi-brittle** behaviour with a small plastic region.

### 2.3.3 R.C.C. Beam

- Concrete is **strong in compression, weak in tension**. Hence steel reinforcement is provided in the tension zone.
- The failure is initiated by **flexural cracking** of concrete in the tension zone, followed by yielding of tension steel, and finally crushing of concrete in the compression zone.
- For a **balanced section**, simultaneous yielding of steel and crushing of concrete occurs.
- Test per **IS 516**: Beam size typically **150 mm × 150 mm × 700 mm** (or 100×100×500 for small specimens) with span = **600 mm** (or 400 mm).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **M.S. Girders** — Used in steel-framed buildings, bridges, crane girders, railway sleepers.
- **Timber Beams** — Used in roof trusses, formwork, traditional housing, and as test specimens for grading structural timber.
- **R.C.C. Beams** — The backbone of every modern building, bridge deck, and flyover.

> The bending test thus determines whether a structural element will safely carry design loads without exceeding allowable stresses or deflections.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Bending Stress in a Rectangular Beam (3-Point Test)

We derive the formula $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{3PL}{2bd^2}$ from first principles.

**Step 1 — Bending Moment at Mid-Span**

For a simply supported beam of span $L$ with a central point load $P$, the reactions at each support equal $\dfrac{P}{2}$ by symmetry.

The bending moment at any section at a distance $x$ from the left support (for $0 \le x \le L/2$) is:

$$M(x) = \frac{P}{2} \cdot x$$

At mid-span, $x = L/2$, therefore:

$$M_{max} = \frac{P}{2} \cdot \frac{L}{2} = \frac{PL}{4}$$

**Step 2 — Section Modulus of a Rectangle**

For a rectangular cross-section of breadth $b$ and depth $d$:

$$I_{NA} = \frac{b d^3}{12}$$

$$y_{max} = \frac{d}{2}$$

Therefore:

$$Z = \frac{I}{y_{max}} = \frac{b d^3 / 12}{d / 2} = \frac{b d^2}{6}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into Flexure Formula**

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M_{max}}{Z} = \frac{PL/4}{bd^2/6}$$

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{PL}{4} \cdot \frac{6}{b d^2} = \frac{6PL}{4 b d^2} = \frac{3PL}{2bd^2}$$

This is the **standard KTU result**. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation: Modulus of Elasticity from Mid-Span Deflection

For a simply supported beam under central point load, the **double-integration method** gives:

$$\Delta_{max} = \frac{P L^3}{48 EI}$$

Rearranging for $E$:

$$E = \frac{P L^3}{48 \Delta \cdot I}$$

Substituting $I = \dfrac{b d^3}{12}$:

$$E = \frac{P L^3}{48 \Delta \cdot \frac{b d^3}{12}}$$

$$E = \frac{P L^3 \cdot 12}{48 \Delta \cdot b d^3} = \frac{P L^3}{4 \Delta \cdot b d^3}$$

This matches the formula in the cheat sheet. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (KTU Style — 14 Marks Type)

### 3.3.1 Problem Statement

A timber beam of cross-section **60 mm × 60 mm** and length **800 mm** is tested under 3-point bending over an effective span of **700 mm**. The load at mid-span at the elastic limit is **4.5 kN**, and the corresponding deflection is **3.2 mm**. Calculate:

**(a)** The maximum bending stress at the elastic limit.
**(b)** The modulus of elasticity of the timber.

### 3.3.2 Solution

#### Part (a): Maximum Bending Stress

**Given:**
- Breadth, $b = 60$ mm
- Depth, $d = 60$ mm
- Span, $L = 700$ mm
- Load at elastic limit, $P = 4.5 \text{ kN} = 4500$ N

**Step 1** — Section modulus:

$$Z = \frac{b d^2}{6} = \frac{60 \times (60)^2}{6} = \frac{60 \times 3600}{6} = 36000 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 2** — Maximum bending moment:

$$M_{max} = \frac{PL}{4} = \frac{4500 \times 700}{4} = 787500 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 3** — Maximum bending stress:

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M_{max}}{Z} = \frac{787500}{36000} = 21.875 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{max} \approx 21.88 \text{ MPa}}$$

#### Part (b): Modulus of Elasticity

**Given:**
- Deflection, $\Delta = 3.2$ mm

**Step 1** — Moment of inertia:

$$I = \frac{b d^3}{12} = \frac{60 \times (60)^3}{12} = \frac{60 \times 216000}{12} = 1080000 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 2** — Apply the formula:

$$E = \frac{P L^3}{4 \Delta \cdot b d^3}$$

$$E = \frac{4500 \times (700)^3}{4 \times 3.2 \times 60 \times (60)^3}$$

**Step 3** — Numerator:

$$4500 \times 700^3 = 4500 \times 343 \times 10^6 = 1.5435 \times 10^{12}$$

**Step 4** — Denominator:

$$4 \times 3.2 \times 60 \times 216000 = 768 \times 216000 = 1.65888 \times 10^8$$

**Step 5** — Final division:

$$E = \frac{1.5435 \times 10^{12}}{1.65888 \times 10^8} = 9304.6 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

$$\boxed{E \approx 9.30 \text{ GPa}}$$

> **Interpretation:** This value is consistent with hardwood timber (teak/sal), which typically has E in the range **8–13 GPa**. The timber is acceptable for structural use.

## 3.4 Worked Example — R.C.C. Beam (4-Point Bending per IS 516)

### 3.4.1 Problem Statement

An R.C.C. beam of size **150 mm × 150 mm × 700 mm** is tested under two-point loading (each at L/3 from support) over a span of **600 mm**. The failure load is **45 kN**. Calculate the modulus of rupture.

### 3.4.2 Solution

**Given:**
- $b = 150$ mm, $d = 150$ mm, $L = 600$ mm
- $P_{total} = 45$ kN, so $P$ per loading point (in 4-point bending, $P$ denotes total load on the beam) = 45 kN = 45000 N

**Step 1** — Maximum bending moment (pure bending region):

$$M_{max} = \frac{P L}{6} = \frac{45000 \times 600}{6} = 4.5 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 2** — Section modulus:

$$Z = \frac{b d^2}{6} = \frac{150 \times (150)^2}{6} = \frac{150 \times 22500}{6} = 562500 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 3** — Modulus of rupture:

$$f_r = \frac{M_{max}}{Z} = \frac{4.5 \times 10^6}{562500}$$

$$f_r = 8.0 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 8.0 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\boxed{f_r = 8.0 \text{ MPa}}$$

> **Note:** For M20 grade concrete, the expected modulus of rupture per IS 456 is approximately **f_r ≈ 0.7 √f_ck = 0.7 √20 ≈ 3.13 MPa**. Our result of 8 MPa indicates a higher-grade concrete (possibly M30+) or a fibre-reinforced mix.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Properties of Test Materials

| Property | M.S. Girder | Timber Beam | R.C.C. Beam |
|---|---|---|---|
| Failure type | Ductile (yields) | Quasi-brittle | Brittle (after steel yields) |
| E (GPa) | ~200 | 8–13 | 25–30 (concrete alone) |
| Failure load range | 50–150 kN | 4–10 kN | 20–60 kN |
| Load-deflection curve | Linear → yield → plastic plateau → necking | Linear → small plastic → fibre failure | Linear → hairline cracks → steel yields → concrete crushes |
| Standard test | IS 1608 | IS 1708 | IS 516 |
| Specimen size | Standard ISMB section | 50×50×750 mm | 150×150×700 mm |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Universal Testing Machine (UTM) — Functional Block Architecture

The UTM is a hydraulically actuated, servo-controlled loading frame. The following Mermaid diagram represents the **functional architecture** of the bending test setup as installed in a KTU-affiliated Strength of Materials Laboratory.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["Hydraulic Power Pack"] --> node2["Control Valve Assembly"]
    node2 --> node3["Loading Cylinder Actuator"]
    node3 --> node4["Crosshead Moving Platen"]
    node4 --> node5["Loading Nose Point or Roller"]
    node5 --> node6["Specimen Beam Under Test"]
    node6 --> node7["Bottom Support Rollers"]
    node7 --> node8["Fixed Bed Platen"]
    node6 --> node9["Dial Gauge LVDT for Deflection"]
    node4 --> node10["Load Cell Pressure Transducer"]
    node9 --> node11["Digital Data Acquisition System"]
    node10 --> node11
    node11 --> node12["Load Deflection Curve Plotter"]
    node2 --> node13["Manual Control Console"]
    node13 --> node2
```

> **Description of Flow:**
> - The **hydraulic power pack** generates pressurized oil.
> - The **control valve** regulates oil flow to the **loading cylinder**, moving the **crosshead platen** downward.
> - The **loading nose** transmits force to the **beam specimen**, which is supported by **bottom rollers** (simply supported configuration).
> - The **load cell** measures the applied force while the **LVDT/dial gauge** records mid-span deflection.
> - Both signals are sent to the **data acquisition system**, which plots the **load–deflection curve** in real-time.

## 4.2 Schematic of Three-Point vs Four-Point Bending

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the loading configuration comparison.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["Support A"] --- B1["Beam Span L"]
    B1 --- C1["Support B"]
    D1["Single Central Load P"] --> B1

    A2["Support A"] --- B2["Beam Span L"]
    B2 --- C2["Support B"]
    D2["Load P at L/3"] --> E2["Mid Point"]
    F2["Load P at 2L/3"] --> E2
```

> **Top:** 3-point bending — Single load at mid-span. Maximum bending moment occurs only at the centre.
> **Bottom:** 4-point bending — Two equal loads at L/3 and 2L/3. Pure bending (zero shear) exists between the two loads.

## 4.3 Typical Load–Deflection Curves (Conceptual Mermaid Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph M.S._Girder
    direction LR
    s1["Linear Elastic Region"] --> s2["Yield Plateau"]
    s2 --> s3["Strain Hardening"]
    s3 --> s4["Necking and Failure"]
    end

    subgraph Timber_Beam
    direction LR
    t1["Linear Elastic Region"] --> t2["Small Plastic Region"]
    t2 --> t3["Fibre Failure Crack"]
    end

    subgraph R.C.C._Beam
    direction LR
    r1["Uncracked Elastic Region"] --> r2["Cracking Initiation"]
    r2 --> r3["Steel Yielding Phase"]
    r3 --> r4["Concrete Crushing Failure"]
    end
```

> **Key Interpretation:**
> - **M.S. Girder:** The curve shows a long plastic region — the material "warns" before failing.
> - **Timber Beam:** Failure is sudden after a short plastic region — quasi-brittle behaviour.
> - **R.C.C. Beam:** Multiple stages — first crack, then yielding of steel, then crushing of concrete.

## 4.4 Specimen Loading Configuration (ASCII Schematic)

For a 3-point bending test on a rectangular specimen:

```
        |                |
        |    [Dial Gauge]|
        |       |        |
        |       v        |
        |                |
   #####|################|#####
   #####|################|#####
   #####|################|#####
   ^^^^^                ^^^^^
   Support 1     Support 2
   (Pin)         (Roller)

            <--- L = 700 mm --->

                |
                v  P (Load)
           ###############
           |             |
           |  Specimen   |
           |  (Timber)   |
           |             |
           ###############
```

> **Reading:** The arrow indicates the **central point load P** applied downward by the UTM crosshead. The supports provide a **simply supported boundary condition** (one pin, one roller to allow in-plane rotation).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

> **Q:** Define **modulus of rupture**. How is it determined from a 3-point bending test on a timber beam of rectangular cross-section?

**Model Answer:**

The **modulus of rupture (f_r)** is the maximum stress sustained by a material just before it fails in a bending test. It represents the material's flexural strength and is calculated using the **flexure formula** evaluated at the failure load.

For a simply supported rectangular beam of breadth $b$ and depth $d$ subjected to a central point load $P_{max}$ over a span $L$:

$$f_r = \frac{M_{max}}{Z} = \frac{P_{max} L / 4}{b d^2 / 6} = \frac{3 P_{max} L}{2 b d^2}$$

**Procedure:**
1. Place the timber specimen on the UTM supports with span = 700 mm.
2. Apply a gradually increasing central load at a constant rate.
3. Record the **maximum load** $P_{max}$ just before fracture.
4. Substitute into the formula above to obtain $f_r$ in N/mm².

> **Key Point:** For brittle and quasi-brittle materials (timber, concrete), the modulus of rupture is taken as the **flexural strength**, since these materials do not yield.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

> **Q:** Differentiate between **3-point bending** and **4-point bending** tests. In which type of test is pure bending obtained?

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | 3-Point Bending | 4-Point Bending |
|---|---|---|
| Loading | One central load | Two loads at L/3 and 2L/3 |
| Max BM | $PL/4$ at mid-span | $PL/6$ in pure bending zone |
| Shear at max BM | Non-zero | Zero |
| Pure bending region | Only at one point | Between the two load points |
| IS Code preferred | IS 1608, IS 1708 | IS 516 (Concrete) |

> **Pure bending** is obtained in **4-point bending** in the region between the two loading points, where the shear force is zero and bending moment is constant.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]

> **Q (a) [7 Marks]:** A simply supported M.S. I-section girder (ISMB 150) of span 2 m is tested in a UTM under a central point load. The following readings were recorded at the elastic limit:
>
> - Load at elastic limit = 35 kN
> - Deflection at elastic limit = 1.85 mm
>
> The properties of ISMB 150 are: $I_{xx} = 1453.2 \times 10^4$ mm⁴, depth = 150 mm. Calculate the **maximum bending stress** and **Young's modulus** at the elastic limit.

> **Q (b) [7 Marks]:** An R.C.C. beam of size 150 mm × 200 mm × 2000 mm is tested under two-point loading (each at L/3 from support) over a span of 1800 mm. The first crack was observed at a load of 18 kN and the failure occurred at 42 kN. Calculate the **flexural strength (modulus of rupture)** and comment on the **failure mode**.

---

#### Solution to Question A — Part (a)

**Given:**
- Span, $L = 2000$ mm
- Load at elastic limit, $P = 35$ kN = 35000 N
- Deflection, $\Delta = 1.85$ mm
- $I_{xx} = 1453.2 \times 10^4$ mm⁴ = $1.4532 \times 10^7$ mm⁴
- Depth $d = 150$ mm, hence $y_{max} = 75$ mm

**Step 1 — Section modulus:**

$$Z = \frac{I_{xx}}{y_{max}} = \frac{1.4532 \times 10^7}{75} = 193760 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 2 — Maximum bending moment:**

$$M_{max} = \frac{PL}{4} = \frac{35000 \times 2000}{4} = 1.75 \times 10^7 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum bending stress:**

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M_{max}}{Z} = \frac{1.75 \times 10^7}{193760} = 90.31 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{max} \approx 90.3 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Step 4 — Young's modulus:**

$$E = \frac{P L^3}{48 \Delta \cdot I_{xx}} = \frac{35000 \times (2000)^3}{48 \times 1.85 \times 1.4532 \times 10^7}$$

**Numerator:**
$$35000 \times 8 \times 10^9 = 2.8 \times 10^{14}$$

**Denominator:**
$$48 \times 1.85 \times 1.4532 \times 10^7 = 88.8 \times 1.4532 \times 10^7 = 1.2904 \times 10^9$$

**Final calculation:**
$$E = \frac{2.8 \times 10^{14}}{1.2904 \times 10^9} = 2.17 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

$$\boxed{E \approx 217 \text{ GPa}}$$

> **Interpretation:** The result is consistent with mild steel (expected ≈ 200 GPa). The slight excess is within experimental tolerance.

---

#### Solution to Question A — Part (b)

**Given:**
- Beam: $b = 150$ mm, $d = 200$ mm, $L = 1800$ mm
- Failure load (max load on beam) $P = 42$ kN = 42000 N
- Loading: 4-point bending (each at L/3)

**Step 1 — Section modulus:**

$$Z = \frac{b d^2}{6} = \frac{150 \times (200)^2}{6} = \frac{150 \times 40000}{6} = 1.0 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 2 — Maximum bending moment (4-point):**

$$M_{max} = \frac{PL}{6} = \frac{42000 \times 1800}{6} = 1.26 \times 10^7 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 3 — Modulus of rupture:**

$$f_r = \frac{M_{max}}{Z} = \frac{1.26 \times 10^7}{1.0 \times 10^6} = 12.6 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

$$\boxed{f_r = 12.6 \text{ MPa}}$$

> **Comment on Failure Mode:**
> - The **first crack at 18 kN** indicates the **cracking moment** has been exceeded — concrete in the tension zone has fractured.
> - The **load continued to rise from 18 kN to 42 kN** — this is characteristic of an **under-reinforced beam**, where steel reinforcement carries the tension after concrete cracks.
> - At 42 kN, either the **tension steel yields** or the **compression concrete crushes**, leading to failure.
> - The ratio of cracking load to failure load: $18/42 = 0.43$ (between 0.4 and 0.6 — consistent with a **balanced to slightly under-reinforced** section).

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question] — Alternative Choice

> **Q (a) [7 Marks]:** A timber beam of cross-section 80 mm × 120 mm and span 1.5 m is tested in a UTM under a central point load. The load at the limit of proportionality is 6 kN, with corresponding deflection 4.5 mm. The ultimate failure load is 9.5 kN. Determine:
> 1. The **modulus of elasticity** at the proportional limit.
> 2. The **maximum bending stress** at the proportional limit.
> 3. The **modulus of rupture**.

> **Q (b) [7 Marks]:** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **load–deflection behaviour** of an M.S. girder in a bending test. Identify and explain **four key points** on the curve.

---

#### Solution to Question B — Part (a)

**Given:**
- $b = 80$ mm, $d = 120$ mm, $L = 1500$ mm
- $P_{prop} = 6$ kN = 6000 N, $\Delta_{prop} = 4.5$ mm
- $P_{ult} = 9.5$ kN = 9500 N

**Step 1 — Moment of inertia:**

$$I = \frac{b d^3}{12} = \frac{80 \times (120)^3}{12} = \frac{80 \times 1728000}{12} = 1.152 \times 10^7 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 2 — Section modulus:**

$$Z = \frac{b d^2}{6} = \frac{80 \times 14400}{6} = 192000 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 3 — Modulus of elasticity at proportional limit:**

$$E = \frac{P_{prop} L^3}{4 \Delta_{prop} \cdot b d^3} = \frac{6000 \times (1500)^3}{4 \times 4.5 \times 80 \times (120)^3}$$

**Numerator:**
$$6000 \times 3.375 \times 10^9 = 2.025 \times 10^{13}$$

**Denominator:**
$$4 \times 4.5 \times 80 \times 1728000 = 18 \times 80 \times 1728000 = 1440 \times 1728000 = 2.48832 \times 10^9$$

**Final calculation:**
$$E = \frac{2.025 \times 10^{13}}{2.48832 \times 10^9} = 8138 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

$$\boxed{E \approx 8.14 \text{ GPa}}$$

**Step 4 — Maximum bending stress at proportional limit:**

$$M_{prop} = \frac{P_{prop} L}{4} = \frac{6000 \times 1500}{4} = 2.25 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M_{prop}}{Z} = \frac{2.25 \times 10^6}{192000} = 11.72 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{max} \approx 11.72 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Step 5 — Modulus of rupture:**

$$f_r = \frac{3 P_{ult} L}{2 b d^2} = \frac{3 \times 9500 \times 1500}{2 \times 80 \times (120)^2}$$

**Numerator:**
$$3 \times 9500 \times 1500 = 4.275 \times 10^7$$

**Denominator:**
$$2 \times 80 \times 14400 = 2.304 \times 10^6$$

**Final calculation:**
$$f_r = \frac{4.275 \times 10^7}{2.304 \times 10^6} = 18.55 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

$$\boxed{f_r \approx 18.55 \text{ MPa}}$$

> **Interpretation:** The timber is a moderately strong hardwood, suitable for structural beams. The E value of 8.14 GPa places it in the **soft-to-medium hardwood category**.

---

#### Solution to Question B — Part (b)

The load–deflection curve of an M.S. girder has the following four key regions:

1. **Point A — Proportional Limit:**
   The maximum stress at which the load–deflection curve remains a **straight line (Hooke's Law valid)**. Below this point, deflection is directly proportional to load. Beyond this point, the curve deviates from linearity.

2. **Point B — Elastic Limit:**
   The maximum stress up to which the material returns to its original shape upon unloading. Beyond this, **permanent (plastic) deformation** begins.

3. **Point C — Yield Point:**
   A characteristic of mild steel where the material deforms **without any increase in load** (upper and lower yield points). This is the design limit for most steel structures. For Fe 410 grade, this is approximately **250 MPa**.

4. **Point D — Ultimate Point:**
   The **maximum load** the specimen can sustain. Beyond this, **necking** begins, cross-sectional area reduces rapidly, and the curve drops until **fracture (Point E)**.

> **Sketch Reference:** See Section 4.3 — "M.S. Girder" sub-block.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls where KTU students lose marks:**
>
> 1. **Using wrong bending moment formula:** Students frequently use $PL/4$ for 4-point bending tests. The correct value is $PL/6$ for IS 516 concrete testing. **Always re-read the question** to identify the loading type.
>
> 2. **Unit conversion errors:** The most common mistake is forgetting to convert kN to N (multiply by 1000) and mm to m (or vice versa). The formula works in N and mm if stress comes out as N/mm² = MPa.
>
> 3. **Mixing up $b$ and $d$:** For a rectangular section, $b$ is the **breadth (width)** and $d$ is the **depth (height in the bending plane)**. Swapping these will give an incorrect section modulus by a factor of $d/b$ — typically **2× to 3× error** for slender sections.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the overhang in timber specimens:** IS 1708 specifies an overhang of 25 mm at each end (specimen length 750 mm, span 700 mm). Students often use the specimen length as span, which is wrong.
>
> 5. **Reporting modulus of rupture for steel:** M.S. is ductile — it does **not** have a true "modulus of rupture." The yield stress is the design value. Using $f_r$ for M.S. loses marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Bending Test on Beams**

- **Bending stress formula (rectangular, 3-point):** $\sigma = \dfrac{3PL}{2bd^2}$
- **Bending stress formula (I-section):** $\sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot y_{max}}{I_{xx}}$
- **Modulus of rupture (rectangular):** $f_r = \dfrac{3 P_{max} L}{2 b d^2}$
- **Section modulus (rectangular):** $Z = \dfrac{b d^2}{6}$
- **Modulus of elasticity (3-point):** $E = \dfrac{P L^3}{4 \Delta b d^3}$
- **4-point bending moment:** $M = PL/6$ (NOT $PL/4$)
- **IS codes:** IS 1608 (steel), IS 1708 (timber), IS 516 (concrete)
- **Standard specimen sizes:**
  - Timber: 50×50×750 mm, span 700 mm
  - R.C.C.: 150×150×700 mm, span 600 mm
- **Young's Modulus reference values:** Steel ≈ 200 GPa, Timber ≈ 8–13 GPa, Concrete ≈ 25–30 GPa
- **Failure types:** Steel = ductile (yields), Timber = quasi-brittle, R.C.C. = crack → yield → crush
- **KTU Examiner's favourite question:** "Calculate the modulus of elasticity from the load–deflection data" — always carry the formula $E = PL^3/(4\Delta bd^3)$ for 3-point bending.
- **Always state units clearly** — MPa or N/mm² for stress, GPa or N/mm² for Young's modulus.
- **Show all intermediate steps** — KTU valuation gives step marks for $Z$, $M$, and final answer separately.

---

**End of Notes — Module Experiment 2 | Bending Test on Beams in UTM**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Ultimate Shear Strength of Mild Steel by Double Shear Test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGL308 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Ultimate Shear Strength of Mild Steel by Double Shear Test

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Double Shear Test** is a standard destructive laboratory experiment conducted on a mild steel specimen to determine its **Ultimate Shear Strength (USS)** — the maximum shear stress a material can withstand before undergoing shear failure. In this test, the specimen is loaded such that it is sheared along **two parallel cross-sectional planes simultaneously**, thereby distributing the applied load across two shear areas.

Mathematically, the Ultimate Shear Strength is defined as:

$$\tau_u = \frac{P_{max}}{2A}$$

where:
- $\tau_u$ = Ultimate Shear Strength (in **N/mm²** or **MPa**)
- $P_{max}$ = Maximum (failure) load applied during the test (in **N**)
- $A$ = Cross-sectional area of one shear plane (in **mm²**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (CO Alignment):**
> This experiment aligns with **Course Outcome CO2**: *"Apply the concepts of stress, strain, and deformation of solids for engineering applications."* It also reinforces **CO4** (Experimental verification of material properties) and the practical competencies expected under the NEP 2020 framework of *Experiential Learning*.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are trying to cut a thin wooden stick using a pair of **scissors**. A single blade of the scissors cuts the stick at one location — that is **single shear**. Now imagine a **paper guillotine** with two blades cutting from both sides at the same instant. The stick breaks along **two cut lines simultaneously** — that is **double shear**.

Similarly, in the laboratory test:
- The mild steel specimen is placed on **two bottom supports**.
- A **central loading pin (punch)** pushes downward on the specimen's mid-section.
- The reaction forces at the two supports push **upward**.
- This creates **two opposing shear planes**, each located between the central punch and one of the supports.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Double" Shear?**
> In single shear, only **one** cross-section resists the load. In double shear, **two** cross-sections share the load — so for the same load, the shear stress developed is **half** that of single shear. This makes the specimen **stronger in double shear**, which is why bolt connections, riveted joints, and structural pins are often designed in double shear.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Test Parameters

The following are the standard reference values used during the experiment:

| Parameter | Typical Range / Value |
|---|---|
| Mild Steel Diameter ($d$) | **10 mm to 25 mm** (commonly 16 mm or 20 mm) |
| Loading Rate (Universal Testing Machine) | **≤ 10 N/mm²/s** (per IS 5242) |
| Expected $\tau_u$ for Mild Steel | **220 – 370 MPa** (typically ~300 MPa) |
| Testing Machine Capacity | **100 kN – 1000 kN UTM** |
| Specimen Material | Mild Steel (per IS 2062 / IS 432 grade specifications) |

> [!NOTE]
> Always confirm the **actual specimen diameter** using a vernier caliper or micrometer at a minimum of **three locations**, and take the **mean value** as per KTU laboratory standards.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Background — The Shear Failure Mechanism

When a cylindrical specimen is subjected to a transverse load at its center while resting on two symmetric supports, the following sequence of events occurs:

1. **Elastic Phase**: The specimen deforms elastically. Internal shear stresses develop along two imaginary vertical planes (between the central load and each support). These stresses remain below the proportional limit.

2. **Yielding Phase**: As the load increases, the material at the shear planes begins to yield. Plastic deformation becomes visible as a slight elongation or "necking" near the shear zones.

3. **Ultimate Phase**: The load reaches its **peak value ($P_{max}$)**. At this instant, the material is at its maximum capacity to resist shear.

4. **Failure Phase**: A sudden **shear fracture** occurs along the two planes. The specimen splits into **three distinct pieces** — a clear visual signature of double shear failure.

### 2.2 Why Two Shear Planes?

In the loading configuration:
- Let the central downward force be $P$ (applied via the punch).
- Each support reacts with an upward force of $P/2$ (by static equilibrium, since the system is symmetric).
- Between the punch and the **left support**: one shear plane carries $P/2$.
- Between the punch and the **right support**: another shear plane carries $P/2$.
- Total shear resistance offered = $P/2 + P/2 = P$ (distributed over **two** planes).

This is why the formula uses the factor **2** in the denominator.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 'Why' Behind the Formula:**
> The shear stress is shear force per unit shear area. In double shear, the *total shear force* resisting the load is $P$, but it is distributed across **two** identical cross-sectional areas. Each area experiences only $P/2$. Hence, the **shear stress on one plane** = $(P/2) / A = P / (2A)$.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}$ | Cross-sectional area of circular specimen | mm² |
| 2 | $P_{total} = 2 \times P_{per\,plane}$ | Load distribution in double shear | N |
| 3 | $\tau_u = \dfrac{P_{max}}{2A}$ | Ultimate Shear Strength | N/mm² (MPa) |
| 4 | $\tau_u = \dfrac{2P_{max}}{\pi d^2}$ | USS (substituting circular area) | MPa |
| 5 | $\tau_u = \dfrac{P_{max}}{2 \times \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}}$ | Expanded form | MPa |
| 6 | $\tau_{average} = \dfrac{P_{max}}{2A_{mean}}$ | Using mean diameter | MPa |

> [!NOTE]
> Always express the final answer in **MPa** (N/mm²). Multiply by $10^{-3}$ if load is in kN and area is in mm²:
> $\tau (\text{MPa}) = \dfrac{P_{max} (\text{kN}) \times 10^3}{2 \times A (\text{mm}^2)}$

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The double shear test is **not merely academic** — its results directly feed into the design of:

- **Riveted and bolted joints** in steel structures (bridges, transmission towers, aircraft fuselages)
- **Pin connections** in heavy machinery and linkages
- **Knuckle joints** in mechanical assemblies
- **Shear connectors** in composite construction (steel-concrete)
- **Design of fasteners** as per IS 800, IS 816, and AISC specifications

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In design practice, engineers use a **Factor of Safety (FoS)** — typically **2 to 4** for shear — to derive the **Permissible Shear Stress** from the Ultimate Shear Strength obtained experimentally:
> $\tau_{permissible} = \dfrac{\tau_u}{\text{FoS}}$
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Double Shear Formula

#### Step 1: Free-Body Analysis of the Specimen

Consider a cylindrical mild steel specimen of diameter $d$ placed on two supports separated by a distance $L$. A central downward force $P$ is applied at the mid-span through a punch.

By **static equilibrium** (taking symmetry into account):

$$R_1 + R_2 = P$$

Since the loading is symmetric about the central punch:

$$R_1 = R_2 = \dfrac{P}{2}$$

where $R_1$ and $R_2$ are the reaction forces at the left and right supports respectively.

---

#### Step 2: Identify the Shear Planes

Two shear planes are formed:
- **Shear Plane 1**: Located between the central punch and the left support.
- **Shear Plane 2**: Located between the central punch and the right support.

Each plane has a cross-sectional area $A$ equal to the cross-section of the specimen:

$$A = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}$$

---

#### Step 3: Compute the Shear Force at Each Plane

By the method of sections, the shear force acting on each plane equals the reaction force:

$$V_1 = R_1 = \dfrac{P}{2}$$

$$V_2 = R_2 = \dfrac{P}{2}$$

---

#### Step 4: Apply the Shear Stress Definition

By definition, **shear stress** is the shear force per unit area:

$$\tau = \dfrac{V}{A}$$

Substituting the values for one shear plane:

$$\tau_u = \dfrac{V_1}{A} = \dfrac{P/2}{A}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_u = \dfrac{P}{2A}}$$

---

#### Step 5: Substitute the Area of a Circular Cross-Section

Since the specimen is circular:

$$A = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}$$

Therefore:

$$\tau_u = \dfrac{P_{max}}{2 \times \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}}$$

$$\tau_u = \dfrac{P_{max} \times 4}{2 \pi d^2}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_u = \dfrac{2 P_{max}}{\pi d^2}}$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** In a double shear test on a mild steel specimen of diameter **$d = 20$ mm**, the specimen failed at a maximum load of **$P_{max} = 188.5$ kN**. Determine the Ultimate Shear Strength of the material.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Convert units to standard SI**

$$d = 20 \text{ mm} = 0.020 \text{ m}$$

$$P_{max} = 188.5 \text{ kN} = 188500 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the cross-sectional area**

$$A = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times (20)^2}{4}$$

$$A = \dfrac{\pi \times 400}{4} = 100\pi$$

$$A = 314.159 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 3: Apply the double shear formula**

$$\tau_u = \dfrac{P_{max}}{2A}$$

$$\tau_u = \dfrac{188500}{2 \times 314.159}$$

$$\tau_u = \dfrac{188500}{628.318}$$

$$\tau_u = 300.0 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 300 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4: Cross-verification using the simplified formula**

$$\tau_u = \dfrac{2P_{max}}{\pi d^2}$$

$$\tau_u = \dfrac{2 \times 188500}{\pi \times (20)^2}$$

$$\tau_u = \dfrac{377000}{\pi \times 400}$$

$$\tau_u = \dfrac{377000}{1256.637}$$

$$\tau_u = 300.0 \text{ MPa}$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{\tau_u = 300 \text{ MPa} \text{ (Ultimate Shear Strength of Mild Steel)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This value of **300 MPa** falls within the standard range for mild steel (220–370 MPa), confirming experimental accuracy.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Single Shear vs. Double Shear

| Parameter | Single Shear Test | Double Shear Test |
|---|---|---|
| Number of shear planes | 1 | 2 |
| Shear force per plane | $P$ | $P/2$ |
| Shear area per plane | $A$ | $A$ |
| Shear stress formula | $\tau = P/A$ | $\tau = P/(2A)$ |
| Specimen pieces after failure | 2 | 3 |
| Load capacity for same $\tau_u$ | Lower | Higher (doubles) |
| Practical applications | Lap joints, simple pins | Riveted joints, knuckle pins |
| Typical setup | Overhang with one shear span | Central load with two symmetric shear spans |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A1 | CO2 | Bloom Level: Remember]**
> **Q1.** Define **Ultimate Shear Strength** of a material. State its SI unit.
>
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> The Ultimate Shear Strength ($\tau_u$) is defined as the **maximum shear stress** that a material can withstand before it fails by shear fracture. It is computed as the ratio of the **maximum (failure) load** to the **resisting shear area**.
>
> $$\tau_u = \dfrac{P_{max}}{A_{shear}}$$
>
> **SI Unit:** N/mm² (MPa) or Pa (N/m²).

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A2 | CO2 | Bloom Level: Understand]**
> **Q2.** Why is a **double shear test** preferred over a single shear test in determining the shear strength of ductile materials like mild steel?
>
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> 1. In double shear, the load is distributed across **two shear planes**, producing a more **uniform and stable stress state** (closer to pure shear).
> 2. It eliminates the **bending moment** that develops in single shear (overhang-type) tests, leading to **more accurate** shear strength values.
> 3. The failure is **clean and reproducible**, with the specimen splitting into three well-defined pieces, making the results easier to interpret and align with design codes.

---

### 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Module Internal Choice — Select Either Question A or Question B**

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper Pattern | CO2, CO4 | Bloom Level: Apply/Analyze]*

**(a)** A mild steel specimen of diameter **$d = 18$ mm** is tested in a double shear apparatus. The specimen failed at a maximum load of **$P_{max} = 152.8$ kN**. Determine:
   1. The Ultimate Shear Strength of the material. **(7 Marks)**
   2. The **Factor of Safety** if the permissible shear stress in design is taken as **80 MPa**. **(3 Marks)**
   3. State **two** reasons why the failed specimen splits into three pieces instead of two. **(4 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a neat **free-body diagram**, the loading arrangement in a double shear test. Derive the formula for Ultimate Shear Strength, clearly indicating the **two shear planes**. **(7 Marks)**

---

### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION A

#### Part (a)(i): Ultimate Shear Strength (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Diameter, $d = 18$ mm
- Maximum load, $P_{max} = 152.8$ kN $= 152800$ N

**Step 1: Compute the cross-sectional area**

$$A = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times (18)^2}{4}$$

$$A = \dfrac{\pi \times 324}{4} = 81\pi$$

$$A = 254.469 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2: Apply the double shear formula**

$$\tau_u = \dfrac{P_{max}}{2A} = \dfrac{152800}{2 \times 254.469}$$

$$\tau_u = \dfrac{152800}{508.938}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_u = 300.24 \text{ MPa} \approx 300 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Marks Distribution:**
- Correct area formula & substitution: **2 Marks**
- Correct application of double shear (factor of 2): **3 Marks**
- Final numerical answer with units: **2 Marks**

---

#### Part (a)(ii): Factor of Safety (3 Marks)

$$\text{FoS} = \dfrac{\tau_u}{\tau_{permissible}} = \dfrac{300}{80}$$

$$\boxed{\text{FoS} = 3.75}$$

---

#### Part (a)(iii): Two Reasons for Three-Piece Failure (4 Marks)

1. **Two shear planes act simultaneously** — between the central punch and each support, the material shears along two distinct vertical planes, producing three segments. **(2 Marks)**
2. **Symmetric loading geometry** — the centrally applied load combined with two reactions ensures that the shear stresses on both sides are equal in magnitude, causing **simultaneous** failure on both planes rather than sequential failure. **(2 Marks)**

---

#### Part (b): Free-Body Diagram & Derivation (7 Marks)

**Free-Body Diagram (Text Representation):**

```
          P (downward)
          ↓
   ┌──────●──────┐
   │   [Specimen] │
   │             │
   └──────┬──────┘
       R1↑   ↑R2
```

Where:
- $P$ = Central applied load
- $R_1$ = Left support reaction $= P/2$
- $R_2$ = Right support reaction $= P/2$

**Derivation Steps (4 Marks for derivation + 3 Marks for FBD and explanation):**

**Step 1:** By symmetry, $R_1 = R_2 = P/2$.

**Step 2:** Two shear planes are formed. Shear force at each plane equals the reaction: $V = P/2$.

**Step 3:** Area of each plane: $A = \pi d^2 / 4$.

**Step 4:** Shear stress:

$$\tau_u = \dfrac{V}{A} = \dfrac{P/2}{\pi d^2/4} = \dfrac{2P}{\pi d^2}$$

**Step 5:** Substituting $P = P_{max}$ at failure gives the final Ultimate Shear Strength.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice | CO2, CO4 | Bloom Level: Analyze/Evaluate*

**(a)** With the help of a **schematic diagram**, describe the **double shear test apparatus** and the **step-by-step experimental procedure** used to determine the ultimate shear strength of mild steel. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** In a double shear test on a mild steel pin of diameter **$d = 25$ mm**, the maximum load recorded before failure was **$P_{max} = 295$ kN**. Calculate:
   1. The Ultimate Shear Strength of the pin material. **(4 Marks)**
   2. If the same pin were tested in **single shear**, what would be the load at which it would fail, assuming the same $\tau_u$? **(3 Marks)**

---

### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION B

#### Part (a): Apparatus Description & Procedure (7 Marks)

**Apparatus (3 Marks):**
The double shear test apparatus consists of:
- A **central hardened steel punch** that applies the downward load.
- **Two hardened support blocks** placed symmetrically below the specimen.
- The mild steel specimen rests across these supports, with the punch aligned to its center.
- The entire assembly is placed in a **Universal Testing Machine (UTM)** with a load indicator.

**Step-by-Step Procedure (4 Marks):**

1. **Measure the specimen diameter** at three different locations using a vernier caliper or micrometer. Compute the **mean diameter** $d_{mean}$. **(1 Mark)**
2. **Place the specimen** in the shear test fixture, ensuring proper alignment with both supports and the central punch. **(1 Mark)**
3. **Position the fixture** in the UTM and **zero** the load indicator. **(0.5 Mark)**
4. **Apply the load gradually** at a uniform rate (≤ 10 MPa/s) until the specimen fails. **(1 Mark)**
5. **Record the maximum load** $P_{max}$ at the instant of failure and observe the failure pattern (three pieces). **(0.5 Mark)**

---

#### Part (b)(i): Ultimate Shear Strength (4 Marks)

**Given:** $d = 25$ mm, $P_{max} = 295$ kN $= 295000$ N

$$A = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times (25)^2}{4} = \dfrac{625\pi}{4} = 490.874 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$\tau_u = \dfrac{P_{max}}{2A} = \dfrac{295000}{2 \times 490.874} = \dfrac{295000}{981.748}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_u = 300.47 \text{ MPa} \approx 300 \text{ MPa}}$$

---

#### Part (b)(ii): Equivalent Single Shear Failure Load (3 Marks)

In single shear, the failure load $P_{single}$ occurs when:

$$\tau_u = \dfrac{P_{single}}{A}$$

$$P_{single} = \tau_u \times A = 300 \times 490.874$$

$$P_{single} = 147262 \text{ N} \approx 147.26 \text{ kN}$$

**Observation:** The single shear failure load (147.26 kN) is **half** the double shear failure load (295 kN), confirming the theoretical expectation.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of "2"** in the double shear formula — this alone causes a **50% error** in the result and typically costs **4–5 marks** out of 7 in a sub-question. *(Always verify: "double shear → divide by 2A")*
>
> 2. **Unit conversion errors** — forgetting to convert kN → N (multiply by $10^3$) before plugging into the formula. Result is **1000× too small**. *(Loss: 2 marks)*
>
> 3. **Using the wrong area formula** — using $\pi d$ or $\pi r^2$ instead of $\pi d^2/4$. *(Loss: 2 marks)*
>
> 4. **Not reporting the unit** — a numerically correct answer without "MPa" or "N/mm²" loses a **mandatory 1 mark** per KTU valuation norms.
>
> 5. **Confusing single shear and double shear** — describing the failure as "two pieces" instead of "three pieces" indicates a conceptual gap. *(Loss: 1–2 marks)*
>
> 6. **Omitting the free-body diagram** in derivations — KTU examiners award **at least 2 marks** specifically for the FBD; its absence is a heavy penalty.

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The **Double Shear Test** determines the **Ultimate Shear Strength (USS)** of mild steel by shearing the specimen along **two simultaneous shear planes**.
- ✅ Core formula: $\tau_u = \dfrac{P_{max}}{2A} = \dfrac{2 P_{max}}{\pi d^2}$ (for circular specimens).
- ✅ Always measure the **mean diameter** at multiple points to ensure accuracy.
- ✅ **Three pieces** after failure = visual confirmation of double shear; **two pieces** = single shear.
- ✅ Typical USS of mild steel: **220–370 MPa** (commonly ~300 MPa).
- ✅ **Factor of Safety** in design = $\tau_u / \tau_{permissible}$ (typically 2–4).
- ✅ Static equilibrium gives: each reaction = $P/2$, hence the shear force per plane = $P/2$.
- ✅ Real-world applications: riveted joints, pin connections, knuckle joints, bolted connections.
- ✅ SI Unit of $\tau_u$ is **MPa (N/mm²)** — always include the unit in your final answer.
- ✅ Per IS 5242, the loading rate should not exceed **10 N/mm² per second** for shear tests.
- ✅ The test is mapped to KTU **CO2** (stress concepts) and **CO4** (experimental validation).
- ✅ Examiner's golden rule: **write the formula first, then substitute, then compute** — never jump to the answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Modulus of Elasticity of Beam Using Maxwell's Reciprocal Theorem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGL308 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Modulus of Elasticity of Beam Using Maxwell's Reciprocal Theorem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Maxwell's Reciprocal Theorem** (also called **Maxwell-Betti's Reciprocal Theorem of Deflections**) is a fundamental principle in the theory of structures that states:

> *"In a linearly elastic structure, the deflection at point A due to a unit load applied at point B is equal in magnitude to the deflection at point B due to a unit load applied at point A."*

Mathematically, for two points $i$ and $j$ on a linear elastic body:

$$\delta_{ij} = \delta_{ji}$$

Where:
- $\delta_{ij}$ = deflection at point $i$ due to a unit load applied at point $j$
- $\delta_{ji}$ = deflection at point $j$ due to a unit load applied at point $i$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Pre-requisite Conditions for Validity**
> 1. The structure must be **linearly elastic** (obeys Hooke's Law).
> 2. The material must be homogeneous and isotropic.
> 3. The deflections must be **small** compared to the structural dimensions.
> 4. The principle of **superposition** must hold.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!TIP]
> **The Spring-Bridge Analogy**
> Imagine two identical rubber bands (Band-A and Band-B) stretched between the same two fixed posts. If you pluck Band-A with your finger, it vibrates. Now if you pluck Band-B with the same force, the vibration pattern is identical. The "energy transfer" is reciprocal — what Band-A does to Band-B, Band-B does to Band-A in the same way.
>
> Similarly, in a beam, if pushing at point C makes point Q deflect by 1 mm, then pushing at point Q (with the same force) will make point C deflect by exactly 1 mm. **The beam does not "remember" where the force came from — it only knows the geometry and stiffness.**

A more physical analogy is a **simply supported plank on two logs** in a river. If you stand at the middle, the plank bends — the same bend is observed whether you stand in the middle or at the quarter-span when measured appropriately.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is this experiment important for KTU?**
> This experiment provides an **indirect method** to verify the linearity of elastic response. It also allows determination of Young's Modulus ($E$) using **deflection measurements at any convenient point** (not necessarily the load point), making the laboratory setup more flexible and the measurements more accurate.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value/Unit |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Young's Modulus of Mild Steel | $E$ | **2.0 × 10⁵ N/mm²** (≈ 200 GPa) |
| Young's Modulus of Cast Iron | $E$ | **1.0 – 1.5 × 10⁵ N/mm²** |
| Young's Modulus of Aluminium | $E$ | **0.7 × 10⁵ N/mm²** |
| Least Count of Dial Gauge (typical) | LC | **0.01 mm** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Conceptual Logic of the Theorem (Why and How)

The theorem rests on **Betti's Law of Reciprocal Deflections** (1872) and can be intuitively justified using the **principle of virtual work** or **Castigliano's Theorem**. The logical steps are:

- **Step 1 — Superposition Validity**: For linear elastic systems, the total work done by external forces equals the total strain energy stored.

$$U = W_{external} = \frac{1}{2}\sum P_i \delta_i$$

- **Step 2 — Energy Equality**: If load $P_1$ is applied at A producing deflection $\delta_1$ at A, and later load $P_2$ is applied at B producing deflection $\delta_2$ at B, the work done is path-independent.

- **Step 3 — Symmetry of Influence Coefficients**: The flexibility (influence) coefficient between any two points is symmetric. This is the mathematical essence of Maxwell's theorem.

- **Step 4 — Practical Application in Beam Testing**: By measuring deflections at two specific points (C and Q) under loads at the same two points (C and Q), we can experimentally validate the theorem and use the measured values to back-calculate $E$.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas required for KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Examination**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Maxwell's Reciprocal Theorem | $\delta_{ij} = \delta_{ji}$ | mm or m | Symmetric influence |
| 2 | Max. deflection of SSB (central point load) | $y_{max} = \dfrac{PL^3}{48EI}$ | mm | Occurs at mid-span |
| 3 | Deflection at quarter-span (load at center) | $y_{Q} = \dfrac{11PL^3}{768EI}$ | mm | At $x = L/4$ |
| 4 | Deflection at center (load at quarter-span) | $y_{C} = \dfrac{11PL^3}{768EI}$ | mm | By Maxwell's theorem |
| 5 | Moment of Inertia (Rectangular) | $I = \dfrac{bd^3}{12}$ | mm⁴ | $b$ = width, $d$ = depth |
| 6 | Young's Modulus (from deflection) | $E = \dfrac{11PL^3}{768 I \, y}$ | N/mm² | Primary working formula |
| 7 | Section Modulus | $Z = \dfrac{bd^2}{6}$ | mm³ | For bending stress |
| 8 | Reactions for SSB | $R_A = \dfrac{Pb}{L}, \; R_B = \dfrac{Pa}{L}$ | N | $a$ and $b$ from supports |
| 9 | Bending Equation | $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$ | — | Flexure formula |
| 10 | Strain Energy (Point Load) | $U = \dfrac{1}{2}P\delta = \dfrac{P^2 L^3}{96EI}$ | N·mm | For central loading |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Substitution Rule:** For rectangular beams, always substitute $I = bd^3/12$ into the deflection formula before computing $E$. A common error is forgetting the cube on $d$.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Utility of Maxwell's Theorem |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Bridge Design** | Determining deflections at multiple points using a single influence line |
| **Aircraft Structures** | Validating linearity in composite wing testing |
| **Machine Tool Beds** | Verifying stiffness symmetry under different loading configurations |
| **Structural Health Monitoring** | Cross-verification of Finite Element Analysis (FEA) results |
| **Civil Engineering Lab** | Quick determination of $E$ without strain gauges (uses only dial gauges) |

---

### 2.4 General Deflection Equations for a Simply Supported Beam (Point Load $P$ at distance $a$ from left support, $b = L - a$)

For 0 ≤ x ≤ a (left of load):

$$y = \frac{Pbx}{6LEI}\left(L^2 - b^2 - x^2\right)$$

For a ≤ x ≤ L (right of load):

$$y = \frac{Pa(L-x)}{6LEI}\left(2Lx - a^2 - x^2\right)$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU examiners often give a load applied at a **non-symmetric point** (not at the center). Master the two-part formula above — it covers all such cases.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Deflection at Quarter-Point due to Load at Center

**Given:**
- Simply Supported Beam (SSB) of span $L$
- Point load $P$ at center (so $a = b = L/2$)
- Find deflection at $x = L/4$

**Step 1: Identify the segment.**

Since $x = L/4 < a = L/2$, we use the left-segment formula:

$$y = \frac{Pbx}{6LEI}\left(L^2 - b^2 - x^2\right)$$

**Step 2: Substitute $b = L/2$ and $x = L/4$.**

The factor $\frac{Pb}{6L}$ becomes:

$$\frac{P \cdot (L/2)}{6L} = \frac{P}{12}$$

So the prefactor is $\frac{P}{12EI} \cdot x = \frac{P}{12EI} \cdot \frac{L}{4} = \frac{PL}{48EI}$.

**Step 3: Evaluate the bracket $\left(L^2 - b^2 - x^2\right)$.**

$$\left(L^2 - \left(\frac{L}{2}\right)^2 - \left(\frac{L}{4}\right)^2\right) = \left(L^2 - \frac{L^2}{4} - \frac{L^2}{16}\right)$$

Taking LCM as 16:

$$= \frac{16L^2 - 4L^2 - L^2}{16} = \frac{11L^2}{16}$$

**Step 4: Combine.**

$$y_{Q} = \frac{PL}{48EI} \cdot \frac{11L^2}{16} = \frac{11PL^3}{768EI}$$

Therefore, the deflection at the quarter-span due to a central load is:

$$\boxed{y_{Q} = \frac{11PL^3}{768EI}}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Deflection at Center due to Load at Quarter-Point

**Given:**
- SSB of span $L$, point load $P$ at $a = L/4$ (so $b = 3L/4$)
- Find deflection at $x = L/2$

**Step 1: Identify the segment.**

Since $x = L/2 > a = L/4$, we use the right-segment formula:

$$y = \frac{Pa(L-x)}{6LEI}\left(2Lx - a^2 - x^2\right)$$

**Step 2: Substitute values.**

The prefactor $\frac{Pa(L-x)}{6L}$ becomes:

$$\frac{P \cdot (L/4) \cdot (L/2)}{6L} = \frac{PL^2}{48L} = \frac{PL}{48}$$

So combined with $EI$:

$$\frac{PL}{48EI}$$

**Step 3: Evaluate the bracket $\left(2Lx - a^2 - x^2\right)$.**

$$\left(2L \cdot \frac{L}{2} - \left(\frac{L}{4}\right)^2 - \left(\frac{L}{2}\right)^2\right) = \left(L^2 - \frac{L^2}{16} - \frac{L^2}{4}\right)$$

Taking LCM as 16:

$$= \frac{16L^2 - L^2 - 4L^2}{16} = \frac{11L^2}{16}$$

**Step 4: Combine.**

$$y_{C} = \frac{PL}{48EI} \cdot \frac{11L^2}{16} = \frac{11PL^3}{768EI}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{y_{C} = \frac{11PL^3}{768EI}}$$

---

### 3.3 Verification of Maxwell's Reciprocal Theorem

By direct comparison of Derivation 1 and Derivation 2:

$$y_{Q} \text{ (due to load at C)} = y_{C} \text{ (due to load at Q)} = \frac{11PL^3}{768EI}$$

This proves:

$$\delta_{QC} = \delta_{CQ}$$

**Hence, Maxwell's Reciprocal Theorem is mathematically and experimentally verified.**

---

### 3.4 Working Formula for Young's Modulus

Rearranging the deflection equation:

$$E = \frac{11PL^3}{768 \cdot I \cdot y}$$

For a rectangular cross-section ($I = bd^3/12$):

$$E = \frac{11PL^3 \cdot 12}{768 \cdot bd^3 \cdot y} = \frac{11PL^3}{64 \cdot bd^3 \cdot y}$$

Simplification (since $768/12 = 64$):

$$\boxed{E = \frac{11 P L^3}{64 \, b \, d^3 \, y} \quad \text{N/mm}^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Unit Consistency Check:**
> $P$ in N, $L$, $b$, $d$, $y$ all in mm → $E$ in N/mm² = MPa.
> If $P$ is in kgf, multiply by $g = 9810$ mm/s² to convert to N.

---

### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU-style Problem)

**Problem Statement:**
A simply supported mild steel beam of span $L = 1500$ mm and rectangular cross-section $b = 40$ mm, $d = 20$ mm is loaded at the center with $P = 200$ N. The measured deflection at the quarter-span is $y_Q = 0.82$ mm. Determine the Young's Modulus and verify the theorem.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute the Moment of Inertia.**

$$I = \frac{bd^3}{12} = \frac{40 \times (20)^3}{12} = \frac{40 \times 8000}{12} = 26666.67 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 2: Apply the deflection formula.**

$$y_Q = \frac{11PL^3}{768EI}$$

**Step 3: Solve for $E$.**

$$E = \frac{11PL^3}{768 \cdot I \cdot y_Q} = \frac{11 \times 200 \times (1500)^3}{768 \times 26666.67 \times 0.82}$$

Numerator:

$$11 \times 200 \times 3.375 \times 10^9 = 7.425 \times 10^{12}$$

Denominator:

$$768 \times 26666.67 \times 0.82 = 16,790,400 \approx 1.679 \times 10^7$$

Therefore:

$$E = \frac{7.425 \times 10^{12}}{1.679 \times 10^7} = 2.22 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 4: Compare with standard value.**

Standard $E$ for mild steel $\approx 2.0 \times 10^5$ N/mm².

Percentage error:

$$\%\text{Error} = \frac{|2.22 - 2.0| \times 10^5}{2.0 \times 10^5} \times 100 = 11\%$$

The deviation is within acceptable laboratory tolerance (5–15% is typical in KTU labs).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
