```mermaid
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Set dial gauge to zero]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Apply incremental loads W1, W2, W3...]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Record corresponding deflections delta1, delta2, delta3...]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Tabulate W vs delta observations]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Plot W on Y axis vs delta on X axis]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9: Determine slope = Experimental k_exp]
    S9 --> S10[Step 10: Calculate theoretical k_th from formula]
    S10 --> S11[Step 11: Compute percentage error]
```

## 4.3 Free-Body Diagram of Wire Cross-Section (Torsional + Direct Shear)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Wire [Wire Cross-Section Diameter d]
        O[Center of Wire]
        I[Inner Fiber Maximum Shear]
        O --> I
    end
    I -- Torque T = W times D/2 --> Shear1[Torsional Shear tau1]
    I -- Direct Force W --> Shear2[Direct Shear tau2]
    Shear1 --> Total[tau_max = tau1 plus tau2]
    Shear2 --> Total
```

## 4.4 Spring Geometry Schematic (Text-Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Spring [Open Coiled Compression Spring Geometry]
        d[Wire Diameter d mm]
        D[Mean Coil Diameter D mm]
        N[Number of Active Coils N]
        L0[Free Length L0 mm]
        alpha[Helix Angle alpha degrees]
        d --> Spring
        D --> Spring
        N --> Spring
        L0 --> Spring
        alpha --> Spring
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **helix angle** $\alpha = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{p}{\pi D}\right)$, where $p$ is the pitch of the coil. For open coiled springs, $\alpha$ is typically **12° to 15°**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define an open coiled helical spring. How is it different from a closed coiled spring?**

**Model Answer:**

An **open coiled helical spring** is one in which there is a clear gap between the consecutive coils when the spring is in the free (unloaded) condition. The coils do not touch each other and do not exert any initial thrust on one another.

| Feature | Open Coiled | Closed Coiled |
|---------|-------------|---------------|
| Coil gap at rest | Yes (visible) | No (touching) |
| Initial tension | May or may not be present | Negligible |
| Helix angle $\alpha$ | Larger ($> 10°$) | Small ($< 10°$) |
| Standard formula | $\tau = \dfrac{8WD}{\pi d^{3}} + \dfrac{4W}{\pi d^{2}}$ | Same (with $P_i = 0$) |

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the formula for the stiffness of a helical spring and explain the significance of the spring index.**

**Model Answer:**

The stiffness (spring rate) is:

$$k = \frac{G d^{4}}{8 D^{3} N} \quad \text{(N/mm)}$$

**Spring index** $C = D/d$ is the ratio of mean coil diameter to wire diameter.

- If $C < 4$: spring is difficult to wind and has high stresses.
- If $C > 12$: spring is too soft and may buckle under compression.
- **Recommended range:** $4 \le C \le 12$.

A higher $C$ gives a softer spring (lower $k$); a lower $C$ gives a stiffer spring.

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** A helical compression spring has wire diameter $d = 5$ mm, mean coil diameter $D = 50$ mm, and $N = 12$ active coils. The material has $G = 80$ GPa. Find:
1. The spring stiffness $k$.
2. The maximum load the spring can carry if the permissible shear stress is $350$ MPa. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the procedure to conduct the **load-deflection test** on an open coiled compression spring using a spring testing machine. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Solution to Question A:**

#### Part (a) Solution:

**Given:** $d = 5$ mm, $D = 50$ mm, $N = 12$, $G = 80 \times 10^{3}$ MPa, $\tau_{permissible} = 350$ MPa.

**1) Spring stiffness $k$:**

$$k = \frac{G d^{4}}{8 D^{3} N} = \frac{80{,}000 \times 5^{4}}{8 \times 50^{3} \times 12}$$

$$k = \frac{80{,}000 \times 625}{8 \times 125{,}000 \times 12} = \frac{5 \times 10^{7}}{1.2 \times 10^{7}}$$

$$\boxed{k = 4.167 \text{ N/mm}}$$

**2) Maximum load $W_{max}$:**

Using $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{8WD}{\pi d^{3}}$ (ignoring direct shear for initial estimate):

$$W_{max} = \frac{\tau_{per} \cdot \pi d^{3}}{8 D} = \frac{350 \times \pi \times 125}{8 \times 50} = \frac{137{,}445.3}{400}$$

$$W_{max} = 343.6 \text{ N}$$

**Including direct shear correction (more accurate):**

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{8WD}{\pi d^{3}} + \frac{4W}{\pi d^{2}}$$

$$350 = \frac{8W \times 50}{\pi \times 125} + \frac{4W}{\pi \times 25} = 1.0186 W + 0.0509 W = 1.0695 W$$

$$\boxed{W_{max} = 327.3 \text{ N}}$$

**Corresponding deflection:**

$$\delta_{max} = \frac{W_{max}}{k} = \frac{327.3}{4.167} = 78.55 \text{ mm}$$

---

#### Part (b) Solution — Experimental Procedure:

1. **Specimen Preparation:** Measure the free length $L_0$ of the spring using a vernier caliper, count the number of active coils $N$, measure the wire diameter $d$ using a micrometer (3 readings averaged), and calculate the mean coil diameter $D$ as the mean of outer and inner coil diameters.

2. **Machine Setup:** Place the spring coaxially between the two compression platens of the spring testing machine. Ensure the spring axis is aligned with the loading axis to avoid buckling.

3. **Initial Seating:** Apply a small initial load (say 10–20 N) to remove any lateral play and seat the spring. Set the dial gauge / LVDT reading to zero at this position.

4. **Incremental Loading:** Apply loads in equal increments (e.g., 20 N each step) up to about 75% of the estimated maximum safe load. At each step, record the load $W_i$ and the corresponding deflection $\delta_i$.

5. **Unloading Cycle:** Gradually unload the spring and verify whether the spring returns to its original length (to check for permanent set).

6. **Tabulation & Plotting:** Tabulate $W_i$ vs $\delta_i$. Plot a graph of $W$ (Y-axis) vs $\delta$ (X-axis). The graph should be a **straight line** passing through the origin.

7. **Calculation of $k$:** The slope of this straight line gives the experimental spring stiffness $k_{exp}$.

8. **Theoretical Comparison:** Calculate $k_{th} = \dfrac{G d^{4}}{8 D^{3} N}$ and find the percentage error:

$$\% \text{Error} = \frac{\vert k_{th} - k_{exp} \vert}{k_{th}} \times 100$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — ALTERNATIVE

**(a)** A close-coiled helical spring is required to carry a load of $W = 500$ N with a deflection of $\delta = 50$ mm. The material has $G = 80$ GPa and permissible shear stress $\tau = 400$ MPa. The spring index $C = 6$. Find:
1. The wire diameter $d$ and mean coil diameter $D$.
2. The number of active coils $N$.
3. The solid length and free length. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with neat sketches the **types of ends** used in helical compression springs and their effect on the number of active coils. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Solution to Question B:**

#### Part (a) Solution:

**Given:** $W = 500$ N, $\delta = 50$ mm, $G = 80{,}000$ MPa, $\tau = 400$ MPa, $C = D/d = 6$.

**1) Wire and mean coil diameters:**

From the shear stress formula (neglecting direct shear for first iteration):

$$d^{3} = \frac{8 W D}{\pi \tau} = \frac{8 \times 500 \times 6d}{\pi \times 400} = \frac{19.1 \, d}{1}$$

$$d^{2} = \frac{8 \times 500 \times 6}{\pi \times 400} = 19.0986 \quad \Rightarrow \quad d = 4.37 \text{ mm}$$

Take standard gauge: $d = 4.5$ mm.

Then $D = C \cdot d = 6 \times 4.5 = 27$ mm.

**2) Number of active coils:**

From $\delta = \dfrac{8 W D^{3} N}{G d^{4}}$:

$$N = \frac{\delta \cdot G \cdot d^{4}}{8 W D^{3}} = \frac{50 \times 80{,}000 \times 4.5^{4}}{8 \times 500 \times 27^{3}}$$

$$N = \frac{50 \times 80{,}000 \times 410.0625}{8 \times 500 \times 19{,}683} = \frac{1.640 \times 10^{9}}{7.873 \times 10^{7}}$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 20.83 \approx 21 \text{ coils}}$$

**3) Solid length and free length:**

Solid length: $L_s = N \cdot d = 21 \times 4.5 = 94.5$ mm.

Free length (assuming a clearance of about 10–15% of solid length):

$$L_0 = L_s + \delta_{max} + \text{clearance} = 94.5 + 50 + 5 \approx 149.5 \text{ mm}$$

---

#### Part (b) Solution — Types of Spring Ends:

| End Type | Sketch | Active Coils | Use |
|----------|--------|--------------|-----|
| **Plain ends** (no loops) | Coil ends are left as cut | All $N_t$ coils active | Low-cost, low-load |
| **Plain ends ground** | Coils ground flat for stability | All $N_t$ coils active | Better load transfer |
| **Squared & ground ends** | End coils touch the next coil at 90° | $N_t - 2$ active | High stability, common |
| **Closed & ground ends** | Last coil wraps fully | $N_t - 2$ active | Maximum stability |

**Effect on active coils:** When end coils are in full contact with the adjacent coil (squared or closed), they do **not deflect** under load. Hence they are **inactive** and must be subtracted from total coils $N_t$ to get the number of active coils $N$ used in the deflection formula.

**Rule of thumb:**
- Plain ends: $N = N_t$
- Squared/Closed ends: $N = N_t - 2$

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the direct shear term** $\dfrac{4W}{\pi d^{2}}$ in $\tau_{max}$ — for springs with $C < 10$, this contributes **5–15%** of the total stress. Examiners explicitly test this. **(−2 marks)**
>
> 2. **Unit inconsistency in $G$:** Using $G = 80$ GPa $= 80{,}000$ MPa $= 80{,}000$ N/mm² in a formula that uses N and mm. Mixing MPa and N/mm² is fine (numerically equal), but mixing with kN causes errors.
>
> 3. **Conflating "active" and "total" coils:** Always subtract the inactive end coils before plugging $N$ into the deflection formula.
>
> 4. **Wrong spring index interpretation:** $C = D/d$, NOT $D = C \cdot d$ inverted. Re-derive from the formula in the question.
>
> 5. **In the graph plotting, missing "best-fit line"** — examiners want a **straight line through origin** with scatter points, NOT a zig-zag connecting every point. The slope = $k$.
>
> 6. **Stating "percentage error" without absolute value sign** — always use $\vert k_{th} - k_{exp} \vert$.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Open coiled spring** = coils separated by a gap in the free state.
- [x] **Master deflection formula:** $\delta = \dfrac{8 W D^{3} N}{G d^{4}}$
- [x] **Master stiffness formula:** $k = \dfrac{G d^{4}}{8 D^{3} N}$
- [x] **Maximum shear stress (with direct shear):** $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{8 W D}{\pi d^{3}} + \dfrac{4 W}{\pi d^{2}}$
- [x] **Maximum shear stress (torsional only, for $C \ge 10$):** $\tau = \dfrac{8 W D}{\pi d^{3}}$
- [x] **Spring index:** $C = D/d$, ideal range $4$–$12$.
- [x] **Strain energy stored:** $U = \dfrac{1}{2} W \delta = \dfrac{1}{2} k \delta^{2}$ (also = area under $W$–$\delta$ graph).
- [x] **Solid length:** $L_s = N \cdot d$ (length when coils are fully compressed, no gaps).
- [x] **Active vs Total coils:** Subtract 2 for squared/closed ends, 0 for plain ends.
- [x] **Hooke's Law in torsion** — the load-deflection graph is **linear** for the entire elastic range.
- [x] **Procedure order:** Measure $d, D, N, L_0$ → Seat spring → Zero dial gauge → Incremental load → Tabulate → Plot $W$ vs $\delta$ → Slope = $k_{exp}$ → Compare with $k_{th}$ → Compute % error.
- [x] **Material property:** For steel, $G = 80$ GPa (or $79.3$ GPa as per IS standards).
- [x] **Helix angle:** $\alpha = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{\text{pitch}}{\pi D}\right)$.
- [x] **Energy method used for derivation:** Castigliano's Theorem (Strain Energy of Torsion).
- [x] **End correction in design:** For static analysis, use $N_a = N_t - 2$ for closed/squared ends.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Workability of Concrete by Slump Test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGL308 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of Workability of Concrete by Slump Test

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Workability of Concrete** is defined as the *property of freshly mixed concrete which determines the ease and homogeneity with which it can be mixed, transported, placed, compacted, and finished*. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, workability is a composite property encompassing **consistency**, **fluidity**, **mobility**, **compactability**, and **finishability** of a concrete mix.

The **Slump Test** is the most widely adopted empirical laboratory test (codified under **IS 1199:1959** and **ASTM C143/C143M**) used to measure the **consistency** of fresh concrete before it sets. It is a *qualitative to semi-quantitative* assessment of workability, particularly suitable for medium-to-high workability mixes with a maximum aggregate size of **38 mm**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 - Experiments):**
> The Slump Test is the **first prescribed experiment** in the laboratory module. It is mandatory for continuous assessment (CE) evaluation, internal lab exams, and forms the base for all subsequent mix-design experiments. Mastery of this test is **non-negotiable** for securing full internal marks.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pancake Batter" Intuition

Imagine you are making pancakes at three different hydration levels:

1. **Stiff, dry batter** → The batter holds its shape when spooned onto a pan. The heap stays tall. → This is a **LOW WORKABILITY** concrete (slump ≈ 0–25 mm).
2. **Medium batter** → The batter slumps slightly, spreading about halfway down. → This is a **MEDIUM WORKABILITY** concrete (slump ≈ 50–100 mm) — the *sweet spot* for most structural pours like beams, columns, and slabs.
3. **Runny, watery batter** → The batter collapses flat and spreads wide. → This is a **HIGH WORKABILITY** concrete (slump ≥ 150 mm), used only for heavily reinforced sections or pumped concrete.

The **Slump Test** is literally this "pancake test" in standardized form — you fill a truncated cone (the *slump cone* or *Abrams cone*) with concrete in three layers, rod each layer 25 times, lift the cone vertically, and measure how much the concrete *slumps* (settles downward) under its own self-weight.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** Workability ≠ Strength. A *high* slump does not mean *stronger* concrete. In fact, excessive water (which increases slump) **reduces** the final compressive strength. The test tells you about *fresh* behavior, not *hardened* performance.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Parameter | Standard Value | Source |
|---|---|---|
| Slump Cone Height | **300 mm** | IS 1199:1959 |
| Slump Cone Top Diameter | **100 mm** | IS 1199:1959 |
| Slump Cone Bottom Diameter | **200 mm** | IS 1199:1959 |
| Tamping Rod Diameter | **16 mm** | IS 1199:1959 |
| Tamping Rod Length | **600 mm** | IS 1199:1959 |
| Number of Layers | **3 equal layers** | IS 1199:1959 |
| Tamping Strokes per Layer | **25 strokes** | IS 1199:1959 |
| Cone Lifting Time | **5 to 10 seconds** | IS 1199:1959 |
| Maximum Aggregate Size Applicable | **38 mm** | IS 1199:1959 |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Principle — Why a Cone?

A truncated cone (frustum) is used because:

- The **tapered geometry** ensures that once the mould is lifted, the concrete subsides due to **self-weight gravity** without any applied external load.
- The **3:2:1 diameter ratio** (top:bottom:height proportions) creates a uniform shear-stress distribution in the fresh concrete, ensuring a *true* measure of internal friction (cohesion + viscosity).
- The small size (**12 inches tall, 8 inches top, 4 inches bottom** in imperial units) makes the test field-portable and quick (<5 minutes per trial).

## 2.2 Types of Slump — The Three Failure Modes

When the cone is lifted, the concrete can subside in **three distinct patterns**, each revealing critical information about the mix's internal rheology:

### 🔹 Type 1: TRUE SLUMP (Ideal)
- The concrete subsides uniformly, maintaining a **symmetrical, truncated-cone shape**.
- Indicates a **well-proportioned, cohesive** mix with good workability.
- This is the **only** slump that can be measured reliably as a single vertical value.
- **Interpretation:** Mix is well-designed; proceed with placement.

### 🔹 Type 2: SHEAR SLUMP
- The concrete slides **obliquely**, breaking off on one side. Half the mass shears down while the other half stays in place.
- Indicates a **harsh, undersanded, or over-stiff** mix lacking cohesion.
- **KTU Pitfall:** Shear slumps are *not valid* for measurement. The test must be **repeated** with a fresh sample. Recording a shear slump as a numerical value is a guaranteed mark-loser.

### 🔹 Type 3: COLLAPSE SLUMP
- The concrete completely **collapses flat**, spreading into a wide, low puddle.
- Indicates a **wet, over-sanded, or over-workable** mix with excessive water content.
- Indicates a high water-cement ratio → **future strength loss and segregation risk**.

> [!WARNING]
> **For KTU Lab Record:** Always classify the slump type *before* recording the numerical value. If you observe a shear slump, write "Shear Slump — Test Inconclusive, Repeat" in your observation table. This shows examiner-level rigor.

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Procedure (Bullet-Logic)

**Step 1: Preparation of Apparatus**
- Clean the slump cone, base plate, and tamping rod. Dampen the cone interior (wet, not dripping) to prevent water absorption from the mix.

**Step 2: Placement of Cone**
- Place the cone on a **smooth, level, non-absorbent base plate** (typically a steel or flat plywood sheet). Hold the cone firmly in place (either by foot-clamps or by a colleague standing on the foot-rests).

**Step 3: Filling in Three Layers**
- Fill the cone in **three equal layers**, each approximately **1/3 the cone height (100 mm)** when compacted.
- **Layer 1:** Pour concrete up to ~1/3 height. Tamp **25 times uniformly** across the cross-section using the rounded end of the rod. Do not strike the bottom plate.
- **Layer 2:** Pour up to 2/3 height. Tamp 25 times, with strokes penetrating into the **first layer by ~25 mm**.
- **Layer 3:** Heap concrete above the top rim. Tamp 25 times, with strokes penetrating into the **second layer by ~25 mm**. Strike off the excess concrete with a trowel to obtain a flush, level top surface.

**Step 4: Cone Removal**
- Lift the cone **vertically upward** in a **single smooth motion** over **5 to 10 seconds**. No twisting or lateral motion.
- The concrete now subsides (or fails to subside) under self-weight.

**Step 5: Measurement**
- Place the slump cone (inverted) *beside* the slumped concrete (NOT on top of it).
- Measure the **vertical difference** between the **top of the cone** (reference height = 300 mm) and the **displaced center of the subsided concrete**.
- Record this as the **Slump Value** in **millimeters (mm)**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| Slump Value | $S = H_c - H_f$ | **mm** | $H_c$ = cone height (300 mm); $H_f$ = final height of subsided concrete |
| Height of Subsided Concrete | $H_f = H_c - S$ | mm | Derived from above |
| % Subsidence (Subsidence Ratio) | $\eta = \dfrac{S}{H_c} \times 100 = \dfrac{S}{300} \times 100$ | **%** | Used for normalized comparison |
| Compaction Factor (Related) | $C_F = \dfrac{W_p}{W_l}$ | Dimensionless | $W_p$ = partially compacted weight; $W_l$ = fully compacted weight |
| Water-Cement Ratio (Empirical link) | $\dfrac{W}{C} \propto S$ | — | Higher W/C gives higher slump; but lower strength |
| Slump Tolerance (per IS 456) | $\pm 25$ mm from target | mm | Acceptable range for nominal mix delivery |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical LaTeX Avoidance Note for Tables:** Notice that the `|` (pipe) symbol is replaced by `\vert` or `\mid` in the formula cells above to prevent breaking the markdown table parser. This is a KTU note-formatting best practice.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Slump Test is the **frontline QC (Quality Control) test** at every ready-mix concrete (RMC) plant and major construction site. Practical applications include:

- **On-site quality assurance:** A truck arriving at site is slump-tested before discharge. If slump < target − 25 mm, the load is *rejected* or *re-tempered* (with strict water-cement ratio limits).
- **Mix-design validation:** Trial mixes in the lab are slump-tested to confirm target workability before structural pours.
- **Formwork pressure estimation:** Highly workable (high-slump) concrete exerts higher lateral pressure on formwork, requiring stronger bracing.
- **Pumping-ability check:** Concrete for pumped delivery typically requires slump ≥ 100 mm; lower slumps cause pump-line blockages.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Slump Measurement Equation

The slump value is fundamentally a **height difference** measurement between a known reference (the empty cone) and the subsided concrete mass. We derive it rigorously:

**Given:**
- $H_c$ = Original (reference) height of the slump cone = **300 mm** (constant per IS 1199).
- $H_f$ = Final (subsided) height of the concrete mass, measured from the base plate to the highest point of the slumped concrete.

**Geometric Setup:**
The cone is a frustum with bottom diameter $D_b = 200$ mm, top diameter $D_t = 100$ mm, and height $H_c = 300$ mm. When the cone is lifted, gravity acts vertically downward on the concrete. The mass settles axially without horizontal constraint (since the mould is removed). The vertical subsidence is purely a function of internal shear resistance.

**Defining Equation:**

The slump is defined as the **vertical subsidence** of the center of the original top surface of the concrete:

$$
S \equiv H_c - H_f
$$

**Solving for the concrete's final height:**

$$
H_f = H_c - S
$$

**Substituting numerical values for the standard cone:**

$$
H_f = 300\ \text{mm} - S
$$

**For a typical medium-workability mix (target $S = 75$ mm):**

$$
H_f = 300 - 75 = 225\ \text{mm}
$$

This means a 75 mm slump concrete subsides to a final height of 225 mm, which is a **25% subsidence** of the original cone height.

## 3.2 Derivation of Percentage Subsidence ($\eta$)

To enable **normalized comparison** between slump values obtained from different cone geometries (in international codes), we define a dimensionless subsidence ratio:

$$
\eta = \frac{\text{Amount of subsidence}}{\text{Original height}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\eta = \frac{S}{H_c} \times 100\%
$$

**For the standard IS cone ($H_c = 300$ mm):**

$$
\eta = \frac{S}{300} \times 100\%
$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**

For $S = 100$ mm:

$$
\eta = \frac{100}{300} \times 100\% = 33.33\%
$$

For $S = 25$ mm (low workability):

$$
\eta = \frac{25}{300} \times 100\% = 8.33\%
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Interpretation of Slump Values

| Slump Range (mm) | Workability Class | Subsidence $\eta$ (%) | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **0 – 25** | Very Low | 0 – 8.3% | Road construction (pavement), mass concrete with vibrators |
| **25 – 50** | Low | 8.3 – 16.7% | Lightly reinforced sections, foundation footings |
| **50 – 100** | Medium | 16.7 – 33.3% | Beams, columns, slabs, walls (general structural work) |
| **100 – 150** | High | 33.3 – 50% | Heavily reinforced sections, narrow formwork |
| **> 150** | Very High | > 50% | Pumped concrete, tremie placement, underwater concrete |

## 3.4 Complete Worked Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem Statement:**
A concrete sample is filled in a standard slump cone in three equal layers, each tamped 25 times. After lifting the cone vertically, the height of the subsided concrete is measured as 220 mm. Determine:
1. The slump value of the concrete.
2. The percentage subsidence.
3. The workability classification.
4. State two suitable applications.

**Step 1: Identify knowns.**

$$
H_c = 300\ \text{mm},\quad H_f = 220\ \text{mm}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate slump.**

$$
S = H_c - H_f = 300 - 220 = 80\ \text{mm}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate percentage subsidence.**

$$
\eta = \frac{S}{H_c} \times 100 = \frac{80}{300} \times 100 = 26.67\%
$$

**Step 4: Classify workability.**

From the analytical matrix above, a slump of 80 mm falls in the range **50 – 100 mm**, classified as **Medium Workability**.

**Step 5: Suggest applications.**

Medium workability mixes are suitable for:
1. Reinforced **beams and columns** of normal cross-section.
2. **Slabs and walls** with standard reinforcement spacing.

## 3.5 Limitations of the Slump Test (Analytical Honesty)

No KTU note is complete without a critical evaluation. The slump test has well-documented limitations:

- **Not suitable for very stiff mixes** (zero-slump concrete for roller-compacted pavements cannot be tested).
- **Not suitable for highly fluid mixes** (self-compacting concrete, SCC, requires the J-ring or slump-flow test).
- **Operator-sensitive:** Rod angle, rod speed, and cone-lifting speed can vary the result by ±10 mm.
- **Single-point measurement:** It only gives consistency; it cannot capture viscosity or stability independently.
- **Aggregate size limit:** Coarse aggregates > 38 mm cause obstruction in the cone and give misleading results.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Practical Tip:** Always conduct the test on a **flat, vibration-free surface**. Vibrations from nearby equipment or foot traffic can cause premature subsidence, leading to a falsely *higher* slump reading — a common error reported in lab evaluations.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Slump Test Procedure

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **complete logical flow** of the Slump Test procedure, from apparatus setup to interpretation:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step A: Apparatus Preparation] --> B[Step B: Position Cone on Base Plate]
    B --> C[Step C1: Fill Layer 1 - 1/3 Height]
    C --> D[Step C1: Tamp 25 Strokes]
    D --> E[Step C2: Fill Layer 2 - 2/3 Height]
    E --> F[Step C2: Tamp 25 Strokes - Penetrating 25mm into Layer 1]
    F --> G[Step C3: Heap Layer 3 - Above Rim]
    G --> H[Step C3: Tamp 25 Strokes - Penetrating 25mm into Layer 2]
    H --> I[Step D: Strike Off Top Surface Flush]
    I --> J[Step E: Lift Cone Vertically in 5-10 Seconds]
    J --> K[Step F: Observe Slump Type]
    K --> L{Step G: Classify Slump Pattern}
    L --> M1[True Slump - Measure S in mm]
    L --> M2[Shear Slump - Reject and Repeat]
    L --> M3[Collapse Slump - Note as Very High Workability]
    M1 --> N[Step H: Record Slump Value]
    M2 --> O[Repeat with Fresh Sample]
    M3 --> N
    N --> P[Step I: Compare with IS Tolerance plus minus 25 mm]
    P --> Q[Step J: Classify Workability and Report]
    O --> B
```

## 4.2 Apparatus Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EQ[Required Apparatus]
        cone[Slump Cone - Truncated Frustum]
        base[Base Plate - Flat Non-absorbent]
        rod[Tamping Rod - 16mm dia, 600mm length]
        ruler[Measuring Scale - mm graduation]
        scoop[Sampling Scoop and Trowel]
    end
    subgraph MIX[Fresh Concrete Sample]
        sample[Mixed Concrete Batch]
    end
    sample --> cone
    rod --> cone
    cone --> base
    cone --> ruler
    scoop --> cone
```

## 4.3 Three Types of Slump — Schematic Block Representation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Fresh Concrete Sample] --> Lift[Lift Slump Cone]
    Lift --> Decide{What Pattern is Observed?}
    Decide --> TS[True Slump - Symmetrical Cone Subsides Uniformly]
    Decide --> SS[Shear Slump - Half Mass Slides Obliquely]
    Decide --> CS[Collapse Slump - Mass Spreads Flat]
    TS --> TS1[Indicates Cohesive Well-proportioned Mix]
    SS --> SS1[Indicates Harsh Undersanded Mix - Repeat Test]
    CS --> CS1[Indicates Over-wet Mix - High W/C Risk]
    TS1 --> Valid[Valid Measurement Record S in mm]
    SS1 --> Invalid[Invalid - Reject and Repeat]
    CS1 --> Valid
```

## 4.4 Cross-Sectional Geometry of the Slump Cone (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ConeGeo[Slump Cone Geometry per IS 1199]
        D1[Top Diameter D sub t = 100 mm]
        D2[Bottom Diameter D sub b = 200 mm]
        H1[Height H sub c = 300 mm]
        D2 --- D1
        D2 --- H1
        D1 --- H1
    end
    subgraph RodGeo[Tamping Rod Geometry]
        D3[Rod Diameter = 16 mm]
        H2[Rod Length = 600 mm]
        D3 --- H2
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The above diagrams are logical/functional flowcharts representing the procedure and apparatus. They are intended as study aids, not physical engineering drawings. For the actual physical representation, refer to IS 1199:1959 — *Methods of Sampling and Analysis of Concrete*.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define the term "workability" of concrete. List the three different types of slump observed during the slump test.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Workability (1 Mark):** Workability is defined as the property of fresh concrete that determines the ease with which it can be mixed, transported, placed, compacted, and finished without segregation, while maintaining homogeneity.

**Three Types of Slump (2 Marks):**

1. **True Slump:** The concrete subsides uniformly, maintaining a symmetrical truncated-cone shape. Indicates a well-proportioned, cohesive mix.
2. **Shear Slump:** Half of the concrete shears down obliquely while the other half remains in place. Indicates a harsh, non-cohesive mix and is *not a valid* measurement.
3. **Collapse Slump:** The concrete collapses completely flat, spreading into a low puddle. Indicates an over-wet mix with excessive water content.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**State the IS code standard dimensions of a slump cone. Mention any two limitations of the slump test.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**IS Code Dimensions (2 Marks):**

| Component | Dimension |
|---|---|
| Height of Cone | **300 mm** |
| Bottom Diameter | **200 mm** |
| Top Diameter | **100 mm** |
| Tamping Rod Length | **600 mm** |
| Tamping Rod Diameter | **16 mm** |

**Two Limitations (1 Mark):**
1. The test is not suitable for very stiff (zero-slump) mixes or for self-compacting concrete.
2. The result is operator-sensitive and depends on the rod angle, rod speed, and cone-lifting technique.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

### 🅰️ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the procedure for determining the workability of fresh concrete by the slump test, with the help of a neat sketch. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A concrete sample tested in the standard slump cone gave a subsided height of 215 mm. Determine the slump value, classify the workability, and state two suitable applications. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Apparatus Required (1 Mark):**
Slump cone (truncated frustum, 300 mm height, 200 mm bottom diameter, 100 mm top diameter), base plate, tamping rod (16 mm diameter, 600 mm long, with rounded end), measuring scale, scoop, and trowel.

**Procedure (5 Marks):**

1. Clean and dampen the slump cone and base plate. Place the cone on a flat, level, non-absorbent base plate and hold it firmly using foot-clamps.
2. Fill the cone with freshly mixed concrete in **three equal layers**, each approximately 100 mm deep when compacted.
3. Tamp each layer **25 times** uniformly with the tamping rod, distributing the strokes over the entire cross-section. For layers 2 and 3, the rod should penetrate about 25 mm into the underlying layer.
4. After the third layer is tamped, strike off the excess concrete with a trowel to obtain a flush, level top surface.
5. Carefully lift the cone **vertically upward** in a single smooth motion over a period of **5 to 10 seconds**, without any twisting or lateral movement.
6. Place the inverted cone beside the subsided concrete. Measure the **vertical difference** between the top of the cone (reference) and the highest point of the subsided concrete. This difference is the **slump value** in mm.
7. Observe and record the **type of slump** (true, shear, or collapse) before noting the numerical value.

**Neat Sketch (1 Mark):** A labelled diagram showing the cone, base plate, three layers with tamping positions, the lifted cone position, and the measurement of slump.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Given (1 Mark):**
$H_c = 300$ mm (standard cone height), $H_f = 215$ mm (subsided height).

**Step 1: Calculate Slump Value (2 Marks)**

$$
S = H_c - H_f = 300 - 215 = 85\ \text{mm}
$$

**Step 2: Classify Workability (2 Marks)**

The slump of 85 mm falls in the range **50 – 100 mm**, which corresponds to **Medium Workability** as per IS 456:2000.

**Step 3: Two Suitable Applications (2 Marks)**

1. **Reinforced concrete beams and columns** with normal cross-sectional dimensions and standard reinforcement spacing.
2. **Slabs and walls** in framed structures, where moderate compaction effort using standard needle vibrators is sufficient.

---

### 🅱️ Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Alternative Choice]

**(a)** What are the factors affecting the workability of concrete? Explain any five factors in detail. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** During a slump test on site, the observed slump of a delivery truck is 30 mm, against a target slump of 75 mm. Discuss the possible reasons for this deviation and the corrective actions to be taken. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Definition of Workability Affecting Factors (1 Mark):**
Workability is influenced by the properties of constituent materials, their proportions, and ambient/site conditions.

**Five Key Factors (6 Marks — 1.2 Marks each):**

1. **Water Content:** Higher water content increases fluidity and slump. However, excess water reduces strength and durability. There is a near-linear positive correlation between water content and slump.

2. **Aggregate Properties:** Shape (rounded aggregates are more workable than angular/crushed ones), surface texture (smooth > rough), grading (well-graded > gap-graded), and maximum size (larger aggregate size gives higher workability for the same water content).

3. **Cement Content:** Increased cement paste volume increases workability, as paste acts as a lubricant between aggregate particles. However, high cement content can lead to stickiness and finishing difficulties.

4. **Admixtures:** Chemical admixtures like **plasticizers** (e.g., lignosulfonates) and **superplasticizers** (e.g., polycarboxylate ethers) dramatically increase workability without adding water. Air-entraining agents also improve workability by creating microscopic bubbles that act as lubricants.

5. **Mix Proportions and W/C Ratio:** The water-cement ratio is the single most dominant factor. A higher W/C ratio produces a more workable but weaker mix. The target W/C ratio for M20 concrete is typically 0.55, and for M25 is 0.50.

6. **Temperature and Time:** Higher ambient temperatures reduce workability due to faster moisture loss and accelerated hydration. Concrete loses workability with time after mixing — typically a 10–25 mm slump loss per hour.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Observation (1 Mark):**
Observed slump = 30 mm; Target slump = 75 mm; Deviation = 45 mm (exceeds ±25 mm tolerance per IS 456).

**Possible Reasons for Low Slump (3 Marks):**

1. **Delay in transit:** The concrete has been in transit too long, causing slump loss due to initial hydration and moisture evaporation.
2. **High ambient temperature:** Hot weather causes rapid water loss and accelerated setting.
3. **Low water content or low W/C ratio in the mix:** The batching plant may have under-dosed water for quality control.
4. **Absorption by dry aggregates:** Highly absorptive aggregates (especially if not pre-wetted) draw water from the mix.
5. **Incorrect admixture dosing:** Insufficient plasticizer or expired admixture.

**Corrective Actions (3 Marks):**

1. **Re-tempering:** A measured, controlled quantity of water or a superplasticizer dose may be added and re-mixed. However, water addition must not exceed the maximum W/C ratio specified in the mix design — this is critical and must be authorized by the site engineer.
2. **Rejection of the load:** If re-tempering compromises the W/C ratio, the load should be **rejected** to avoid strength loss.
3. **Process improvements:** Reduce transit time, use chilled water in hot weather, pre-wet aggregates, and add retarders to extend workability life for future deliveries.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to state the IS code reference.** Always cite **IS 1199:1959** (for the test method) and **IS 456:2000** (for tolerance limits) in your answer. Marks are specifically reserved for code citations.
> 2. **Recording a shear slump numerically.** A shear slump is **invalid** — you must state "Test Inconclusive" and recommend repetition. Writing a number for it costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Unit errors.** Slump is in **mm**, not cm. A 75 mm slump written as "7.5 cm" or "75 cm" loses 0.5 mark.
> 4. **Mixing up $H_c$ and $H_f$.** $H_c$ = cone height (300 mm, always); $H_f$ = subsided height (your measured value). Reversing these gives a nonsensical negative slump.
> 5. **Conflating workability and strength.** High workability ≠ high strength. Do not write "this concrete is strong because it has a high slump" — it is a *strength-loss indicator* in many cases.
> 6. **Skipping the rod-penetration step.** When tamping layers 2 and 3, the rod **must** penetrate 25 mm into the previous layer. Skipping this detail costs 1 mark in the procedure.

---

## 5.3 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Workability = ease of mixing, transporting, placing, compacting, and finishing fresh concrete.
- ✅ Slump test measures **consistency**, which is a component of workability.
- ✅ **IS Code:** IS 1199:1959 governs the slump test; IS 456:2000 governs acceptance tolerance (±25 mm).
- ✅ **Cone dimensions:** Top = 100 mm, Bottom = 200 mm, Height = 300 mm. Always remember 1:2:3 ratio.
- ✅ **Tamping rod:** 16 mm diameter, 600 mm long, 25 strokes per layer, 3 equal layers.
- ✅ **Cone-lifting time:** 5 to 10 seconds. Slower = more reliable measurement.
- ✅ **Slump equation:** $S = H_c - H_f = 300 - H_f$ (in mm).
- ✅ **Three slump types:** True (valid), Shear (invalid — repeat), Collapse (valid — over-wet).
- ✅ **Workability classification:** 0–25 (Very Low) → 25–50 (Low) → 50–100 (Medium) → 100–150 (High) → >150 (Very High).
- ✅ **Applications by slump:** Beams/columns (medium), pumped concrete (high), pavements (low).
- ✅ **High W/C → High slump BUT Low strength.** The single most important trade-off.
- ✅ **Maximum aggregate size for validity:** 38 mm.
- ✅ **Tolerance per IS 456:** ±25 mm from target slump at the point of delivery.
- ✅ **Always classify slump type FIRST, then measure value.** Shear slump must be rejected.
- ✅ **Operator-sensitivity:** Test must be on a flat, vibration-free, non-absorbent surface.
- ✅ **Key formulas to memorize:** $S = H_c - H_f$ and $\eta = \dfrac{S}{300} \times 100\%$.

---

> **End of Notes — Module Experiment 2: Slump Test | PCAGL308 — Strength of Materials | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of Workability of Concrete by Compaction Factor Test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAGL308 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Determination of Workability of Concrete by Compaction Factor Test # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) The **Compaction Factor Test** is a laboratory method standardized under **IS 1199:1959 (Reaffirmed 2018)** for determining the **workability of fresh concrete**, particularly suited for **low to medium workability mixes** where the conventional slump test fails to register measurable differences. The test was originally developed by **Glanville, Collins, and Matthews** in 1947 and is also referred to as the "Glanville Compaction Test." > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Compaction Factor (CF) is defined as the **ratio of the mass of partially compacted concrete** (achieved through a standardized drop sequence through two hoppers) **to the mass of fully compacted concrete** (achieved through hand or vibration compaction in the same mould). Mathematically, the workability parameter is expressed as: $$CF = \frac{\text{Mass of Partially Compacted Concrete}}{\text{Mass of Fully Compacted Concrete}}$$ The value of CF always lies between **0.70 and 1.00**, where higher values indicate greater workability and easier compaction under standard effort. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview Imagine pouring a sticky, thick honey-oat mixture into a tall glass. - If the mixture flows easily and settles into a dense, compact mass under its own weight alone, it has **high workability** — like a thin smoothie that settles in a glass without air pockets. - If the mixture is stiff and chunky (like a thick cookie dough) and refuses to fill the corners of the glass, leaving air gaps, it has **low workability**. The Compaction Factor Test simulates the "honey-oat" behaviour precisely. We drop concrete from a fixed height through two hoppers and see how densely it packs itself into a cylinder **without any vibration or rodding**. The denser it settles on its own, the more workable it is. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Context:** The CF Test is especially important in Kerala's infrastructure sector where **Ready-Mix Concrete (RMC)** is used in dense reinforcement zones — such as pile caps, bridge girders, and foundation rafts — where the concrete must flow into narrow gaps between rebar cages under self-weight without segregation. ## 1.3 Classification of Workability Based on CF Values | Compaction Factor Range | Workability Classification | Typical Application | |---|---|---| | 0.70 – 0.80 | **Very Low** | Mass concrete dams, road bases | | 0.80 – 0.85 | **Low** | Pavements, lightly reinforced sections | | 0.85 – 0.92 | **Medium** | Beams, columns with normal reinforcement | | 0.92 – 0.95 | **High** | Heavily reinforced sections, slabs | | 0.95 – 1.00 | **Very High** | Tremie concrete, pumped concrete, SCC | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Reference Threshold (IS 1199:1959):** A CF value of **0.85** is generally accepted as the lower limit for **practical site workability** in most KTU lab assessment questions. ## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bold Highlights) - **Density of fully compacted concrete (ρ_c):** ≈ **2400 kg/m³** (normal weight) - **Drop height in upper hopper:** Fixed by **IS 1199 apparatus geometry** - **Free-fall path in lower hopper:** Standardized at **200 mm** - **Mould dimensions:** Standard **150 mm × 150 mm × 300 mm** (or 150 mm dia × 300 mm height cylinder) - **Acceleration due to gravity (g):** **9.81 m/s²** (assumed standard at KTU Kerala lab conditions) # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Basis — The "Why" Behind the Test The fundamental principle of the Compaction Factor Test rests on **Newtonian fluid-mechanics concepts** combined with **rheological workability theory**. When fresh concrete is allowed to fall freely through a vertical distance, the work done by **gravity (W = mgh)** is partially absorbed by the internal friction between aggregate particles, cement paste viscosity, and surface tension at the cement-water interface. The test quantifies the **self-compacting energy** of the concrete — i.e., the ability of the mix to consolidate under its own weight, eliminating entrapped air voids. A higher CF implies lower internal friction and greater lubricity of the paste matrix. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Cognitive Link (Bloom's Level 2 – Understand):** The student must understand that workability is **NOT a single property** but a composite of *consistency, mobility, and compactability* — and CF Test primarily measures **compactability**. ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of the Test The Compaction Factor Test follows a rigidly defined sequence under IS 1199: 1. **Sample Preparation:** Fresh concrete is sampled and placed gently (without compaction) into the **upper hopper** of the apparatus. 2. **First Drop:** The hinged door of the upper hopper is released, allowing concrete to fall freely into the **lower hopper**. 3. **Second Drop:** The lower hopper door is opened, allowing concrete to fall into the **cylindrical mould** positioned below. 4. **Surface Strike-off:** Excess concrete is struck off flush with the top of the mould using a trowel. 5. **Weighing (Partial Compaction Mass):** The mould + partially compacted concrete is weighed → **W₁ (Partially Compacted Mass)**. 6. **Full Compaction:** The concrete is then re-compacted in the same mould using hand rodding (25 strokes in 3 layers) or vibration until full compaction is achieved. 7. **Surface Strike-off (Again):** The top is levelled again. 8. **Weighing (Full Compaction Mass):** The mould + fully compacted concrete is weighed → **W₂ (Fully Compacted Mass)**. 9. **Mould Tare Weight:** The empty mould weight → **W₀**. 10. **Calculation:** The CF is calculated using the mass of concrete (not the gross mass). ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Quantity | Symbolic Form | SI Unit | Engineering Meaning | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Compaction Factor** | $CF = \dfrac{M_p}{M_f}$ | Dimensionless | Workability index | | 2 | **Mass of Partially Compacted Concrete** | $M_p = W_1 - W_0$ | **kg** | Concrete mass after standard drop | | 3 | **Mass of Fully Compacted Concrete** | $M_f = W_2 - W_0$ | **kg** | Concrete mass after rodding/vibration | | 4 | **Density of Fresh Concrete (Theoretical)** | $\rho_c = \dfrac{M_f}{V_m}$ | **kg/m³** | Confirms mix design compliance | | 5 | **Volume of Standard Mould** | $V_m = A_m \times h_m$ | **m³** | Cylinder: π × (d/2)² × h | | 6 | **Relative Compaction Ratio** | $R_c = \dfrac{M_p}{M_f} \times 100\%$ | **%** | Percentage of full compaction achieved | | 7 | **Air Content (Approximate)** | $A_v = \left(1 - CF\right) \times V_m$ | **m³** | Entrapped air volume | | 8 | **Specific Gravity Check (Gravel vs CF)** | $G_s \propto \dfrac{1}{CF}$ for fixed w/c | **—** | Higher aggregate SG → lower CF | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Examination Note (KTU 2024):** The CF formula uses **MASS of CONCRETE only** (i.e., $W_1 - W_0$ and $W_2 - W_0$). A common student error is to use the gross weights $W_1$ and $W_2$ directly — this is **WRONG** and results in **2 to 3 mark deductions** in KTU valuation. ## 2.4 Engineering Utility of the Compaction Factor Test - **Pumped Concrete Verification:** Ensures pipe-line concrete is not too stiff to pump. - **Tremie Concrete (Underwater):** Critical for offshore and pier construction in Kerala's backwater regions. - **Quality Control at RMC Plants:** Used as acceptance criterion for delivery batches. - **Mix Design Validation:** Confirms whether a designed w/c ratio is appropriate for the intended placement method. - **Comparison of Admixtures:** Quantifies the efficiency of plasticizers and superplasticizers. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy (Engineering Utility):** The CF test is to a civil engineer what a **fuel-flow meter** is to an automobile engineer — it doesn't measure performance directly, but it tells you whether the "engine" (concrete mix) will run smoothly under operational stresses. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Compaction Factor Formula The derivation begins from the principle of **bulk density comparison** between two states of the same concrete mass. ### Step 1: Define Bulk Densities Let: - $\rho_p$ = Bulk density of partially compacted concrete (kg/m³) - $\rho_f$ = Bulk density of fully compacted concrete (kg/m³) - $V_m$ = Volume of the standard mould (m³) By definition of density: $$ \begin{aligned} \rho_p &= \frac{M_p}{V_m} \\ \rho_f &= \frac{M_f}{V_m} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2: Form the Ratio Take the ratio of the two bulk densities: $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{\rho_p}{\rho_f} &= \frac{\dfrac{M_p}{V_m}}{\dfrac{M_f}{V_m}} \\ &= \frac{M_p}{M_f} \end{aligned} $$ The $V_m$ terms cancel because the **same mould** is used for both measurements. ### Step 3: Identify the Ratio as the Compaction Factor By definition: $$ CF = \frac{M_p}{M_f} = \frac{\rho_p}{\rho_f} $$ This is the final compact, dimensionless form used universally in KTU lab records. ## 3.2 Derivation of the Air-Volume Relationship Let $V_a$ be the volume of entrapped air in the partially compacted state. The mass of concrete solids remains constant: $$ \begin{aligned} M_f &= M_p \\ V_m \cdot \rho_f &= (V_m - V_a) \cdot \rho_p \\ V_a &= V_m \cdot \left(1 - \frac{\rho_p}{\rho_f}\right) \\ V_a &= V_m \cdot (1 - CF) \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (KTU Numerical Pattern) > [!NOTE] > **Standard KTU Numerical Format:** Given experimental data, calculate CF, classify workability, and compute air content. **Given Data:** - Empty mould mass, $W_0 = 5.20$ **kg** - Mass of mould + partially compacted concrete, $W_1 = 18.45$ **kg** - Mass of mould + fully compacted concrete, $W_2 = 19.80$ **kg** - Mould volume, $V_m = 0.0053$ **m³** (≈ 5.3 litres) **Step 1: Compute Mass of Partially Compacted Concrete** $$ \begin{aligned} M_p &= W_1 - W_0 \\ &= 18.45 - 5.20 \\ &= 13.25 \; \text{kg} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Compute Mass of Fully Compacted Concrete** $$ \begin{aligned} M_f &= W_2 - W_0 \\ &= 19.80 - 5.20 \\ &= 14.60 \; \text{kg} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Compute Compaction Factor** $$ \begin{aligned} CF &= \frac{M_p}{M_f} \\ &= \frac{13.25}{14.60} \\ &= 0.9075 \\ &\approx 0.91 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Classify Workability** Since $CF = 0.91$ falls in the range **0.85 – 0.92**, the mix has **MEDIUM workability**, suitable for **lightly reinforced beams, columns, and slabs**. **Step 5: Compute Density of Fresh Concrete** $$ \begin{aligned} \rho_f &= \frac{M_f}{V_m} \\ &= \frac{14.60}{0.0053} \\ &= 2754.72 \; \text{kg/m}^3 \\ &\approx 2755 \; \text{kg/m}^3 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6: Compute Air Content (Entrapped Air)** $$ \begin{aligned} V_a &= V_m \cdot (1 - CF) \\ &= 0.0053 \cdot (1 - 0.9075) \\ &= 0.0053 \cdot 0.0925 \\ &= 0.000490 \; \text{m}^3 \\ &\approx 490 \; \text{cm}^3 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 7: Express Air Content as Percentage** $$ \begin{aligned} A_v (\%) &= (1 - CF) \times 100 \\ &= 0.0925 \times 100 \\ &= 9.25\% \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Validation Check:** Air content in well-compacted normal concrete should lie between **0.5% and 2%** by volume. A value of **9.25%** indicates the mix is **poorly consolidated** and requires either a plasticizer addition or vibration during placement. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Source of Errors and Their Effect on CF | # | Source of Error | Effect on Measured CF | Direction of Deviation | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Concrete sticking to hopper walls | Lower $M_p$ | **CF Underestimated** (falsely low workability) | | 2 | Insufficient rodding in full compaction | Lower $M_f$ | **CF Overestimated** (falsely high workability) | | 3 | Door of hopper not opening fully | Lower $M_p$ | **CF Underestimated** | | 4 | Mould not cleaned of residue | Higher $W_0$ | **Both $M_p$ and $M_f$ underestimate equally** (CF less affected) | | 5 | Vibration of apparatus during test | Higher $M_p$ | **CF Overestimated** | | 6 | Sample segregation during placement | Non-uniform mass | **CF Not reliable** | | 7 | Conical heap not struck off properly | Excess concrete | Higher $M_f$ marginally | **CF Slightly Underestimated** | | 8 | Wet/dry mop not done on mould exterior | Water adds to mass | Higher $W_0$, $W_1$, $W_2$ | Negligible if applied uniformly | ## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Table — CF Test vs Slump Test | Parameter | Compaction Factor Test | Slump Test | |---|---|---| | **Sensitivity to low workability** | **High** | Low (slump ≈ 0 for stiff mixes) | | **Quantitative Output** | Yes (dimensionless ratio) | Yes (mm of slump) | | **Equipment Cost** | Moderate | Low | | **Time Required** | ~10 minutes | ~5 minutes | | **Suitability for SCC** | Limited (use flow table) | Not suitable | | **Standard Reference** | IS 1199:1959 | IS 1199:1959 | | **KTU Exam Frequency** | **Very High** | High | | **Result Interpretation Skill Required** | Moderate | Low | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Apparatus Block Diagram (Compaction Factor Apparatus) The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the **functional architecture and flow of concrete** through the standard IS 1199 Compaction Factor Apparatus. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Fresh Concrete Sample] --> B[Upper Hopper
Conical Shape
Door Held Closed] B --> C{Door Released
Free Fall 1} C --> D[Lower Hopper
Cylindrical-Conical
Door Held Closed] D --> E{Door Released
Free Fall 2} E --> F[Receiving Cylindrical Mould
150 mm dia x 300 mm height] F --> G[Strike Off Surface
with Trowel] G --> H[Weigh Mould + Concrete
Record W1] H --> I[Empty Concrete Back into Mould
Rod in 3 Layers 25 Strokes] I --> J[Strike Off Surface Again] J --> K[Weigh Mould + Concrete
Record W2] K --> L[Compute Mp = W1 - W0] L --> M[Compute Mf = W2 - W0] M --> N[Compute CF = Mp / Mf] N --> O[Classify Workability
as per CF Range] ``` ## 4.2 Physical Apparatus Schematic Description The standard Compaction Factor Apparatus consists of **two rigid hoppers and a cylindrical mould** mounted on a sturdy metallic frame. The components are described below: ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph FRAME[COMPACTION FACTOR APPARATUS - IS 1199] UH[Upper Hopper
Conical 254 mm top dia
130 mm bottom dia] LH[Lower Hopper
Cylindrical 254 mm dia
200 mm height] MD[Cylindrical Mould
150 mm dia x 300 mm height] ST[Steel Frame Base
with Levelling Screws] end UH -->|Door 1| LH LH -->|Door 2| MD MD --> ST ``` ## 4.3 Functional Process Flowchart (Test Procedure Sequence) ```mermaid flowchart TD START([Test Initiated]) --> P1[Clean and Tare the Mould] P1 --> P2[Sample Fresh Concrete] P2 --> P3[Place Concrete in Upper Hopper
Close Door 1] P3 --> P4[Wait 30 Seconds for Stability] P4 --> P5[Release Door 1
Concrete Falls to Lower Hopper] P5 --> P6[Wait 30 Seconds] P6 --> P7[Release Door 2
Concrete Falls to Mould] P7 --> P8[Strike Off Top Surface] P8 --> P9[Weigh and Record W1] P9 --> P10[Re-compact using Rodding
25 Strokes in 3 Equal Layers] P10 --> P11[Strike Off Again] P11 --> P12[Weigh and Record W2] P12 --> P13[Apply Formula CF = W1 - W0 over W2 - W0] P13 --> P14[Report CF Value and Workability Class] P14 --> END([Test Complete]) ``` ## 4.4 Workability Classification Decision Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD CF[Compute CF Value] --> Q1{CF in 0.70 to 0.80?} Q1 -->|Yes| R1[VERY LOW WORKABILITY
Mass Concrete Dams] Q1 -->|No| Q2{CF in 0.80 to 0.85?} Q2 -->|Yes| R2[LOW WORKABILITY
Pavements, Light Reinforcement] Q2 -->|No| Q3{CF in 0.85 to 0.92?} Q3 -->|Yes| R3[MEDIUM WORKABILITY
Beams, Columns, Normal RCC] Q3 -->|No| Q4{CF in 0.92 to 0.95?} Q4 -->|Yes| R4[HIGH WORKABILITY
Heavily Reinforced Sections] Q4 -->|No| Q5{CF > 0.95?} Q5 -->|Yes| R5[VERY HIGH WORKABILITY
Pumped or Tremie Concrete] Q5 -->|No| R6[REPEAT TEST
Out of Valid Range] ``` ## 4.5 Apparatus Component Data Table (KTU Lab Record) | Component | Shape | Top Diameter | Bottom Diameter | Height | Material | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Upper Hopper | Conical | 254 mm | 130 mm | 230 mm | Mild Steel (Galvanized) | | Lower Hopper | Cylindrical-Conical | 254 mm | 230 mm × 130 mm | 200 mm | Mild Steel (Galvanized) | | Receiving Mould | Cylindrical | 150 mm | 150 mm | 300 mm | Steel (Seamless) | | Hinged Doors | Rectangular | 130 mm × 130 mm | — | — | Steel with quick-release latch | | Supporting Frame | Truss Type | — | — | ~900 mm total height | Mild Steel Angle | # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper)]** **Q: Define Compaction Factor Test. State the formula used to calculate the Compaction Factor and list the IS code reference.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The Compaction Factor Test is a laboratory method for determining the workability of fresh concrete, particularly for **low to medium workability mixes**, by measuring the ratio of the mass of partially compacted concrete to the mass of fully compacted concrete obtained under standardized dropping conditions through two hoppers. $$ CF = \frac{M_p}{M_f} = \frac{W_1 - W_0}{W_2 - W_0} $$ Where $W_0$ = empty mould mass, $W_1$ = mould + partially compacted concrete mass, $W_2$ = mould + fully compacted concrete mass. **IS Code Reference:** **IS 1199:1959** (Methods of Sampling and Analysis of Concrete). **(1 Mark for definition, 1 Mark for formula, 1 Mark for IS code)** ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model Paper)]** **Q: List any three advantages and two limitations of the Compaction Factor Test over the Slump Test.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Advantages:** 1. The test is **more sensitive to low-workability mixes** where the slump test fails to register any measurable value. 2. It provides a **quantitative dimensionless index** that can be directly correlated with the degree of compaction under site conditions. 3. It is more **representative of field conditions** for stiff mixes used in mass concrete and pavements. **Limitations:** 1. The apparatus is **bulky, costly, and requires more laboratory space** compared to the simple slump cone. 2. The test is **not suitable for very high-workability or self-compacting concrete (SCC)** — for such mixes, the flow table test is preferred. ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice ### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1 **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2 Experiment]** **Q: (a)** Describe in detail the apparatus, procedure, and observations required to determine the workability of concrete using the **Compaction Factor Test** as per **IS 1199:1959**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** The following data were obtained during a Compaction Factor Test conducted on a fresh concrete mix: - Empty mould mass, $W_0 = 5.40$ **kg** - Mould + partially compacted concrete mass, $W_1 = 19.25$ **kg** - Mould + fully compacted concrete mass, $W_2 = 20.60$ **kg** - Mould volume = 0.0053 m³ Calculate the **Compaction Factor**, classify the **workability**, and determine the **percentage of entrapped air** in the mix. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks **Apparatus (2 Marks):** The apparatus consists of two conical hoppers (upper and lower) fitted with hinged quick-release doors, mounted on a rigid steel frame, with a cylindrical receiving mould of standard dimensions (150 mm dia × 300 mm height) placed below the lower hopper. **Procedure (3 Marks):** 1. Clean and dry the mould. Weigh the empty mould as $W_0$. 2. Fill the upper hopper with fresh concrete sample to its brim, with door closed. 3. Wait 30 seconds, then release the upper hopper door — concrete falls into lower hopper. 4. Wait 30 seconds, then release the lower hopper door — concrete falls into the cylindrical mould. 5. Strike off the top surface flush using a trowel. Weigh as $W_1$. 6. Re-fill the mould using the same concrete, compacting in **3 equal layers with 25 strokes of a tamping rod** per layer. 7. Strike off the top surface again. Weigh as $W_2$. **Observations & Calculation (2 Marks):** Record all three weights ($W_0$, $W_1$, $W_2$), calculate $M_p = W_1 - W_0$ and $M_f = W_2 - W_0$, then compute: $$ CF = \frac{M_p}{M_f} = \frac{W_1 - W_0}{W_2 - W_0} $$ Classify the workability using standard CF range tables. --- #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks **Step 1: Mass of Partially Compacted Concrete (2 Marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} M_p &= W_1 - W_0 \\ &= 19.25 - 5.40 \\ &= 13.85 \; \text{kg} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Mass of Fully Compacted Concrete (2 Marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} M_f &= W_2 - W_0 \\ &= 20.60 - 5.40 \\ &= 15.20 \; \text{kg} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Compute Compaction Factor (1 Mark)** $$ \begin{aligned} CF &= \frac{M_p}{M_f} \\ &= \frac{13.85}{15.20} \\ &= 0.9112 \\ &\approx 0.91 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Classify Workability (1 Mark)** CF = 0.91 falls in the range **0.85 – 0.92** → **MEDIUM WORKABILITY** → suitable for **lightly reinforced beams, columns, and slabs**. **Step 5: Entrapped Air Percentage (1 Mark)** $$ \begin{aligned} A_v (\%) &= (1 - CF) \times 100 \\ &= (1 - 0.91) \times 100 \\ &= 0.09 \times 100 \\ &= 9.0\% \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** The mix exhibits medium workability but contains approximately **9% entrapped air by volume**, which is **higher than the recommended 0.5–2%** for well-compacted concrete — indicating a need for **better compaction effort or plasticizer addition**. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2 Experiment]** **Q: (a)** Explain the **theoretical basis** of the Compaction Factor Test. Discuss the factors affecting the test results and state the standard ranges of CF for different workability classifications. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A concrete mix is designed for a heavily reinforced bridge girder. The Compaction Factor Test yields the following data: - $W_0 = 6.10$ **kg**, $W_1 = 21.05$ **kg**, $W_2 = 22.30$ **kg** - Mould internal diameter = **150 mm**, height = **300 mm** Determine: (i) Compaction Factor, (ii) Workability classification, (iii) Fresh concrete density, (iv) Volume of entrapped air. Comment on the suitability of the mix for the intended application. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks **Theoretical Basis (3 Marks):** The Compaction Factor Test measures the **self-compacting ability** of fresh concrete under standardized free-fall conditions. The work done by gravity as concrete falls through two hoppers simulates the energy input during site placement. The **ratio of bulk densities** (partially compacted to fully compacted) gives the **degree of self-compaction** the mix achieves without external effort, which is a direct measure of workability. **Factors Affecting Test Results (2 Marks):** - Water-cement ratio (higher w/c → higher CF) - Aggregate shape, size, and grading - Cement content and fineness - Admixture dosage (plasticizers increase CF) - Temperature and time delay between mixing and testing - Operator consistency in rodding and striking off **Standard CF Ranges (2 Marks):** Same as Section 1.3 table — 0.70–0.80 (very low), 0.80–0.85 (low), 0.85–0.92 (medium), 0.92–0.95 (high), 0.95–1.00 (very high). --- #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks **Step 1: Mass Values (1 Mark)** $$ \begin{aligned} M_p &= W_1 - W_0 = 21.05 - 6.10 = 14.95 \; \text{kg} \\ M_f &= W_2 - W_0 = 22.30 - 6.10 = 16.20 \; \text{kg} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Compaction Factor (1 Mark)** $$ \begin{aligned} CF &= \frac{M_p}{M_f} = \frac{14.95}{16.20} = 0.9228 \approx 0.92 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Workability Classification (1 Mark)** CF = 0.92 → falls at the **upper boundary of medium / lower boundary of HIGH workability** → **MEDIUM-to-HIGH workability** → generally **acceptable for heavily reinforced sections**. **Step 4: Mould Volume and Fresh Density (2 Marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} V_m &= \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 \cdot h = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.150)^2 \times 0.300 \\ &= \frac{3.1416}{4} \times 0.0225 \times 0.300 \\ &= 0.7854 \times 0.0225 \times 0.300 \\ &= 0.005301 \; \text{m}^3 \\ &\approx 0.0053 \; \text{m}^3 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \rho_f &= \frac{M_f}{V_m} = \frac{16.20}{0.0053} \\ &= 3056.6 \; \text{kg/m}^3 \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Note:** The fresh density of **3056.6 kg/m³** is **higher than the standard 2400 kg/m³**, suggesting the mix may have **excessively high cement or aggregate content** — this should be verified against the mix design. **Step 5: Volume of Entrapped Air (1 Mark)** $$ \begin{aligned} V_a &= V_m \cdot (1 - CF) \\ &= 0.0053 \times (1 - 0.92) \\ &= 0.0053 \times 0.08 \\ &= 0.000424 \; \text{m}^3 \\ &\approx 424 \; \text{cm}^3 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6: Air Content Percentage (1 Mark)** $$ \begin{aligned} A_v (\%) &= (1 - 0.92) \times 100 = 8.0\% \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion / Comment on Suitability (included in marks):** The mix has a CF of 0.92, indicating medium-to-high workability — **acceptable for a heavily reinforced bridge girder**. However, the **8% entrapped air content** is **significantly above the recommended 0.5–2%** for structural concrete, which may compromise strength and durability. The mix is **borderline acceptable** — recommended to add a **superplasticizer** and ensure **proper vibration during placement** to bring the air content within acceptable limits. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **MOST COMMON ERROR:** Using gross weights $W_1$ and $W_2$ instead of net concrete masses $M_p = W_1 - W_0$ and $M_f = W_2 - W_0$. → **Lose 2 marks** > 2. **UNITS ERROR:** Reporting CF with units (e.g., "0.91 kg") — CF is **dimensionless**. → **Lose 0.5 mark** > 3. **CLASSIFICATION ERROR:** Classifying CF = 0.92 as "high" without showing the range boundary analysis. → **Lose 1 mark** > 4. **PROCEDURE OMISSION:** Forgetting to mention the **30-second wait time** between drops, the **25 strokes × 3 layers** for full compaction. → **Lose 1–2 marks** > 5. **FINAL COMMENT MISSING:** Not commenting on the **suitability vs. application** in part (b) numerical questions. → **Lose 1 mark** (specifically asked: "comment on suitability") ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Rapid-Revision Master Checklist — Compaction Factor Test** - [x] **IS Code:** IS 1199:1959 (Reaffirmed 2018) - [x] **Core Formula:** $CF = \dfrac{M_p}{M_f} = \dfrac{W_1 - W_0}{W_2 - W_0}$ — **Dimensionless** - [x] **CF is ALWAYS ≤ 1.00**, never exceeds 1 (CF > 1 indicates experimental error) - [x] **Three Weighings Required:** $W_0$ (empty mould), $W_1$ (partial), $W_2$ (full) - [x] **Apparatus:** Two hoppers + cylindrical mould + steel frame - [x] **Standard Mould Size:** 150 mm dia × 300 mm height (~5.3 litres volume) - [x] **Test Sensitivity:** Superior to slump test for **LOW-workability mixes** - [x] **CF Range 0.85 – 0.92** = **Medium workability** (most KTU questions land here) - [x] **Workability Threshold:** **CF ≥ 0.85** is generally accepted for site work - [x] **Air Content Formula:** $A_v (\%) = (1 - CF) \times 100$ - [x] **Acceptable Air Content:** **0.5% – 2%** for normal concrete - [x] **Standard Wait Times:** **30 seconds** between each door release - [x] **Full Compaction Procedure:** 3 equal layers, **25 strokes per layer** with 16 mm rod - [x] **Density of Fresh Concrete:** ~2400 kg/m³ (normal weight) - [x] **High CF Application:** Pumped concrete, tremie concrete, SCC-adjacent mixes - [x] **Limitations:** Bulky apparatus, not suitable for SCC, sensitive to operator error - [x] **Always use NET mass** ($M_p$, $M_f$) — **NEVER** gross mass ($W_1$, $W_2$) - [x] **Always comment on suitability** when a specific application is mentioned - [x] **Originally developed by:** Glanville, Collins, and Matthews (1947) - [x] **Common Confusions:** CF is **NOT** a measure of strength — it is purely a **workability index** - [x] **Higher w/c ratio → Higher CF** (inverse relationship with stiffness) - [x] **Higher aggregate content (especially coarse) → Lower CF** (more internal friction) - [x] **Plasticizers and superplasticizers → Increase CF** without adding water

topics.title: Determination of Initial and Final Setting Time for Cement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAGL308 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Determination of Initial and Final Setting Time for Cement # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Setting Time of Cement** is defined as the time elapsed between the moment water is added to the cement and the moment the cement paste attains a specific, measurable degree of stiffness (or resistance to penetration) under standard test conditions prescribed by **IS 4031 (Part 5) – 1988**. It is classified into two stages: - **Initial Setting Time:** The period measured from the instant mixing water is added to the cement, until the paste begins to lose its plasticity and becomes unworkable. By IS standards, this is the time at which the Vicat needle penetrates the paste to a depth of **33 mm to 35 mm from the top** of the mould (equivalently, **5 mm to 7 mm measured from the bottom of the mould**). - **Final Setting Time:** The period from the addition of water until the paste has hardened sufficiently to bear a defined load. By IS standards, this is the time at which the Vicat needle (with annular collar attachment) **ceases to leave an impression** on the paste surface, while the central needle **does not penetrate** more than 0.5 mm. > [!IMPORTANT] > **IS Specifications for Ordinary Portland Cement (OPC) — IS 269:2015** > - **Initial Setting Time: NOT less than 30 minutes** > - **Final Setting Time: NOT more than 600 minutes (10 hours)** ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English) Imagine you are baking a cake batter. The moment you pour milk into the flour is like adding water to cement — the mix is now in a *plastic, workable* state. As time passes, the batter slowly thickens until you can no longer pour it; this "thickening point" is the **initial setting time**. Continue waiting, and the cake batter becomes a semi-solid mass that holds its own shape and resists a light poke — that is the **final setting time**. For cement, these two milestones are critical because: - Before *initial set*, the concrete must be **transported, placed, compacted, and finished**. - After *final set*, the concrete gains strength rapidly and is no longer workable. > [!NOTE] > **Why setting time matters in engineering:** If the initial setting time is too short, the concrete will stiffen before the workers can place it — leading to **cold joints** and structural weakness. If it is too long, construction schedules are delayed. Hence, IS code enforces minimum and maximum limits. ## 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics - **Code of Practice:** IS 4031 (Part 5) – 1988 - **OPC Specification:** IS 269 – 2015 - **Test Temperature:** **27 °C ± 2 °C** - **Test Humidity:** **90 % ± 5 % Relative Humidity** # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Underlying Physical & Chemical Concept When water is added to cement, a chemical reaction called **hydration** begins. The principal clinker minerals — *Tricalcium Silicate (C₃S), Dicalcium Silicate (C₂S), Tricalcium Aluminate (C₃A), and Tetracalcium Aluminoferrite (C₄AF)* — react with water to form **Calcium Silicate Hydrate (C-S-H)** gel and **Calcium Hydroxide (CH)**. - **C-S-H gel** is the primary strength-giving phase. - **C₃A** reacts almost instantly, which is why **gypsum (CaSO₄·2H₂O)** is added (typically 3–5 %) to retard its reaction and prevent **flash set** (premature stiffening). The setting is essentially the **progressive stiffening** of the cement paste as hydration products interlock into a rigid skeleton. ## 2.2 Vicat Apparatus — Component Breakdown The **Vicat Apparatus** (named after French engineer Louis Vicat) is the standard instrument used. | Component | Specification / Function | |---|---| | **Vicat Frame** | A rigid metal stand with a movable plunger/needle holder weighing **300 g** total assembly. | | **Vicat Mould** | A truncated conical ring of height **40 mm**, top internal diameter **70 mm**, bottom internal diameter **60 mm**, resting on a non-porous base plate. | | **Plunger (for consistency test)** | Square needle, cross-section **10 mm × 10 mm**, length **50 mm**. | | **Initial-Set Needle** | Cylindrical needle of diameter **1.13 mm** (area ≈ **10 mm²**), length **50 mm**. | | **Final-Set Needle** | Same **1.13 mm** needle fitted with an **annular metal collar** (outer 7.13 mm, inner 1.13 mm). | | **Scale / Dial Gauge** | Graduated in millimetres to measure penetration depth. | > [!NOTE] > The cross-sectional area of the needle is **10 mm²**. Combined with the **300 g** plunger assembly, the test stress on the cement surface is approximately **0.3 N / 10 mm² = 0.03 N/mm² = 0.03 MPa**. ## 2.3 Concept of Standard Consistency Before setting time is measured, the **water content for standard consistency (P)** must be known. This is the percentage of water (by mass of cement) at which the Vicat *plunger* penetrates **10 mm ± 1 mm** from the bottom of the mould in **30 seconds**. For the **setting time test**, only **0.85 P %** of water is used (less than standard consistency) so that the test paste is **stiffer** and gives reproducible penetration readings. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Water for setting time test | $W_s = 0.85 \, P$ | % of cement mass | | 2 | Mass of water required (g) | $W_s = \dfrac{0.85 \, P \times M_c}{100}$ | grams | | 3 | Depth of penetration at initial set | $d_i = 33 \text{ to } 35$ (from top) | mm | | 4 | Equivalent from bottom | $d_i = 5 \text{ to } 7$ | mm | | 5 | Plunger stress | $\sigma = \dfrac{m g}{A}$ | N/mm² | | 6 | OPC minimum initial set | $t_i \geq 30$ | minutes | | 7 | OPC maximum final set | $t_f \leq 600$ | minutes | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-world Engineering Utility:** Setting time governs the **concrete scheduling plan**, the choice of **admixtures (retarders/accelerators)**, the feasibility of **mass pours**, and the **transport time** from batching plant to site. For deep foundations, underwater concreting, and hot-weather pours, *retarders* extend the initial set; for precast and cold-weather works, *accelerators* shorten the final set. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Procedure, Calculations & Worked Example ## 3.1 Apparatus & Materials Required 1. Vicat apparatus (frame, mould, base plate, initial-set needle, final-set needle, plunger) 2. **400 g** of cement sample (passed through 90-micron IS sieve) 3. Gauging trowel, mixing tray, stopwatch 4. **Potable water** at $27 \pm 2 \, ^\circ\text{C}$ 5. Glass graduated cylinder 6. Cabinet / humidity chamber maintained at $27 \pm 2 \, ^\circ\text{C}$ and $90 \pm 5 \,\%$ RH 7. Weighing balance (accuracy $\pm 0.1$ g) ## 3.2 Detailed Experimental Procedure **Step A — Mixing the Paste** Take **400 g of cement** in the mixing tray. Add water equal to **0.85 P %** of cement mass (where P is determined from the standard consistency test of the same cement). Mix thoroughly for **3 to 5 minutes** to obtain a uniform, plastic paste. **Step B — Filling the Mould** Oil the Vicat mould lightly. Fill the mould with the cement paste, taking care to **remove entrapped air** by tamping gently. Strike off the excess paste at the top with a trowel to obtain a level surface. Place the mould on the base plate under the needle. **Step C — Mounting the Needle** Lower the initial-setting needle (1.13 mm dia.) gently until it just touches the paste surface. Note the reading on the scale (or set the dial to zero). Release the needle quickly and allow it to penetrate for **30 seconds**. **Step D — Recording Penetration** Note the depth of penetration from the scale. Clean the needle and reset. Repeat the penetration at **regular intervals** (typically every 5–10 minutes), each time at a fresh location on the paste, keeping the mould in the humidity chamber between tests. **Step E — Determining Initial Set** The **initial setting time** is the elapsed time (from the moment water was added) at which the needle penetrates to a depth of **5 mm to 7 mm from the bottom of the mould**. **Step F — Determining Final Set** Replace the initial-set needle with the **annular collar assembly**. Continue tests. The **final setting time** is the elapsed time at which the needle no longer leaves a clear impression on the paste surface, and the annular collar fails to make a circular cut on the paste. The needle should not penetrate more than **0.5 mm** at this point. ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern) **Question:** For an OPC sample, the standard consistency was found to be **30 %**. Calculate the amount of water to be added to 400 g of cement for the setting time test. State the IS specification that must be satisfied. ### Solution: Given: - Mass of cement, $M_c = 400 \text{ g}$ - Standard consistency, $P = 30 \,\%$ - Water for setting time = 0.85 P **Step 1 — Water percentage for setting time** $$ W_s = 0.85 \times P $$ $$ W_s = 0.85 \times 30 = 25.5 \,\% $$ **Step 2 — Mass of water required** $$ W_{\text{mass}} = \dfrac{W_s \times M_c}{100} $$ $$ W_{\text{mass}} = \dfrac{25.5 \times 400}{100} $$ $$ W_{\text{mass}} = \dfrac{10200}{100} = 102 \text{ g} $$ **Answer:** Add **102 g of water** to 400 g of cement. **IS Specification (IS 269:2015):** - Initial setting time **≥ 30 minutes** - Final setting time **≤ 600 minutes (10 hours)** ## 3.4 Tabulated Experimental Observation Format (For Lab Record) | S. No. | Time elapsed (min) | Penetration depth (mm from bottom) | Remarks | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | 0 | — | Water added; t = 0 | | 2 | 30 | 30 | Needle still penetrates deeply | | 3 | 60 | 22 | Paste thickening | | 4 | 90 | 15 | Approaching initial set | | 5 | 120 | 6 | **Initial Setting Time ≈ 120 min** | | 6 | 150 | 2 | Past initial set | | 7 | 240 | < 0.5 (collar) | **Final Setting Time ≈ 240 min** | | 8 | 600 | No impression | Within IS limit ✓ | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Vicat Apparatus — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB start([Start: Test on Cement Paste]) --> frame["Vicat Frame (Rigid Stand)"] frame --> moveplunger["Movable Plunger Holder
Total mass = 300 g"] moveplunger --> needle1["Initial-Set Needle
Diameter = 1.13 mm
Length = 50 mm"] moveplunger --> needle2["Final-Set Assembly
Needle + Annular Collar
Outer = 7.13 mm, Inner = 1.13 mm"] moveplunger --> plunger1["Standard Consistency Plunger
10 mm x 10 mm cross section"] frame --> mould["Vicat Mould
Top ID = 70 mm
Bottom ID = 60 mm
Height = 40 mm"] mould --> base["Non-porous Base Plate"] frame --> scale["Graduated Scale
Least count = 1 mm
Range 0 to 40 mm"] mould --> paste["Cement Paste
Water = 0.85 P percent
Cement = 400 g"] paste --> humidity["Humidity Chamber
Temperature = 27 +/- 2 C
Humidity = 90 +/- 5 percent RH"] needle1 --> read1["Read penetration
5 to 7 mm from bottom
= Initial Set"] needle2 --> read2["Annular collar fails to cut
Needle penetration < 0.5 mm
= Final Set"] plunger1 --> read3["Penetration = 10 +/- 1 mm
in 30 seconds
= Standard Consistency P"] read1 --> result["Result: Setting Time
Initial Set ≥ 30 min
Final Set ≤ 600 min"] read2 --> result read3 --> result result --> stop([End: Report to IS 269 / IS 4031]) ``` ## 4.2 Experimental Procedure Flow Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A([Weigh 400 g Cement]) --> B[Add 0.85 P percent Water] B --> C[Mix Thoroughly 3 to 5 min] C --> D[Fill Vicat Mould, Tame Air Voids] D --> E[Place in Humidity Chamber] E --> F[Lower Initial-Set Needle] F --> G{Penetration
5 to 7 mm
