from bottom?} G -- No --> H[Wait, Repeat at Intervals] H --> F G -- Yes --> I["Record Initial Set Time
(must be ≥ 30 min)"] I --> J[Switch to Annular Collar Needle] J --> K{Collar Cuts
the Paste?} K -- Yes --> H K -- No --> L["Record Final Set Time
(must be ≤ 600 min)"] L --> M([Report to IS 269:2015]) ``` ## 4.3 Cement Hydration & Role of Gypsum — Conceptual Block ```mermaid flowchart TB rawclinker["Cement Clinker
C3S + C2S + C3A + C4AF"] --> addwater["Add Water"] addwater --> hydration["Hydration Reactions Begin"] hydration --> csh["C-S-H Gel Forms
(Strength Giving Phase)"] hydration --> ch["Ca(OH)2 Forms
(Secondary Product)"] hydration --> c3areact["C3A + Water
(Flash Set in Minutes)"] c3areact --> gypsum["Gypsum CaSO4.2H2O
(3 to 5 percent)"] gypsum --> retard["Forms Ettringite
Retards Flash Set"] retard --> controlled["Controlled Setting
Initial Set ≥ 30 min"] controlled --> strength["Final Strength Development
Final Set ≤ 600 min"] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **Define (i) Initial setting time and (ii) Final setting time of cement as per IS 4031 (Part 5) – 1988. State the IS specification limits for OPC.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Model Answer:** - **(i) Initial Setting Time:** It is the time measured from the moment water is added to the cement until the Vicat needle (1.13 mm dia., 300 g assembly) penetrates the paste to a depth of **5 mm to 7 mm from the bottom** of the mould (i.e., 33 to 35 mm from the top). - **(ii) Final Setting Time:** It is the time elapsed from the moment of water addition until the Vicat needle fitted with the annular collar **ceases to leave a clear impression** on the paste surface, and the central needle does **not penetrate more than 0.5 mm** into the paste. - **IS 269:2015 Specifications for OPC:** - Initial setting time **NOT less than 30 minutes** - Final setting time **NOT more than 600 minutes (10 hours)** *(3 marks — 1 mark each for the two definitions and 1 mark for IS limits)* --- ### Question 2 **Why is gypsum added to cement? What is the typical range of gypsum addition?** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** - **Purpose:** Gypsum (CaSO₄·2H₂O) is added to cement clinker during grinding to **retard the rapid hydration of Tricalcium Aluminate (C₃A)**. Without gypsum, the cement would undergo **flash set** — a near-instantaneous stiffening within a few minutes of water addition, making the cement unworkable. - **Mechanism:** Gypsum reacts with C₃A and water to form **calcium sulfoaluminate (ettringite)**, which forms a protective layer around the C₃A grains, slowing down further hydration and allowing **controlled setting**. - **Typical Range:** **3 % to 5 %** by mass of cement clinker. - **Excess gypsum**, however, leads to **delayed expansion and unsoundness** due to the formation of secondary ettringite in the hardened paste. *(3 marks — 1 mark purpose, 1 mark mechanism, 1 mark range and warning)* --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **With a neat labelled sketch, describe the Vicat apparatus used for determining the setting time of cement. List the various needles used and their specific use.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` **Model Answer:** **Vicat Apparatus (description):** The Vicat apparatus consists of a metal frame carrying a movable plunger/needle holder, the total weight of which is **300 g**. A Vicat mould of truncated conical shape (top ID 70 mm, bottom ID 60 mm, height 40 mm) is placed on a non-porous base plate. A graduated scale (0–40 mm) is provided to measure penetration depth. For setting time determination, the apparatus is kept in a humidity chamber maintained at **27 ± 2 °C** and **90 ± 5 % RH**. **Needles/Plungers and their uses:** | Accessory | Specification | Use | |---|---|---| | Plunger (square) | $10 \times 10$ mm, 50 mm long | Determine **standard consistency (P)** | | Initial-Set Needle | 1.13 mm dia., 50 mm long | Measure **initial setting time** | | Final-Set Assembly | 1.13 mm needle + annular collar (outer 7.13 mm) | Measure **final setting time** | *Sketch:* (Refer to block diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes.) *(7 marks — 3 marks description + 2 marks needles + 2 marks uses table)* #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Describe the procedure for determining the initial and final setting time of cement. State the IS specification limits.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` **Model Answer:** **Procedure (as per IS 4031 Part 5):** 1. Weigh **400 g of cement** accurately. Calculate water required as **0.85 P %** of cement mass (where P is the standard consistency). 2. Mix cement and water in a non-absorbent tray for **3 to 5 minutes** to obtain a uniform plastic paste. 3. Fill the **Vicat mould** (lightly oiled) with the paste, tamp gently to remove air voids, and strike off the surface level. 4. Place the mould in the **humidity chamber** at $27 \pm 2 \, ^\circ\text{C}$ and $90 \pm 5 \,\%$ RH. 5. Lower the **1.13 mm initial-set needle** gently to just touch the paste surface. Release the needle and allow it to penetrate for **30 seconds**. Note the penetration depth. 6. Repeat the test at **regular intervals** (every 5 to 10 minutes), each time at a fresh spot on the paste. 7. **Initial setting time** = time from water addition to the instant penetration is **5 to 7 mm from the bottom** of the mould. 8. Replace the initial-set needle with the **annular collar needle**. Continue testing. 9. **Final setting time** = time from water addition to the instant the annular collar **fails to make a clear cut** on the paste surface and the central needle penetration is **less than 0.5 mm**. **IS Specification (IS 269:2015) for OPC:** - Initial setting time **NOT less than 30 minutes** - Final setting time **NOT more than 600 minutes (10 hours)** *(7 marks — 5 marks procedure, 2 marks IS limits)* --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **What is meant by standard consistency of cement? How is it determined using the Vicat apparatus? Explain with the formula used for water calculation in the setting time test.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Model Answer:** **Standard Consistency:** Standard consistency of cement paste is defined as the **percentage of water (by mass of cement)** at which the Vicat plunger (10 mm × 10 mm) penetrates to a depth of **10 mm ± 1 mm from the bottom of the mould in 30 seconds**. It is denoted by **P %** and is determined first, as it is a prerequisite for conducting tests such as setting time, compressive strength, and soundness. **Determination Procedure:** 1. Take **400 g of cement** in a mixing tray. Add a trial percentage of water (say 25 %). 2. Mix thoroughly for 3 to 5 minutes. Fill the Vicat mould, level the top. 3. Lower the **square plunger (10 × 10 mm)** gently to the paste surface, set the scale to zero. 4. Release the plunger and measure penetration after **30 seconds**. 5. Repeat the experiment with varying water content (25 %, 27 %, 29 %, 31 % ...) until the plunger penetrates **10 ± 1 mm** from the bottom. 6. The corresponding water percentage is the **standard consistency P %**. **Water Calculation for Setting Time Test:** $$ W_s = 0.85 \times P \,\, (\% \text{ of cement mass}) $$ For example, if P = 30 %, then $W_s = 25.5 \,\%$, and water required for 400 g of cement is: $$ W_{\text{mass}} = \dfrac{0.85 \times 30 \times 400}{100} = 102 \text{ g} $$ *(7 marks — 2 marks definition, 3 marks procedure, 2 marks formula & example)* #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **The standard consistency of an OPC sample was found to be 32 %. Calculate (i) the water required for the setting time test on 400 g of cement, and (ii) the percentage of water that should have been used in the standard consistency test that resulted in a penetration of 10 mm.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Model Solution:** **Given:** $P = 32 \,\%$, $M_c = 400 \text{ g}$ **(i) Water required for setting time test:** $$ W_s = 0.85 \times P = 0.85 \times 32 $$ $$ W_s = 27.2 \,\% $$ $$ W_{\text{mass}} = \dfrac{27.2 \times 400}{100} = \dfrac{10880}{100} = 108.8 \text{ g} $$ $$ \boxed{W_{\text{mass}} = 108.8 \text{ g}} $$ **(ii) Percentage of water used in standard consistency test:** Since standard consistency is the water content that gives a penetration of 10 mm from the bottom of the mould, the water percentage is: $$ \boxed{P = 32 \,\%} $$ i.e., **128 g** of water on 400 g of cement. **Verification of penetration depth at initial set:** If mould height is 40 mm, then penetration of 5 to 7 mm from the bottom corresponds to the needle entering **5–7 mm** — i.e., failing to penetrate the bottom **5–7 mm** of the mould. *(7 marks — 3 marks part (i), 2 marks part (ii), 2 marks conceptual clarity)* --- ## 5.1 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:** > 1. **Confusing 0.85 P with P/4 + 3 %:** Some students wrongly use an empirical formula $P/4 + 3$ that belongs to a different (concrete mix design) context. For cement setting time, **only 0.85 P is correct** per IS 4031. > 2. **Mixing up the two needles:** A common error is using the **square plunger** to test setting time — this is meant only for **standard consistency**. For initial and final set, the **1.13 mm cylindrical needle** (and annular collar for final) must be used. > 3. **Forgetting the 30-second penetration window:** The plunger must penetrate in **30 seconds** in the standard consistency test. Students often leave it for longer, leading to deeper penetration and incorrect P. > 4. **Wrong IS specification values:** Initial set is $\geq 30$ min (NOT 45), final set is $\leq 600$ min (10 hours) per IS 269:2015. Earlier IS 269 versions mentioned 30 min and 10 hours — same values — but students sometimes write "45 min" confusing with 43-grade cement. > 5. **Not specifying units in the answer:** Always write "30 minutes" not just "30", and "600 minutes or 10 hours" for final set. > 6. **Skipping humidity chamber details:** Examiners award full marks only when **$27 \pm 2 \, ^\circ\text{C}$** and **$90 \pm 5 \,\%$ RH** conditions are stated. --- ## 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **Code:** IS 4031 (Part 5) – 1988 for test method; **IS 269:2015** for OPC specification. - **Sample size:** **400 g of cement** for setting time test. - **Water used:** **0.85 P %** of cement mass, where P = standard consistency water %. - **Test conditions:** $27 \pm 2 \, ^\circ\text{C}$ and $90 \pm 5 \,\%$ relative humidity. - **Vicat needle diameter:** **1.13 mm** (cross-section $\approx 10$ mm²). - **Plunger assembly mass:** **300 g** (gives stress $\approx 0.03$ N/mm² on paste). - **Initial set = needle penetrates 5–7 mm from bottom of mould** (33–35 mm from top). - **Final set = annular collar fails to cut paste; needle penetration < 0.5 mm.** - **OPC limits:** Initial set $\geq 30$ min; Final set $\leq 600$ min (10 hours). - **Plunger (10 × 10 mm) is for standard consistency, NOT for setting time.** - **Gypsum (3–5 %)** retards flash set by forming ettringite with C₃A. - **Excess gypsum → unsoundness**; insufficient gypsum → flash set. - **Lower water content (0.85 P) for setting time** ensures a stiffer, more reproducible paste than standard consistency. - **Reason for "initial set" significance:** Concrete must be **placed, compacted, and finished** before initial set. - **Reason for "final set" significance:** Concrete begins to gain **structural strength** after final set. - **Practical use of retarders:** In hot-weather concreting, deep foundation pours, and long-distance transport. - **Practical use of accelerators:** In cold-weather concreting, precast yards, and repair works.

topics.title: Determination of the Fatigue Behaviour of a Specimen Using Fatigue Test Machine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGL308 - STRENGTH OF MATERIALS
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of the Fatigue Behaviour of a Specimen Using Fatigue Test Machine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fatigue** is the progressive and localized structural damage of a material caused by **cyclic loading** at stress levels significantly lower than the material's ultimate tensile strength (UTS) or yield strength. When a material fails under these repeated or fluctuating stresses, the phenomenon is termed **Fatigue Failure**.

**Fatigue Test Machine (Rotating Bending Fatigue Testing Machine):** A specialized electromechanical apparatus designed to subject a standardized specimen to a controlled, fully-reversed cyclic bending stress (stress ratio R = −1) while simultaneously counting the number of cycles until fracture. The primary output is the **S–N Curve (Wöhler Curve)**, which plots the cyclic stress amplitude (S) against the number of cycles to failure (N).

**Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus Glossary):**

| Term | Standard Definition | Symbol / Unit |
|------|---------------------|---------------|
| Fatigue Life | Total number of stress cycles sustained before failure | N (cycles) |
| Stress Amplitude | Half the range of fluctuating stress | σₐ (MPa) |
| Mean Stress | Arithmetic average of maximum and minimum stress | σₘ (MPa) |
| Stress Ratio | Ratio of minimum to maximum stress | R = σₘᵢₙ / σₘₐₓ |
| Endurance Limit | Maximum stress below which material can endure infinite cycles (ferrous) | σₑ (MPa) |
| Fatigue Strength | Stress amplitude at a specified number of cycles | σₙ (MPa) |

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The Paperclip Analogy:** Take a standard metal paperclip and bend it back and forth repeatedly. The first few bends cause no visible change. However, after perhaps 10–15 bends, you hear a faint *click* and it snaps cleanly. The paperclip never came close to being pulled apart (its static tensile strength is hundreds of megapascals), yet it failed under tiny alternating stresses. **This is fatigue failure in its purest form** — damage accumulation under sub-yield cyclic loading.

A real engineering example is the **failure of an aircraft wing spar** during normal cruising flight. The wing flexes up and down with every turbulence gust. Although the cyclic stresses are well below the yield stress of the aluminum alloy, after thousands of flight hours, microscopic cracks initiate at surface defects, propagate, and suddenly cause catastrophic failure. This is why fatigue testing is **mandatory** for all aerospace, automotive, and rotating-machinery components.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard test frequency for rotating beam machines:** 3000 to 10000 cycles per minute (RPM)
- **Standard reference temperature:** **23°C** (as per ASTM E466 / IS 5074)
- **Standard test environment:** Normal ambient laboratory air (~50% humidity)
- **Standard specimen geometry per IS 5074:** Hourglass (waisted) profile, gauge diameter 6–12 mm
- **Reference design fatigue life for steels:** **10⁶ to 10⁷ cycles** (endurance threshold)
- **For non-ferrous metals (aluminum, copper):** No true endurance limit; reference is taken at **5 × 10⁸** or **10⁹** cycles

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked to define fatigue, always include the phrase *"at stress levels below the yield strength"* — examiners award full marks only when this distinction from static failure is explicitly mentioned.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Fatigue Failure — Three Sequential Stages

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Fatigue failure is **NOT** a sudden event. It is a cumulative, time-dependent degradation process occurring in three distinct, sequential stages. Each stage must be understood independently for the experiment to be analyzed correctly.

### Stage 1 — Crack Initiation (Nucleation)
- Microscopic cracks form at points of **stress concentration**: surface scratches, machining marks, inclusions, grain boundaries, or microvoids.
- Plastic deformation (although bulk stress is elastic) occurs locally in these micro-zones.
- The crack begins as a slip-band extrusion/intrusion on the surface.
- **Duration:** Typically 50–90% of total fatigue life in ductile metals.

### Stage 2 — Crack Propagation
- The initiated micro-crack grows incrementally with each load cycle, propagating perpendicular to the maximum tensile stress direction.
- Each cycle produces a small advancement (Δa) governed by **Paris Law**.
- Characteristic **"beach marks"** or **striations** appear on the fracture surface, which forensic engineers use to estimate load history.

### Stage 3 — Final Fracture
- When the crack reaches a critical length, the remaining cross-section can no longer support the applied load.
- Sudductile/brittle fracture occurs. The fracture surface shows two distinct zones: a smooth fatigue zone and a rough overload zone.

## 2.2 The S–N Curve (Wöhler Curve) — Master Plot of Fatigue

The S–N curve is the **central graphical output** of every fatigue test. It is plotted on **semi-logarithmic axes**: stress amplitude (linear, Y-axis) versus number of cycles to failure (logarithmic, X-axis).

**For Ferrous Metals (Steel, Cast Iron):**
- The curve drops and then **becomes horizontal** at a stress value called the **Endurance Limit (σₑ)**.
- Below σₑ, the material is said to have **"infinite life."**

**For Non-Ferrous Metals (Aluminum, Copper, Brass):**
- The curve keeps dropping slowly; there is **no true endurance limit**.
- An **arbitrary fatigue strength** is reported at a high cycle count (e.g., 5 × 10⁸ cycles).

## 2.3 Types of Fatigue Testing Machines (KTU Lab Equipment)

| Machine Type | Loading Mode | Stress Ratio (R) | Standard |
|--------------|-------------|------------------|----------|
| **Rotating Bending Machine** | Pure bending, rotating specimen | R = −1 (fully reversed) | IS 5074 / ASTM E466 |
| **Axial Fatigue Machine** | Push-pull (tension-compression) | Variable, often R = −1 or 0.1 | ASTM E466 |
| **Torsional Fatigue Machine** | Pure torsion, alternating twist | R = −1 | ASTM E2207 |
| **Servo-Hydraulic Machine** | Programmable arbitrary waveform | Any (R = −∞ to +1) | ASTM E468 |

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU laboratory experiment typically uses the **Rotating Bending Fatigue Testing Machine** because of its simplicity, self-contained loading mechanism (no external actuator), and the well-defined fully-reversed stress state (R = −1).

## 2.4 Operational Working Principle of the Rotating Beam Machine

1. A DC motor rotates the specimen at a fixed speed (typically 3000–6000 RPM).
2. A **static dead-weight** is applied to one end of the specimen, creating a constant bending moment along the gauge length.
3. As the specimen rotates, every fiber on its outer surface experiences **alternating tension and compression** — once per revolution.
4. Therefore, **one revolution = one complete stress cycle**.
5. A **revolution counter** automatically records the number of cycles to fracture.
6. When the specimen breaks, the machine trips the counter and stops.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** Below, all mathematical separators (e.g., absolute value bars) inside table cells are written using `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking the markdown table parser. Do NOT use raw `|` inside the table.

| # | Formula / Equation | Description / Where Used | Variables & Units |
|---|-------------------|--------------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | $\sigma_{a} = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I} = \dfrac{32 \cdot W \cdot L}{\pi \cdot d^{3}}$ | Bending stress amplitude on rotating specimen | M = bending moment (N·m), W = applied dead-weight load (N), L = lever arm (m), d = gauge diameter (m), I = moment of inertia (m⁴), y = radius (m) |
| 2 | $\sigma_{max} = \sigma_{m} + \sigma_{a}$ | Maximum cyclic stress | σₘ = mean stress, σₐ = stress amplitude (MPa) |
| 3 | $\sigma_{min} = \sigma_{m} - \sigma_{a}$ | Minimum cyclic stress | All in MPa |
| 4 | $R = \dfrac{\sigma_{min}}{\sigma_{max}}$ | Stress ratio (R = −1 for rotating beam) | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $\sigma_{m} = \dfrac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2}$ | Mean stress | MPa |
| 6 | $\sigma_{a} = \dfrac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2}$ | Stress amplitude | MPa |
| 7 | $\sigma \cdot N^{b} = C$ | **Basquin's Equation** (high-cycle fatigue) | σ = stress amplitude, N = cycles, b and C are material constants |
| 8 | $\sigma_{a} = \sigma_{f}^{\prime} \cdot (2N_{f})^{b}$ | Modified Basquin's form | σ′f = fatigue strength coefficient, b = fatigue strength exponent |
| 9 | $\dfrac{\sigma_{a}}{\sigma_{e}} + \dfrac{\sigma_{m}}{\sigma_{u}} = 1$ | **Goodman Line** (mean stress correction) | σₑ = endurance limit, σᵤ = UTS (MPa) |
| 10 | $\dfrac{\sigma_{a}}{\sigma_{e}} + \left(\dfrac{\sigma_{m}}{\sigma_{u}}\right)^{2} = 1$ | **Gerber Parabola** (less conservative) | Same as above |
| 11 | $\dfrac{\sigma_{a}}{\sigma_{e}} + \dfrac{\sigma_{m}}{\sigma_{y}} = 1$ | **Soderberg Line** (most conservative, uses yield) | σᵧ = yield strength |
| 12 | $\dfrac{da}{dN} = C \cdot (\Delta K)^{m}$ | **Paris Law** (crack growth rate) | da/dN = crack growth per cycle, ΔK = stress intensity range, C, m = material constants |
| 13 | $K_{t} = \dfrac{\sigma_{max}}{\sigma_{nom}}$ | Theoretical stress concentration factor | Dimensionless |
| 14 | $q = \dfrac{K_{f} - 1}{K_{t} - 1}$ | **Notch Sensitivity** (0 ≤ q ≤ 1) | Kf = fatigue stress concentration, Kt = theoretical concentration |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Domain | Component Subjected to Fatigue | Required Test |
|--------------------|-------------------------------|----------------|
| Aerospace | Wing spars, turbine blades, landing gear | Rotating bending + axial |
| Automotive | Crankshafts, connecting rods, suspension springs | Rotating bending |
| Civil / Bridges | Weldments, pre-stressed cables, hanger rods | Axial fatigue |
| Power Generation | Steam/gas turbine shafts, generator rotors | Rotating bending |
| Biomedical | Hip implants, dental implants (walking cycles) | Axial fatigue (R = 0.1) |
| Railways | Axles, wheels, rails | Rotating bending (R = −1) |

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters:** A single fatigue failure in service can cause loss of life and catastrophic economic loss. The S–N curve generated from this experiment is the direct input to **design codes** like ASME BPVC, IS 2062, and AISC, ensuring structures have a **factor of safety of 1.5 to 3.0** on fatigue strength.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Bending Stress on Rotating Specimen

Consider a cylindrical specimen of gauge diameter **d** mounted in the rotating bending machine. A static load **W** is applied through a lever arm of length **L**, creating a constant bending moment at the critical section.

**Step 1: Bending Moment generated by the dead weight**

The dead weight W acts at the free end of a lever of effective arm L. The moment at the gauge section is:

$$
M = W \cdot L
$$

**Step 2: Section modulus of a solid circular cross-section**

For a solid round bar of diameter d, the moment of inertia is:

$$
I = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^{4}}{64}
$$

The distance from the neutral axis to the outer fiber is:

$$
y = \dfrac{d}{2}
$$

Therefore, the section modulus Z is:

$$
Z = \dfrac{I}{y} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^{4} \div 64}{d \div 2} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^{3}}{32}
$$

**Step 3: Apply the flexure formula**

Using the bending equation:

$$
\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} \implies \sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}
$$

Substituting M = W·L, y = d/2, and I = πd⁴/64:

$$
\sigma = \dfrac{W \cdot L \cdot (d \div 2)}{\pi \cdot d^{4} \div 64} = \dfrac{32 \cdot W \cdot L}{\pi \cdot d^{3}}
$$

**Final Master Equation for the Experiment:**

$$
\boxed{\sigma_{a} = \dfrac{32 \cdot W \cdot L}{\pi \cdot d^{3}}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the subscript 'a' (amplitude)?** As the specimen rotates, every fiber at the outer surface alternates between maximum tension and maximum compression of equal magnitude. Hence the maximum stress **equals** the stress amplitude, since the mean stress is zero (R = −1 ⇒ σₘ = 0).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Relationship Between Stress Ratio R and Mean/Amplitude Stresses

We begin with the standard definitions:

$$
\sigma_{m} = \dfrac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2} \qquad \text{(Equation A)}
$$

$$
\sigma_{a} = \dfrac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2} \qquad \text{(Equation B)}
$$

**Step 1: Express σmax and σmin in terms of R**

From $R = \sigma_{min} / \sigma_{max}$, we get $\sigma_{min} = R \cdot \sigma_{max}$.

**Step 2: Solve for σmax**

Substitute into Equation A:

$$
\sigma_{m} = \dfrac{\sigma_{max} + R \cdot \sigma_{max}}{2} = \sigma_{max} \cdot \dfrac{1+R}{2}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{2 \cdot \sigma_{m}}{1+R}
$$

**Step 3: Solve for σmin**

$$
\sigma_{min} = R \cdot \sigma_{max} = \dfrac{2R \cdot \sigma_{m}}{1+R}
$$

**Step 4: Substitute into Equation B to get σa in terms of σm and R**

$$
\sigma_{a} = \dfrac{\sigma_{max} - \sigma_{min}}{2} = \dfrac{1}{2}\left[\dfrac{2\sigma_{m}}{1+R} - \dfrac{2R \cdot \sigma_{m}}{1+R}\right] = \dfrac{\sigma_{m}(1-R)}{1+R}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\sigma_{a} = \sigma_{m} \cdot \dfrac{1-R}{1+R}}
$$

**Verification for Rotating Beam (R = −1):**

$$
\sigma_{a} = \sigma_{m} \cdot \dfrac{1-(-1)}{1+(-1)} = \sigma_{m} \cdot \dfrac{2}{0} \to \infty
$$

This confirms that for fully reversed loading (R = −1), if σm = 0, the amplitude must be specified independently — which is exactly the case in the rotating beam test, where σa is prescribed by the operator and σm is automatically zero.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Basquin's Equation (Linearized S–N Relationship)

The S–N curve for **high-cycle fatigue** (10³ < N < 10⁶) appears as a straight line on a **semi-log plot** (σ linear, log N linear).

**Step 1: Empirical form**

The straight-line equation is:

$$
\log(\sigma_{a}) = \log(C') - b \cdot \log(N)
$$

where C′ and b are constants for the material.

**Step 2: Exponentiate both sides**

$$
\sigma_{a} = C' \cdot N^{-b}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\sigma_{a} \cdot N^{b} = C'
$$

**Step 3: Express in cyclic stress-strain form**

The fatigue strength coefficient σ′f (≈ true fracture strength σ′f) is related to the reference stress at 2Nf = 1 reversal (extrapolated):

$$
\boxed{\sigma_{a} = \sigma_{f}^{\prime} \cdot (2N_{f})^{b}}
$$

where:
- σ′f = fatigue strength coefficient (≈ σᵤ for many metals)
- b = fatigue strength exponent (typically −0.05 to −0.15 for steels)
- 2Nf = number of reversals to failure

**Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern):**
For a steel with σ′f = 1000 MPa and b = −0.10, the fatigue strength at N = 10⁵ cycles is:

$$
\sigma_{a} = 1000 \cdot (2 \times 10^{5})^{-0.10}
$$

First compute the exponent:

$$
2 \times 10^{5} = 200000
$$

$$
(200000)^{-0.10} = e^{-0.10 \cdot \ln(200000)} = e^{-0.10 \cdot 12.206} = e^{-1.2206} = 0.2947
$$

$$
\sigma_{a} = 1000 \cdot 0.2947 = 294.7 \text{ MPa}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Paris Law for Crack Propagation (Stage 2 Fatigue)

Once a crack of length `a` exists, the stress intensity factor at the crack tip in mode I opening is:

$$
K = Y \cdot \sigma \cdot \sqrt{\pi \cdot a}
$$

where Y is a geometry factor (Y ≈ 1 for a central crack).

The stress intensity range per cycle is:

$$
\Delta K = K_{max} - K_{min} = Y \cdot \Delta\sigma \cdot \sqrt{\pi \cdot a}
$$

**Empirical Paris Law:**

$$
\boxed{\dfrac{da}{dN} = C \cdot (\Delta K)^{m}}
$$

where C and m are material constants (typical m = 2 to 7 for metals).

**Integrating** from initial crack length aᵢ to critical aᶜ:

$$
\int_{a_i}^{a_c} \dfrac{da}{(Y \cdot \Delta\sigma \cdot \sqrt{\pi a})^{m}} = C \cdot N_f
$$

For m ≠ 2, the integration yields:

$$
N_{f} = \dfrac{a_{c}^{(1-m/2)} - a_{i}^{(1-m/2)}}{C \cdot (Y \cdot \Delta\sigma \cdot \sqrt{\pi})^{m} \cdot (1 - m/2)}
$$

This is the **predicted fatigue life from crack propagation theory**, used in damage-tolerant design (e.g., aircraft).

## 3.5 Complete Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem Statement (typical KTU 14-mark question):**
A rotating bending fatigue test is performed on a steel specimen of gauge diameter **d = 8 mm**. The applied dead-weight load is **W = 25 N**, the lever arm is **L = 250 mm**, and the machine speed is **N₀ = 3000 rpm**. The specimen failed after **t = 18.2 minutes** of testing. Determine: (a) the stress amplitude, (b) the total number of cycles to failure, (c) the stress ratio, and (d) state which type of cyclic loading this represents.

**Given Data:**
- d = 8 mm = 0.008 m
- W = 25 N
- L = 250 mm = 0.250 m
- N₀ = 3000 rpm
- t = 18.2 min

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Stress Amplitude**

Using the derived master equation:

$$
\sigma_{a} = \dfrac{32 \cdot W \cdot L}{\pi \cdot d^{3}}
$$

Step-by-step substitution:

$$
\sigma_{a} = \dfrac{32 \times 25 \times 0.250}{\pi \times (0.008)^{3}}
$$

Compute numerator:

$$
32 \times 25 \times 0.250 = 200 \text{ N·m}
$$

Compute denominator:

$$
\pi \times (0.008)^{3} = \pi \times 5.12 \times 10^{-7} = 1.6085 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^{3}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\sigma_{a} = \dfrac{200}{1.6085 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.243 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa} \approx 124.3 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Part (b) — Total Number of Cycles**

One revolution = one cycle. Total revolutions = speed × time:

$$
N = N_{0} \times t = 3000 \times 18.2 = 54{,}600 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Part (c) — Stress Ratio**

For a rotating beam machine with constant bending moment, the bending stress is fully reversed:

$$
\sigma_{max} = +\sigma_{a}, \quad \sigma_{min} = -\sigma_{a}
$$

$$
R = \dfrac{\sigma_{min}}{\sigma_{max}} = \dfrac{-124.3}{+124.3} = -1
$$

**Part (d) — Type of Cyclic Loading**

R = −1 corresponds to **fully reversed / symmetric cyclic loading**, with **zero mean stress** (σₘ = 0). This is the most conservative test condition and is the standard reference for fatigue data.

**Summary of Results:**

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Stress Amplitude σₐ | **124.3 MPa** |
| Fatigue Life N | **54,600 cycles** |
| Stress Ratio R | **−1** |
| Mean Stress σₘ | **0** |
| Type of Loading | Fully Reversed |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Rotating Bending Fatigue Test Setup

The diagram below maps the functional interaction of every major subassembly in the standard KTU laboratory fatigue testing machine.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SupplyAC["AC POWER SUPPLY 230V 50Hz"] --> Rectifier["VARIAC VOLTAGE REGULATOR"]
    Rectifier --> DCMotor["DC DRIVE MOTOR 1HP 3000 RPM"]
    DCMotor --> CouplingA["FLEXIBLE COUPLING A"]
    CouplingA --> Specimen["SPECIMEN WITH GAUGE SECTION"]
    Specimen --> CouplingB["FLEXIBLE COUPLING B"]
    CouplingB --> Counter["CYCLES COUNTER WITH TRIP MECHANISM"]
    Counter --> SafetyStop["SAFETY CUTOFF SWITCH"]
    SafetyStop --> MainsTrip["MAINS TRIP RELAY"]
    LoadCell["DEAD WEIGHT PAN 5KG TO 50KG"] --> LeverArm["LEVER ARM RATIO 1 TO 10"]
    LeverArm --> Specimen
    Specimen --> Fails["FRACTURE EVENT"]
    Fails --> Counter
    Counter --> DisplayReadout["DIGITAL READOUT IN CYCLES"]
    SpeedSensor["TACHOMETER 0 TO 10000 RPM"] --> DCMotor
    SpeedSensor --> OperatorPanel["OPERATOR PANEL WITH RHEOSTAT"]
    OperatorPanel --> DCMotor
```

## 4.2 Flow Diagram — Experimental Procedure and Data Acquisition

This flowchart captures the **end-to-end logic** of the KTU lab protocol, from specimen preparation to the final S–N plot.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    StepA["START RECEIVE SPECIMEN"] --> StepB["MEASURE GAUGE DIAMETER d AT 3 LOCATIONS USE AVERAGE"]
    StepB --> StepC["MEASURE GAUGE LENGTH LG USING VERNIER CALIPER"]
    StepC --> StepD["POLISH GAUGE SECTION WITH 600 GRIT EMERY PAPER"]
    StepD --> StepE["INSTALL SPECIMEN IN CHUCKS TORQUE TO 25 NM"]
    StepE --> StepF["APPLY DEAD WEIGHT W TO LOADING PAN"]
    StepF --> StepG["SET MOTOR SPEED N0 VIA RHEOSTAT TYPICALLY 3000 RPM"]
    StepG --> StepH["START MOTOR AND COUNTER SIMULTANEOUSLY"]
    StepH --> StepI{"MONITOR FOR FRACTURE"}
    StepI -->|NO AFTER FIXED CYCLES| StepJ["STOP MACHINE AND INSPECT SPECIMEN"]
    StepJ --> StepK["RECORD CYCLES N AS RUNOUT INFINITE LIFE"]
    StepI -->|YES BREAKAGE DETECTED| StepL["RECORD N AT FAILURE FROM COUNTER"]
    StepL --> StepM["CALCULATE STRESS AMPLITUDE SIGMAA USING FORMULA 32WL OVER PI D CUBED"]
    StepM --> StepN["REPEAT FOR 6 TO 8 SPECIMENS AT DIFFERENT LOADS"]
    StepN --> StepO["PLOT SEMILOG GRAPH SIGMAA VS LOG N WOHLER CURVE"]
    StepO --> StepP["IDENTIFY ENDURANCE LIMIT SIGMAE FROM KNEE OF CURVE"]
    StepP --> StepQ["REPORT RESULTS AND END"]
```

## 4.3 Schematic — S–N Curve Interpretation Block

This block diagram replaces a hand-drawn graph with a structured flow showing how to read and interpret the experimental output.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    GraphStart["WOHLER S N CURVE SEMILOG AXES"] --> Region1["REGION 1 LOW CYCLE FATIGUE N LESS THAN 10 TO THE POWER 3"]
    Region1 --> Char1["CHARACTER HIGH PLASTIC STRAIN DOMINANT STRAIN CONTROLLED"]
    GraphStart --> Region2["REGION 2 HIGH CYCLE FATIGUE 10 TO THE POWER 3 LESS THAN N LESS THAN 10 TO THE POWER 6"]
    Region2 --> Char2["CHARACTER LINEAR SEMILOG BASQUIN EQUATION VALID"]
    GraphStart --> Region3["REGION 3 INFINITE LIFE N GREATER THAN 10 TO THE POWER 6 STEELS ONLY"]
    Region3 --> Char3["CHARACTER HORIZONTAL PLATEAU ENDURANCE LIMIT SIGMAE"]
    Region1 --> End1["FAILURE INITIATED BY STRAIN"]
    Region2 --> End2["FAILURE INITIATED BY STRESS CRACK GROWTH"]
    Region3 --> End3["NO FAILURE BELOW SIGMAE STEELS ONLY"]
```

The KTU standard specimen follows the hourglass profile per IS 5074, as shown in this functional block.

```mermaid
    Gauge --> FilletB["TRANSITION FILLET RADIUS R EQUAL TO 5D"]

    OuterFiber --> MidFiber["MIDDLE FIBER STRESS SIGMAM EQUAL TO ZERO NEUTRAL AXIS"]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (Conceptual — Definition Type)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**

> **Q1.** Define **fatigue failure**. How is it fundamentally different from static failure? State the role of stress concentration in fatigue.

**Model Answer:**

Fatigue failure is the failure of a material under the action of **repeated or fluctuating stresses** at stress levels **below the yield strength** of the material. It occurs after a finite number of cycles and is characterized by the sudden, brittle-appearing fracture of an otherwise ductile material.

It is fundamentally different from static failure because:
- Static failure occurs under a **single, sustained load**, governed by ultimate tensile strength (UTS).
- Fatigue failure occurs under **cyclic loading**, governed by the endurance limit (σₑ) and the number of cycles (N).

**Role of stress concentration:** Surface scratches, sharp corners, keyways, and inclusions act as **stress raisers** where local stress may exceed yield even when nominal stress is low. Fatigue cracks almost always initiate at these points. Polishing the specimen surface (a key step in the lab experiment) deliberately **minimizes** stress concentration to obtain intrinsic material behavior.

> [!TIP]
> **Marks Distribution (3 marks):** Definition — 1 mark, Comparison with static failure — 1 mark, Role of stress concentration — 1 mark.

---

### Question 2 (Conceptual — Practical Type)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper 2]**

> **Q2.** Why is a rotating bending fatigue test machine preferred for laboratory fatigue studies? Justify with the standard stress ratio obtained.

**Model Answer:**

The rotating bending fatigue test machine is preferred because:
1. **It produces a fully reversed stress cycle (R = −1)** automatically, eliminating the need for a separate compression actuator.
2. **Simple loading mechanism** — only a static dead weight is required.
3. **Self-contained and safe** — no risk of buckling (which occurs in axial compression tests of slender specimens).
4. **Inexpensive and reliable** — uses a simple DC motor and counter.
5. **Reproducible results** — the standard specimen geometry (hourglass) is well-defined in IS 5074.

**Stress Ratio:** In rotating beam, the bending stress on any fiber alternates between +σₐ (tension) and −σₐ (compression) once per revolution, giving:

$$
R = \dfrac{\sigma_{min}}{\sigma_{max}} = \dfrac{-\sigma_{a}}{+\sigma_{a}} = -1
$$

This is the **most conservative test condition** and provides the lowest fatigue strength for design purposes.

> [!TIP]
> **Marks Distribution (3 marks):** Three reasons — 2 marks, Stress ratio derivation — 1 mark.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Part B questions of Module Experiments carry 14 marks and offer an **internal choice** (either OR). You must answer either full Question A or full Question B. Each question is split into two sub-parts of 7 marks each (typical pattern). Below, both alternatives are provided with complete solutions.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module Experiment 2, Q9(a) OR Q9(b)]**

> **Q9(a).** Describe with a neat sketch the **Rotating Bending Fatigue Testing Machine**. Explain the procedure to determine the **S–N curve** of a given specimen material, and state the precautions to be observed during the test. (7 marks)

> **Q9(b).** A rotating bending fatigue test was conducted on a steel specimen of **gauge diameter d = 10 mm**. The lever arm length is **L = 300 mm**, and the test speed is **N₀ = 3000 rpm**. For a particular load of **W = 40 N**, the specimen failed after **t = 12.5 minutes**. Determine: (i) the stress amplitude σₐ, (ii) the number of cycles to failure N, (iii) the maximum and minimum stresses, and (iv) the mean stress and stress ratio. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to Q9(a):

**Apparatus Description (3 marks):**
The Rotating Bending Fatigue Testing Machine consists of:
- A **DC drive motor** (≈ 1 HP) with a speed-control rheostat.
- A **specimen holder** consisting of two chucks with collets, one rigidly fixed to a bearing housing and the other free to slide longitudinally.
- A **dead-weight loading pan** attached to a lever arm of fixed ratio (typically 1:10 or 1:20) that applies a constant bending load.
- A **revolution counter** with a trip mechanism that stops the counter and motor when the specimen fractures.
- A **safety enclosure / guard** to protect the operator from flying fragments.
- A **tachometer** to monitor RPM.

**Test Procedure (3 marks):**
1. Prepare a standard hourglass specimen per IS 5074. Polish the gauge section with 600-grit emery paper in the **axial direction only** (to avoid circumferential scratches that would act as crack initiators).
2. Measure the gauge diameter `d` at three locations using a micrometer; record the average.
3. Mount the specimen in the chucks, ensuring it is **perfectly aligned** to avoid superimposed bending and torsion.
4. Set the desired dead weight `W` on the loading pan.
5. Start the motor and bring it to the test speed (typically 3000 rpm). Simultaneously engage the counter.
6. The specimen rotates, every fiber on the outer surface is subjected to fully reversed bending stress.
7. When the specimen breaks, the counter is automatically tripped and records the total number of cycles `N`.
8. Repeat the test for **6 to 8 specimens** at different loads (typically 70%, 60%, 50%, 40%, 30% of UTS) to obtain sufficient data points.
9. Plot σₐ vs `N` on a **semi-log graph** to obtain the S–N curve.
10. Identify the **endurance limit σₑ** from the knee of the curve (or report fatigue strength at 10⁶ cycles).

**Precautions (1 mark):**
- Specimen surface must be free of circumferential scratches.
- Specimen must be **dynamically balanced** before testing.
- Avoid eccentric loading; check alignment with a dial gauge.
- Run the test at a **constant speed** to avoid transient stresses.
- Use a **safety enclosure** to contain fragments.
- Do not exceed the elastic limit during initial static loading.

---

#### Model Solution to Q9(b):

**Given:**
- d = 10 mm = 0.010 m
- L = 300 mm = 0.300 m
- N₀ = 3000 rpm
- W = 40 N
- t = 12.5 min

**Part (i) — Stress Amplitude**

$$
\sigma_{a} = \dfrac{32 \cdot W \cdot L}{\pi \cdot d^{3}}
$$

$$
\sigma_{a} = \dfrac{32 \times 40 \times 0.300}{\pi \times (0.010)^{3}}
$$

Compute numerator:

$$
32 \times 40 \times 0.300 = 384 \text{ N·m}
$$

Compute denominator:

$$
\pi \times (0.010)^{3} = \pi \times 10^{-6} = 3.1416 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^{3}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\sigma_{a} = \dfrac{384}{3.1416 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.222 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa} \approx 122.2 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Part (ii) — Number of Cycles to Failure**

$$
N = N_{0} \times t = 3000 \text{ rpm} \times 12.5 \text{ min} = 37{,}500 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Part (iii) — Maximum and Minimum Stresses**

For a rotating beam with R = −1:

$$
\sigma_{max} = +\sigma_{a} = +122.2 \text{ MPa (tensile)}
$$

$$
\sigma_{min} = -\sigma_{a} = -122.2 \text{ MPa (compressive)}
$$

**Part (iv) — Mean Stress and Stress Ratio**

$$
\sigma_{m} = \dfrac{\sigma_{max} + \sigma_{min}}{2} = \dfrac{122.2 + (-122.2)}{2} = 0 \text{ MPa}
$$

$$
R = \dfrac{\sigma_{min}}{\sigma_{max}} = \dfrac{-122.2}{+122.2} = -1
$$

**Summary Table:**

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Stress Amplitude σₐ | **122.2 MPa** |
| Fatigue Life N | **37,500 cycles** |
| σmax | +122.2 MPa |
| σmin | −122.2 MPa |
| Mean Stress σₘ | **0** |
| Stress Ratio R | **−1** |

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module Experiment 2, Q10(a) OR Q10(b)]**

> **Q10(a).** What is an **S–N curve**? Sketch a typical S–N curve for a ferrous metal and a non-ferrous metal, clearly indicating the **endurance limit** and **fatigue strength**. (7 marks)

> **Q10(b).** The following data were obtained from rotating bending fatigue tests on a steel alloy. Plot the S–N curve on a semi-log graph and determine the **endurance limit**. Use the values to estimate the fatigue strength at **5 × 10⁵ cycles** using Basquin's equation. (7 marks)

| Specimen | Stress Amplitude σₐ (MPa) | Cycles to Failure N |
|----------|---------------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | 480 | 1.2 × 10⁴ |
| 2 | 420 | 4.5 × 10⁴ |
| 3 | 380 | 1.3 × 10⁵ |
| 4 | 350 | 4.0 × 10⁵ |
| 5 | 330 | 1.1 × 10⁶ |
| 6 | 320 | 8.0 × 10⁶ (runout) |

#### Model Solution to Q10(a):

**Definition (2 marks):**
The **S–N curve (Wöhler curve)** is a graph of **cyclic stress amplitude (S)** on the Y-axis versus the **number of cycles to failure (N)** on the X-axis (logarithmic), used to characterize the fatigue behavior of a material. The curve is generated by testing multiple specimens at different stress levels.

**S–N Curve for Ferrous Metal (Steel) (3 marks):**
For a typical carbon steel, the S–N curve has three distinct regions:
- **High-stress, low-cycle region (N < 10³):** Steep drop, dominated by plastic strain.
- **High-cycle region (10³ < N < 10⁶):** Nearly straight line on semi-log axes (Basquin's equation valid).
- **Infinite-life region (N > 10⁶):** The curve becomes **horizontal** at a stress value called the **endurance limit σₑ**. Below this stress, the material can theoretically endure an **infinite** number of cycles without failure.

**S–N Curve for Non-Ferrous Metal (Aluminum) (2 marks):**
For aluminum alloys and other non-ferrous metals:
- The curve **continues to drop slowly** with no horizontal plateau.
- There is **no true endurance limit**.
- An **arbitrary fatigue strength** is reported at a high cycle count (typically 5 × 10⁸ or 10⁹ cycles).

```
Steel S-N curve:
S(MPa)
  |\
  | \
  |  \  Basquin line
  |   \
  |    \____  σₑ  ← Endurance Limit
  |         ‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾
  |__________________________ log N
  10³     10⁴    10⁵    10⁶   10⁷

Aluminum S-N curve (no plateau):
S(MPa)
  |\
  |  \
  |    \
  |      \  No endurance limit
  |        \  (continuously decreasing)
  |          \___________
  |__________________________ log N
  10³      10⁶          10⁹
```

---

#### Model Solution to Q10(b):

**Step 1: Plot the S–N Curve (2 marks)**

The X-axis is logarithmic, with `N` values: 1.2 × 10⁴, 4.5 × 10⁴, 1.3 × 10⁵, 4.0 × 10⁵, 1.1 × 10⁶, and 8.0 × 10⁶ (runout).

Plotting the six points and joining them, we observe a knee forming near N = 10⁶, after which the curve flattens.

**Step 2: Identify the Endurance Limit (2 marks)**

From the data, Specimen 5 (σₐ = 330 MPa) failed at N = 1.1 × 10⁶, while Specimen 6 (σₐ = 320 MPa) did **not** fail at N = 8.0 × 10⁶ (runout). Therefore, the **endurance limit lies between 320 and 330 MPa**. By convention, the endurance limit is taken as the lower value (more conservative):

$$
\boxed{\sigma_{e} \approx 320 \text{ MPa}}
$$

**Step 3: Estimate Fatigue Strength at 5 × 10⁵ cycles using Basquin's Equation (3 marks)**

We use the two extreme data points in the high-cycle region (N between 10⁴ and 10⁶) to determine b and C.

Take Points 1 and 5:
- Point 1: σₐ₁ = 480 MPa, N₁ = 1.2 × 10⁴
- Point 5: σₐ₂ = 330 MPa, N₂ = 1.1 × 10⁶

Basquin's equation:

$$
\sigma_{a} \cdot N^{b} = C
$$

Taking logarithms:

$$
\log(\sigma_{a}) = \log(C) - b \cdot \log(N)
$$

Slope between the two points:

$$
b = \dfrac{\log(\sigma_{a1}) - \log(\sigma_{a2})}{\log(N_{2}) - \log(N_{1})}
$$

Compute:

$$
\log(480) = 2.681
$$

$$
\log(330) = 2.519
$$

$$
\log(1.1 \times 10^{6}) = 6.041
$$

$$
\log(1.2 \times 10^{4}) = 4.079
$$

$$
b = \dfrac{2.681 - 2.519}{6.041 - 4.079} = \dfrac{0.162}{1.962} = 0.0826
$$

Note: b is positive here because we are solving for the slope in log-log. In Basquin's form σa = C'·N^(−b), the exponent is −0.0826.

Now compute C:

$$
C = \sigma_{a1} \cdot N_{1}^{b} = 480 \cdot (1.2 \times 10^{4})^{0.0826}
$$

Compute (1.2 × 10⁴)^0.0826:

$$
\ln(12000) = 9.393
$$

$$
0.0826 \times 9.393 = 0.776
$$

$$
e^{0.776} = 2.173
$$

Therefore:

$$
C = 480 \times 2.173 = 1043
$$

**Step 4: Compute Fatigue Strength at N = 5 × 10⁵**

$$
\sigma_{a} = \dfrac{C}{N^{b}} = \dfrac{1043}{(5 \times 10^{5})^{0.0826}}
$$

Compute (5 × 10⁵)^0.0826:

$$
\ln(500000) = 13.122
$$

$$
0.0826 \times 13.122 = 1.084
$$

$$
e^{1.084} = 2.956
$$

Therefore:

$$
\sigma_{a} = \dfrac{1043}{2.956} = 352.8 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{\sigma_{N=5 \times 10^{5}} \approx 353 \text{ MPa}}
$$

This value lies between σₐ₄ = 350 MPa (at N = 4 × 10⁵) and σₐ₅ = 330 MPa (at N = 1.1 × 10⁶), which is physically consistent.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (Loss of 1–3 marks per question):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units:** A very common error is using `d` in mm in the denominator with `W·L` in N·m, leading to a wrong answer by a factor of 10⁹. Always convert **all dimensions to SI units (m)** before substituting.
>
> 2. **Mis-identifying the stress ratio:** Students often write R = +1 (which is a static load) instead of R = −1 for a rotating beam. The KTU examiner will deduct 1 mark for this conceptual error.
>
> 3. **Confusing endurance limit with fatigue strength:** Endurance limit σₑ is the stress at **infinite life** (only defined for ferrous metals). Fatigue strength σₙ is the stress at a **specific finite life N**. Do not interchange these terms.
>
> 4. **Mixing up fatigue and creep:** Fatigue is due to **cyclic** loading at any temperature; creep is due to **sustained** loading at **high temperature**. They are different mechanisms.
>
> 5. **Not stating the standard specimen code:** In 14-mark questions, always mention "Specimen prepared as per **IS 5074** or **ASTM E466**" for full marks.
>
> 6. **Missing the role of surface finish:** Surface polishing direction matters — polishing **circumferentially** leaves circumferential grooves that act as stress raisers; always polish **axially (longitudinally)**.
>
> 7. **Inadequate sketch:** A block diagram or labeled schematic is **mandatory** for 7-mark descriptive questions. A text-only answer loses at least 2 marks.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Determination of Fatigue Behaviour**

- [ ] **Fatigue** = failure under cyclic stress **below** yield strength.
- [ ] **Three stages of fatigue:** Crack initiation → Crack propagation → Final fracture.
- [ ] **S–N curve (Wöhler curve)** = σₐ vs N, plotted on **semi-log axes**.
- [ ] **Endurance limit σₑ** = horizontal plateau in S–N curve, present **only in ferrous metals** (typically at 10⁶ cycles).
- [ ] **Non-ferrous metals** (Al, Cu) have **no endurance limit**; report fatigue strength at 5×10⁸ or 10⁹ cycles.
- [ ] **Rotating beam machine** gives fully reversed loading: **R = −1, σₘ = 0**.
- [ ] **Master bending formula:** $\sigma_{a} = \dfrac{32 W L}{\pi d^{3}}$ — must use SI units.
- [ ] **One revolution = one stress cycle** on rotating beam machine.
- [ ] **Basquin's equation (high cycle):** $\sigma_{a} = \sigma_{f}^{\prime} (2N_{f})^{b}$.
- [ ] **Paris Law (crack growth):** $\dfrac{da}{dN} = C(\Delta K)^{m}$.
- [ ] **Mean stress correction lines:** **Soderberg** (most conservative) → **Goodman** (commonly used) → **Gerber** (least conservative).
- [ ] **Test standards:** IS 5074 (Indian), ASTM E466 (American).
- [ ] **Precautions:** Polished surface, dynamic balance, alignment check, constant speed, safety guard.
- [ ] **Number of specimens:** Minimum **6 to 8** to draw a reliable S–N curve.
- [ ] **Runout:** Specimen that has **not failed** at the cut-off cycle (typically 10⁷). Marked with an arrow → on the S–N curve.
- [ ] **Surface finish** strongly affects fatigue life: polished > machined > forged > corroded.
- [ ] **Stress concentration (Kt, Kf)** must be minimized in specimen design for valid material data.
- [ ] **Real-world failure examples to cite:** Aircraft Comet disasters, Liberty ships WWII, railway axles.
